```mermaid
    P2 --> P3[Mild Steel Plate 6 mm k 45]
    P3 --> P4[Rockwool Insulation 50 mm k 0.040]
    P4 --> P5[Aluminium Cladding 1 mm k 205]
    P5 --> P6[Convective Film ho]
    P6 --> P7[Accommodation Air 22 C]
```

## 4.3 Insulation Selection Workflow (Block-Level Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Heat Source Temperature] --> S2[Determine Target Surface Temperature]
    S2 --> S3{Cylindrical or Plane Geometry}
    S3 --> S3a[Pipe Duct Exhaust] --> S4a[Use Logarithmic Formula]
    S3 --> S3b[Bulkhead Deck] --> S4b[Use Plane Wall Formula]
    S4a --> S5[Select k from Material Database]
    S4b --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Calculate Minimum L for Target U Value]
    S6 --> S7{Check Fire Class A B C}
    S7 --> S7a[Add Fire Rated Layer]
    S7 --> S7b[Accept Current Spec]
    S7a --> S8{Check Acoustic Target}
    S7b --> S8
    S8 --> S8a[Add Mass Layer]
    S8 --> S8b[Skip]
    S8a --> S9{Check Vapour Barrier}
    S8b --> S9
    S9 --> S9a[Add Foil or Membrane]
    S9 --> S9b[Skip]
    S9a --> S10[Final Insulation Specification]
    S9b --> S10
```

## 4.4 Insulation Material Property Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Insulation Materials] --> M1[Mineral Wool Rockwool Glass Wool]
    M0 --> M2[Synthetic Foam PIR PUR Phenolic]
    M0 --> M3[Aerogel Cryogel Pyrogel]
    M0 --> M4[Calcium Silicate]
    M0 --> M5[Ceramic Fibre]
    M1 --> P1[k 0.035 to 0.045 Fire Class A1]
    M2 --> P2[k 0.020 to 0.028 Fire Class 0 to 1]
    M3 --> P3[k 0.013 to 0.020 Premium Cryogenic]
    M4 --> P4[k 0.055 to 0.065 High Temperature Steam]
    M5 --> P5[k 0.045 to 0.090 Extreme Heat 1260 C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define thermal conductivity and thermal resistance. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Thermal conductivity (k or λ)** is the property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat. It is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient under steady-state conditions. SI unit: **W/(m·K)**.
- **Thermal resistance (R)** is the opposition offered by a material (or layer) to the flow of heat. For a plane wall, $R = L/k$. SI unit: **m²·K/W**.
- Higher $k$ → better conductor (e.g., steel, copper); lower $k$ → better insulator (e.g., Rockwool, aerogel).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four properties required for an insulation material used in shipboard applications.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Low thermal conductivity** (k) to minimise heat flow.
2. **Non-combustibility** as per SOLAS FTP Code (Class A1/A2 preferred).
3. **Low density** to reduce ship displacement and fuel consumption.
4. **Resistance to moisture absorption** to maintain thermal performance and prevent corrosion.
5. *Optional 5th*: Resistance to vibration, chemical inertness, ease of fabrication, fire rating (A-60 etc.).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of insulation used on board ships. Discuss the selection criteria for engine room insulation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four major types of marine insulation are:

1. **Thermal Insulation:** Controls heat transfer between hot/cold zones. Used on steam pipes (180 °C), exhaust uptakes (500 °C), HVAC ducts (15 °C), and cold rooms (−18 °C).
2. **Acoustic Insulation:** Reduces airborne and structure-borne noise from main engines, gearboxes, and generators. Target: ≤ 75 dB(A) in machinery spaces, ≤ 55 dB(A) in cabins (per **IMO Res. A.468(XII)**).
3. **Fire Insulation:** Maintains integrity of A-60, A-30, B-15 bulkheads for specified durations (60, 30, 15 minutes) under **SOLAS Ch. II-2** and the **FTP Code**.
4. **Vibration Insulation:** Resilient mounts and floating floor systems to isolate machinery vibration.

**Selection criteria for engine room insulation:**
- **High-temperature endurance** (exhaust surface ~500 °C).
- **Non-combustibility** (Class A1 — Rockwool, calcium silicate, ceramic fibre).
- **Low thermal conductivity** (k < 0.05 W/(m·K)).
- **Mechanical robustness** — engine rooms have high vibration and oil contamination.
- **Oil and grease resistance** — closed-fibre structure preferred.
- **Acoustic performance** — engine room noise target ≤ 110 dB(A), cabin-side ≤ 55 dB(A).

**Q.A (b)** A plane steel bulkhead 8 mm thick (k = 45 W/(m·K)) is insulated on the accommodation side with 60 mm Rockwool (k = 0.04 W/(m·K)) and clad with 2 mm aluminium (k = 205 W/(m·K)). Engine room air = 50 °C, accommodation air = 24 °C, $h_i = 20$ W/(m²·K), $h_o = 9$ W/(m²·K). Calculate (i) total thermal resistance per m², (ii) U-value, (iii) heat flux. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resistances (per m²):**

$$\frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.0500$$

$$\frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.008}{45} = 0.000178$$

$$\frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.060}{0.04} = 1.5000$$

$$\frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.002}{205} = 0.0000098$$

$$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{9} = 0.1111$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{tot} = 0.0500 + 0.000178 + 1.5000 + 0.0000098 + 0.1111 = 1.6613 \;\; m^2 \cdot K / W$$

**Step 3 — U-value:**

$$U = \frac{1}{1.6613} = 0.602 \;\; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$$

**Step 4 — Heat flux:**

$$\Delta T = 50 - 24 = 26 \;\; K$$

$$q'' = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.602 \times 26 = 15.65 \;\; W/m^2$$

**Answer:** (i) **R = 1.6613 m²·K/W**, (ii) **U = 0.602 W/(m²·K)**, (iii) **q″ = 15.65 W/m²**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the modes of heat transfer. Derive an expression for the rate of heat conduction through a plane wall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Three modes of heat transfer:**

1. **Conduction:** Heat transfer through solids (or stationary fluids) by molecular vibration and free-electron drift. Governed by **Fourier's Law**: $q = -kA(dT/dx)$.
2. **Convection:** Heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid. Governed by **Newton's Law**: $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$.
3. **Radiation:** Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves, even across vacuum. Governed by **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**: $q = \varepsilon\sigma A(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$.

**Derivation for steady-state plane wall:**

Consider a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ and area $A$, with surfaces maintained at $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold). At steady state, heat flow $q$ is constant and temperature distribution is linear.

Starting from Fourier's Law:

$$q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Rearranging:

$$q \, dx = -kA \, dT$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $T = T_1$) to $x = L$ (where $T = T_2$):

$$\int_0^L q \, dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$q \cdot L = -kA (T_2 - T_1) = kA(T_1 - T_2)$$

$$\boxed{q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}}$$

**Q.B (b)** An exhaust pipe of outside diameter 220 mm is lagged with 40 mm of mineral wool ($k = 0.045$ W/(m·K)). Outer surface temperature of pipe = 320 °C, ambient = 35 °C, outer $h = 12$ W/(m²·K). Calculate the heat loss per metre of pipe length. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

$$r_i = 0.110 \;\; m, \quad r_o = 0.110 + 0.040 = 0.150 \;\; m$$

**Step 2 — Conduction resistance (cylindrical):**

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$

For 1 m length:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.150/0.110)}{2\pi \times 0.045 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(1.3636)}{0.2827} = \frac{0.3102}{0.2827} = 1.097 \;\; m \cdot K / W$$

**Step 3 — Outer surface area:**

$$A_o = 2\pi r_o = 2\pi \times 0.150 = 0.9425 \;\; m^2 \; (\text{per m length})$$

**Step 4 — Convection resistance:**

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA_o} = \frac{1}{12 \times 0.9425} = \frac{1}{11.310} = 0.0884 \;\; m \cdot K / W$$

**Step 5 — Total resistance and heat loss:**

$$R_{tot} = 1.097 + 0.0884 = 1.1854 \;\; m \cdot K / W$$

$$\Delta T = 320 - 35 = 285 \;\; K$$

$$q = \frac{285}{1.1854} = 240.4 \;\; W \; (\text{per metre of pipe})$$

**Answer:** Heat loss ≈ **240.4 W/m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using plane-wall formula for pipes:** Students often write $R = L/k$ instead of $\ln(r_o/r_i)/(2\pi kL)$. This yields wrong answers and loses **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting convective film resistance:** Many skip $1/(hA_o)$, losing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m in $L/k$ calculation. Always convert to SI first.
> 4. **Wrong temperature difference:** Using Fahrenheit, or forgetting to convert °C → K (note: $\Delta T$ is the same in both, but absolute temps need K).
> 5. **Confusing $k$ with $K$ (Kelvin):** Always write $k$ or $\lambda$ for conductivity, $K$ for temperature.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Insulation (Module 1):**

- ☐ Insulation = control of heat / sound / fire / vibration / electrical transfer.
- ☐ Three modes of heat transfer: **Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann)**.
- ☐ **Thermal conductivity (k)** units: **W/(m·K)**; lower k = better insulator.
- ☐ **Thermal resistance (R)** units: **m²·K/W**; $R = L/k$ for plane wall.
- ☐ Composite wall: resistances **add in series**.
- ☐ **U = 1/R_total**; heat flux $q'' = U \cdot \Delta T$.
- ☐ **Cylindrical (pipe) insulation** uses **logarithmic** formula — NOT plane-wall formula.
- ☐ Key materials: **Rockwool, Glass Wool, Calcium Silicate, Aerogel, PIR, Phenolic, Ceramic Fibre**.
- ☐ Material selection governed by: **SOLAS Ch. II-2, FTP Code, IMO Res. A.468(XII)**, IACS UR F17.
- ☐ Fire classes: **A-60 / A-30 / A-15 / B-15 / B-0** (time in minutes for integrity).
- ☐ Dew-point check mandatory for **cold rooms** to prevent condensation.
- ☐ LNG requires **multi-layer insulation (MLI) + aerogel** due to −163 °C operation.
- ☐ Surface films $h_i, h_o$ are often **neglected by students but critical in KTU papers**.
- ☐ For engine rooms: prioritised properties = **non-combustibility + high-temp endurance + low k**.
- ☐ Insulation contributes to **EEDI/EEXI/CII compliance** (energy efficiency).
- ☐ SI constants: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴); $h_{air} = 5–25$ W/(m²·K); $h_{water} = 500–10{,}000$ W/(m²·K).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological Process in the Hull and Machinery Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Technological Process in the Hull and Machinery Installation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PENST632 – Shipbuilding Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the **Technological Process in Hull and Machinery Installation** is defined as the chronologically ordered, interlinked sequence of industrial operations — spanning material procurement, steel fabrication, sub-assembly, block erection, joining, outfitting, and machinery setting — by which a ship’s load-bearing **hull structure** and its operational **propulsion/auxiliary machinery** are transformed from raw engineering materials into an integrated, seaworthy marine system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The *technological process* refers to the **planned workflow** that converts design drawings (offsets, lines plan, general arrangement) into a physically realized vessel, conforming to classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd’s, DNV, ABS) and the **Shipbuilding Quality Standard ISO 9001:2015**. The process can be sub-divided into two interlocking production streams: - **Hull Production Stream** — Steel cutting, forming, sub-assembly, block assembly, erection, and welding. - **Machinery Installation Stream** — Pre-outfitting (FO), main engine/auxiliaries mounting, shaft alignment, piping, cabling, and commissioning. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of shipbuilding the way you would build a giant, floating **LEGO skyscraper with a working engine inside**: 1. You don’t build the tower from the top floor downward, nor do you try to carry the whole skyscraper to the site in one piece. Instead, you **fabricate smaller blocks on the ground** (pre-assembly), join them into larger **super-blocks**, and then **lift these huge modules** into position using giant gantry cranes. 2. While the “skeleton” (the hull) is being welded together, electricians and pipefitters **simultaneously thread cables and pipes** through the bulkheads like nerves and arteries in a body. 3. Only after the body is whole is the “heart” (main engine) and “lungs” (compressors, pumps) installed in pre-prepared foundations — much like a doctor fits a transplant only once the patient is stable on the operating table. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Modern shipyards use the **"Berth-Building with Zone Outfitting"** philosophy — *build in parallel, integrate in series*. This is the single most important paradigm shift in modern shipbuilding compared to 19th-century “scaffold-around-the-ship” methods. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants | Metric | Standard Value | Context | |---|---|---| | **Block weight limit (Panamax crane)** | ≤ **600 tonnes** | Maximum liftable unit | | **Welding heat input limit** | ≤ **50 kJ/cm** (for DH36/EH36) | To preserve mechanical properties | | **Block tolerance (length)** | **± 3 mm at 10 m** | DNV/IRS tolerance class | | **Pre-outfitting completion target** | **≥ 70 %** | Modern modular yard benchmark | | **Hull erection cycle (VLCC)** | **~ 3–4 months** | From keel-laying to launch | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Phases of Hull Technological Process The hull technological process is universally accepted to comprise **five sequential, partially overlapping phases**: #### Phase 1 — Steel Stockyard and Material Preparation - Incoming steel plates (Grades A, B, D, E, AH32, DH36, EH36) are **shot-blasted, primed, and stored** in climate-controlled stockyards. - Plates are **nested** on CNC cutting tables to maximize material utilization (typically 80–85 %). - **Why?** Surface preparation is non-negotiable — mill scale and rust cause weld porosity and HIC (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking). #### Phase 2 — Cutting and Forming - Plates are cut using **NC plasma, oxy-fuel, or laser cutters** based on thickness: - **< 6 mm**: Laser - **6–25 mm**: Plasma - **> 25 mm**: Oxy-fuel (for thick plates) - Rolled plates are **cold-formed** (line heating or mechanical press-brake) into curved hull panels — shell plates, bulkheads, decks. - **How?** Three-roll pyramid bending machines achieve curvature by passing plates through offset rollers. #### Phase 3 — Sub-Assembly (Unit Construction) - Flat panels, stiffeners, and brackets are welded into **2D units** (sub-blocks). - A sub-assembly may be a single plate with welded stiffeners or a small panel section. - Sub-assemblies are then **joined to form 3D blocks**. #### Phase 4 — Block Assembly - Sub-assemblies are welded together on **panel/sub-block lines** to form complete **3D blocks**. - Block dimensions are dictated by: - **Crane lifting capacity** ($C_L$) - **Yard transport clearance** - **Dry-dock/building dock dimensions** #### Phase 5 — Hull Erection and Joining - Blocks are transported to the **building berth/dock** and lifted into position by goliath/gantry cranes. - Blocks are welded together at **block joints**, completing the hull envelope. - **Two principal erection philosophies exist:** | Method | Block Size | Equipment | Productivity | |---|---|---|---| | **Unit Construction (Japanese)** | Small units (10–30 t) | Multiple jib cranes | High — labour-intensive | | **Block Construction (Western/Modern)** | Large blocks (200–600 t) | Goliath cranes (800–1500 t) | Very High — capital intensive | | **Modular/Super-Block** | Mega-blocks (1000+ t) | Strand-jack systems | Highest — integrated outfitting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus emphasizes the **Block Construction Method** as the dominant technological process because it integrates **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)** into block fabrication — the lynchpin of modern shipbuilding productivity. ### 2.2 Machinery Installation — The Pre-Outfitting Concept Machinery installation is engineered to occur **simultaneously with hull construction**, not after it. This is termed **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)**. **Three tiers of outfitting:** | Tier | Location | Activities | |---|---|---| | **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)** | During block fabrication | Pipe racks, cable trays, ducting, equipment foundations | | **Stage Outfitting** | After block erection, before launch | Engine-room auxiliaries, funnel, accommodation ladders | | **Post-Outfitting** | After launch, at fitting-out berth | Main engine alignment, propeller shaft, sea trials | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following formulae are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Semester examinations: | # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_b = \rho_s \cdot V_b$ | $W_b$ = block weight (t), $\rho_s = 7.85\,\text{t/m}^3$, $V_b$ = block volume (m³) | Estimating block mass for crane selection | | 2 | $E = \frac{V \cdot I}{1000}$ | $E$ = heat input (kJ/cm), $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), denominator: travel speed (mm/min) | Welding parameter control | | 3 | $T_{tot} = n_b \cdot T_b + n_{jt} \cdot T_{jt}$ | $T_b$ = time/block, $T_{jt}$ = time/joint | Production cycle time | | 4 | $\eta_{mat} = \frac{A_{cut}}{A_{plate}} \times 100\%$ | $A_{cut}$ = cut part area, $A_{plate}$ = plate area | Material utilization efficiency | | 5 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | Von-Mises equivalent stress (N/mm²) | Foundation bolt load check | | 6 | $P_{crane} \geq k_s \cdot W_b$ | $P_{crane}$ = crane capacity, $k_s = 1.25$ safety factor | Crane sizing criterion | | 7 | $H_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Centre of gravity height (m), used in stability | Block lifting rigging design | | 8 | $\delta_t = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal distortion (mm), $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Welding distortion allowance | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The heat input formula $E = V \cdot I / \text{speed}$ is **literally used on every shipyard shop floor** to qualify welding procedure specifications (WPS) per **AWS D1.1 / IACS UR W11**. ### 2.4 Sequence of Machinery Installation (Logical Flow) A logically sequenced machinery install must respect the **dependency hierarchy**: 1. **Foundation preparation** — chock placement, epoxy grouting, alignment checks. 2. **Main engine bedplate mounting** — confirmed within **0.05 mm/m** longitudinal level. 3. **Shaft line installation** — main bearing → intermediate shaft → tail shaft → propeller. 4. **Auxiliary machinery** — generators, purifiers, compressors, pumps, heat exchangers. 5. **Pipe work** — sea-water cooling, fuel, lube-oil, ballast, bilge, fire-fighting. 6. **Cable laying and termination** — power, control, instrumentation. 7. **HVAC and accommodation outfit** — galley, navigation bridge electronics. 8. **Commissioning and sea trials.** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Welding Heat Input and Its Effect on Plate Distortion Modern KTU problems frequently test the relationship between **welding heat input** and **transverse shrinkage distortion** in a butt-welded plate. Let us derive the controlling relationship step-by-step. #### Given Data (Typical KTU-style problem): - Plate thickness $t = 20\,\text{mm}$ - Butt joint, single V-groove, root gap $g = 2\,\text{mm}$ - Welding current $I = 280\,\text{A}$ - Arc voltage $V = 30\,\text{V}$ - Travel speed $s = 300\,\text{mm/min}$ - Plate coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$ - Ambient-to-peak temperature rise $\Delta T = 800\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (localized near weld) #### Step 1 — Calculate Heat Input $E$ By the standard **arc energy formula**: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= \frac{V \cdot I}{s \times 1000} \times 60 \quad \text{[kJ/cm]} \\ &= \frac{30 \times 280}{300 \times 1000} \times 60 \\ &= \frac{8400}{300{,}000} \times 60 \\ &= 0.028 \times 60 \\ &= 1.68\,\text{kJ/mm} \\ &\Rightarrow 16.8\,\text{kJ/cm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** Heat input $E$ is energy deposited per unit weld length. Dividing $V \cdot I$ (power, in watts) by travel speed gives J/mm; converting to kJ/cm and multiplying by 60 normalizes to the unit kJ/cm used in shipyard WPS sheets. #### Step 2 — Check Against Allowable Heat Input For grade **DH36 steel** plate, **IACS UR W11** specifies: $$ E_{max} = 50\,\text{kJ/cm} $$ Since $16.8\,\text{kJ/cm} < 50\,\text{kJ/cm}$, the procedure is **within allowable limits** ✓. #### Step 3 — Estimate Transverse Shrinkage Distortion $\delta_t$ Empirically validated by **Terai–Saito equation** for butt welds in mild steel: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_t &= \frac{0.16 \cdot E}{t} + 0.05 \cdot g \quad \text{[mm]} \\ &= \frac{0.16 \times 1680}{20} + 0.05 \times 2 \\ &= \frac{268.8}{20} + 0.10 \\ &= 13.44 + 0.10 \\ &= 13.54\,\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** The first term captures shrinkage proportional to deposited energy; the second captures the contribution from the open root gap. Both are added because they act in the same transverse direction. #### Step 4 — Thermal Expansion-Free Thermal Strain $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{th} &= \alpha \cdot \Delta T \\ &= 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 800 \\ &= 9.6 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \end{aligned} $$ For a $L = 10\,\text{m}$ plate, this would yield a free thermal expansion of: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta L &= \varepsilon_{th} \cdot L \\ &= 9.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 10{,}000\,\text{mm} \\ &= 96\,\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ In practice, restraints reduce this, but the calculation sets the **distortion allowance** that must be **pre-cambered** into plates during forming. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Construction Methods | Criterion | Unit Construction | Block Construction | Modular Construction | |---|---|---|---| | **Typical block mass** | 10–30 t | 200–600 t | 800–1500 t | | **Crane type** | Jib/tower | Goliath (800 t) | Strand-jack / Mega-goliath | | **Pre-FO level** | 30 % | 60–70 % | 85–90 % | | **Labour intensity** | Very high | Moderate | Low | | **Capital cost** | Low | High | Very high | | **Construction cycle (VLCC)** | 14–18 months | 9–12 months | 6–9 months | | **Tolerance control** | Loose | Tight | Very tight | | **Suitable for** | Small craft, repair | Tankers, bulk carriers | Large vessels, cruise ships | | **Typical yards** | Older Japanese/Korean | European, Chinese | South Korean mega-yards | ### 3.3 Derivation: Crane Capacity Verification for Block Lift #### Given: - Block weight $W_b = 450\,\text{t}$ - Rigging + hook mass $W_r = 12\,\text{t}$ - Dynamic amplification factor $k_d = 1.15$ - Standard safety factor $k_s = 1.25$ #### Step 1 — Total Static Load $$ \begin{aligned} W_{tot} &= W_b + W_r \\ &= 450 + 12 \\ &= 462\,\text{t} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2 — Dynamic Load (during lift-off) $$ \begin{aligned} W_{dyn} &= W_{tot} \cdot k_d \\ &= 462 \times 1.15 \\ &= 531.3\,\text{t} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Required Rated Capacity $$ \begin{aligned} P_{crane} &= W_{dyn} \cdot k_s \\ &= 531.3 \times 1.25 \\ &= 664.1\,\text{t} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Selection Conclusion A yard with a **750 t goliath crane** would be suitable, while a **600 t crane** would fail. This calculation is the **standard shipyard practice** before any block lift. ### 3.4 Time-Cycle Derivation (KTU-style numerical) For a ship with $n_b = 80$ blocks and $n_{jt} = 240$ block joints: - Time per block on assembly line $T_b = 18\,\text{h}$ - Time per joint during erection $T_{jt} = 6\,\text{h}$ - Daily work hours $= 24\,\text{h}$ (3-shift operation) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tot} &= n_b \cdot T_b + n_{jt} \cdot T_{jt} \\ &= 80 \times 18 + 240 \times 6 \\ &= 1440 + 1440 \\ &= 2880\,\text{h} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{days} &= \frac{T_{tot}}{24} \\ &= \frac{2880}{24} \\ &= 120\,\text{days} \approx 4\,\text{months} \end{aligned} $$ This matches the typical VLCC hull erection cycle of **3–4 months** quoted earlier — a useful **sanity check** for exam answers. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Flowchart of Hull + Machinery Technological Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Steel Stockyard
Receiving and Blasting] --> B[Nesting and CNC Cutting] B --> C[Forming and Edge Preparation] C --> D[Sub Assembly Shop
Stiffener Welding] D --> E[Panel Line
2D Panels] E --> F[Block Assembly Shop
3D Block Fabrication] F --> G[Pre Outfitting in Block
Pipes Cables Foundations] G --> H[Block Painting and Drying] H --> I[Transport to Building Berth] I --> J[Hull Erection
Block Joining and Welding] J --> K[Stage Outfitting
Engine Room Auxiliaries] K --> L[Launch] L --> M[Post Outfitting at Fitting Out Berth] M --> N[Main Engine and Shaft Alignment] N --> O[Sea Trials and Commissioning] O --> P[Delivery to Owner] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hull vs. Machinery Streams ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HULL[HULL PRODUCTION STREAM] H1[Steel Preparation] --> H2[Cutting] H2 --> H3[Forming] H3 --> H4[Sub Assembly] H4 --> H5[Block Assembly] H5 --> H6[Erection] H6 --> H7[Launch] end subgraph MACH[MACHINERY INSTALLATION STREAM] M1[Pre Outfitting Foundations] --> M2[Auxiliary Equipment Mounting] M2 --> M3[Pipe and Cable Laying] M3 --> M4[Main Engine Installation] M4 --> M5[Shaft Alignment] M5 --> M6[Commissioning] end H5 -.-> M1 H6 -.-> M2 H7 -.-> M3 M6 -.-> P[Integrated Vessel Ready] H7 -.-> P ``` ### 4.3 Detailed Block Construction Sequence (Cross-Sectional View) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Flat Plate on Assembly Floor] --> S2[Step 2: Fit Longitudinal and Transverse Stiffeners] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Tack Weld Stiffeners] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Full Penetration Welding of Stiffeners] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Fit Internal Brackets and Foundations] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Pre Outfitting Pipes Cables and Ducts] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Join with Companion Sub Assembly to Form Block] S7 --> S8[Step 8: NDT Testing UT MPI] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Surface Treatment and Primer] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Lift onto Transport Vehicle] ``` ### 4.4 Machinery Installation Sequence (Engine Room Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Step 1: Foundation Cleaning and Chock Leveling] --> Q2[Step 2: Epoxy Grouting and Curing] Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Main Engine Bedplate Mounting] Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Crankshaft and Cylinder Liner Installation] Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Auxiliary Engines and Generators] Q5 --> Q6[Step 6: Pumps Compressors Heat Exchangers] Q6 --> Q7[Step 7: Sea Water Fuel Oil and Lube Oil Piping] Q7 --> Q8[Step 8: Electrical Cable Termination] Q8 --> Q9[Step 9: Shaft Line and Propeller Fitting] Q9 --> Q10[Step 10: Harbour and Sea Trials] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the term “Block Construction Method” in shipbuilding. List any two advantages over the traditional unit construction method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Block Construction Method** is a shipbuilding technological process in which the hull is fabricated as large, three-dimensional prefabricated blocks (typically **200–600 tonnes**) in a covered assembly shop, fitted with pre-outfitted pipe-work, cables, and foundations, and then transported to the building berth/dock for final erection using heavy-duty gantry/goliath cranes. **Two advantages over unit construction:** 1. **Higher productivity:** Work is performed at ground level in workshops, enabling better access, mechanization, and parallel work, reducing overall construction time by **30–40 %**. 2. **Improved quality control:** Welding, NDT, and outfitting can be done in controlled workshop conditions rather than on the open berth, leading to higher dimensional accuracy and reduced rework. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)? Why is it critical to modern shipbuilding productivity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)** is the technological practice of installing outfit items — such as pipe spools, cable trays, ventilation ducts, machinery foundations, and brackets — **inside hull blocks during block fabrication**, rather than after erection on the building berth. **Criticality to productivity:** 1. **Parallel work streams:** It allows outfitting work to proceed in parallel with hull erection, compressing the total project schedule by **40–60 %**. 2. **Improved working conditions:** Work is done at ground level in covered shops instead of high scaffolds in the open, improving safety, weld quality, and labour efficiency. 3. **Reduced post-launch work:** Less work at the fitting-out berth means faster vessel delivery and lower capital lock-up. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **five phases of the hull technological process** with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of CNC cutting in the productivity of modern shipyards. **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to a typical 25 mm thick DH36 plate welded by SAW (Submerged Arc Welding), the following data is given: - Arc voltage $V = 32\,\text{V}$ - Welding current $I = 600\,\text{A}$ - Travel speed $s = 450\,\text{mm/min}$ - Plate yield strength $f_y = 355\,\text{N/mm}^2$ Calculate: 1. Heat input per unit length in kJ/cm 2. Whether the procedure satisfies the **IACS UR W11** limit of 50 kJ/cm 3. Approximate transverse shrinkage using the Terai–Saito relation $\delta_t = 0.16E/t + 0.05g$, assuming root gap $g = 2\,\text{mm}$ **Model Solution:** **(a) Detailed phases:** (Refer to Section 2.1 above for the five-phase description. A student should produce a labelled flowchart similar to Section 4.1, plus a 2–3 line note for each phase: Stockyard → Cutting → Forming → Sub-assembly → Block Assembly → Erection. CNC cutting discussion should highlight nesting optimization (80–85 % material utilization), repeatability, and integration with CAD/CAM nesting software such as **Tribon, Aveva Marine, or SmartShip**.) **(b) Step-by-step calculation:** 1. **Heat Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= \frac{V \cdot I}{s \times 1000} \times 60 \quad \text{[kJ/cm]} \\ &= \frac{32 \times 600}{450 \times 1000} \times 60 \\ &= \frac{19{,}200}{450{,}000} \times 60 \\ &= 0.04267 \times 60 \\ &= 2.56\,\text{kJ/mm} \\ &\Rightarrow 25.6\,\text{kJ/cm} \end{aligned} $$ 2. **Check vs. IACS limit:** $$ 25.6\,\text{kJ/cm} \; < \; 50\,\text{kJ/cm} \quad \checkmark \;\text{Procedure is acceptable.} $$ 3. **Transverse Shrinkage:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_t &= \frac{0.16 \times E_{\text{(J/mm)}}}{t} + 0.05g \\ &= \frac{0.16 \times 2560}{25} + 0.05 \times 2 \\ &= \frac{409.6}{25} + 0.10 \\ &= 16.38 + 0.10 \\ &= 16.48\,\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** A transverse shrinkage of **16.48 mm** must be compensated by **pre-cambering** the plate or by back-step welding sequence to maintain flatness post-weld. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Block Construction Method** with reference to (i) block fabrication sequence, (ii) Pre-Outfitting, and (iii) hull erection. Compare its productivity with the unit construction method. **(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard intends to lift a hull block weighing **380 tonnes** using a gantry crane. The rigging and hook assembly weighs **10 tonnes**. The crane catalogue offers two capacities: **600 t** and **750 t**. The standard safety factor is **1.25** and the dynamic amplification factor at lift-off is **1.15**. Determine: 1. The total dynamic load on the crane. 2. Whether the 600 t crane is adequate; if not, recommend the correct one. 3. Comment on the importance of locating the **centre of gravity** of the block before rigging. **Model Solution:** **(a)** Detailed narrative should cover (i) flat-panel → 3D block workflow in the panel/block line, (ii) Pre-FO insertion of pipe racks/cable trays/foundations during block assembly, (iii) block transport and erection using goliath cranes on the building berth. The comparison table in **Section 3.2** should be reproduced. Emphasize the **60–70 % Pre-FO completion** benchmark. **(b) Numerical Solution:** 1. **Total Dynamic Load:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{tot} &= W_b + W_r = 380 + 10 = 390\,\text{t} \\ W_{dyn} &= W_{tot} \times k_d = 390 \times 1.15 = 448.5\,\text{t} \end{aligned} $$ 2. **Required Rated Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{req} &= W_{dyn} \times k_s = 448.5 \times 1.25 = 560.6\,\text{t} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison with catalogue options:** - 600 t crane → **Adequate** ($600 \geq 560.6$ ✓) - However, considering the 600 t crane is the **absolute minimum**, and for an **80-tonne safety margin** (often preferred for fatigue, slewing, and asymmetric load conditions), the **750 t crane** is the **recommended selection**. 3. **Importance of Centre of Gravity:** The block centre of gravity must be located **before rigging design** to: - Determine the **sling angle** (typically $\geq 60°$ from horizontal) to avoid sling overload. - Prevent **tilt or rotation** during lift. - Ensure **load symmetry** between the crane’s twin hooks (in twin-lift operations). - Calculate the **heeling moment** for the crane and verify that the lifting point does not exceed the block’s **metacentric height**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most common mistake in Part-B numericals:** Students forget to **convert travel speed units** (mm/min vs. cm/min) and end up with heat input off by a factor of 10. Always write units explicitly. > 2. **Theoretical questions:** Examiners award **at least 2 marks** for a labelled **flowchart/sketch**. A text-only answer caps your marks around **10–11/14**. > 3. **Crane capacity questions:** Always show **all three steps** — static load → dynamic load → factored capacity. Skipping the dynamic amplification factor loses **1 full mark**. > 4. **Terminology trap:** Students often confuse **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO)** with **Pre-Equipment Installation**. Pre-FO includes foundations, pipe racks, and cable trays — **not** the actual machinery (which comes in stage outfitting). > 5. **Distortion vs. Tolerance:** Many students write “distortion = tolerance” — examiners deduct marks. **Distortion is the cause; tolerance allowance is the compensation.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Technological Process in Hull and Machinery Installation** - [x] **Technological process** = chronological, planned workflow from raw steel to commissioned vessel. - [x] **Five phases of hull production:** Stockyard → Cutting → Forming → Sub-assembly → Block Assembly → Erection. - [x] **Block Construction Method** dominates modern shipyards due to higher productivity and quality. - [x] **Block weight limit** governed by **gantry/goliath crane capacity** ($P_{crane} \geq 1.25 \cdot 1.15 \cdot W_b$). - [x] **Pre-Outfitting (Pre-FO):** Installing pipes, cables, ducts, and foundations **inside blocks before erection**; target **≥ 70 %** completion. - [x] **Welding heat input formula:** $E = (V \cdot I / s) \times 60 / 1000\,\text{kJ/cm}$; **IACS limit = 50 kJ/cm** for DH36/EH36. - [x] **Transverse shrinkage:** $\delta_t = 0.16E/t + 0.05g$ (Terai–Saito). - [x] **CNC cutting methods:** Laser (< 6 mm), Plasma (6–25 mm), Oxy-fuel (> 25 mm). - [x] **Material nesting efficiency:** Target **80–85 %** of plate area utilized. - [x] **Three tiers of outfitting:** Pre-FO (block), Stage FO (post-erection), Post-FO (after launch). - [x] **Machinery install sequence:** Foundations → Main engine → Auxiliaries → Piping → Cabling → Shaft → Trials. - [x] **Thermal expansion coefficient of steel:** $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$ — use this in all distortion problems. - [x] **Quality standard:** **ISO 9001:2015**; classification rules: **IRS / Lloyd’s / DNV / ABS**. - [x] **Modern trends:** Modular super-block construction; **digital twin** integration; **robotic welding** in panel lines. - [x] **Productivity gains:** Block construction cuts hull cycle by **30–40 %**; Pre-FO adds another **40–60 %** schedule compression.

topics.title: Technological Process in Installing the Main Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Technological Process in Installing the Main Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Technological Process in Installing the Main Machinery** is the defined sequence of preparatory, lifting, positioning, aligning, fixing, and commissioning operations executed on board a ship (or at the building berth) to install the propulsion prime-mover (main engine), reduction gear, shaft line (intermediate + propeller shaft), stern-tube assembly, propeller, and associated auxiliary machinery, in strict compliance with the shipyard's approved Build Strategy, the maker's erection manuals, and the classification society's alignment tolerance rules (e.g., **DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS, ABS**).

The "main machinery" in a marine context refers to the complete propulsion complex, broadly classified as:

| Sub-System | Examples | Typical Unit Weight (Guideline) |
|---|---|---|
| Prime Mover | 2-stroke / 4-stroke marine diesel engine, gas turbine, steam turbine | 50 – 1,200 tonnes |
| Power Transmission | Reduction gear, clutch, flexible coupling | 5 – 80 tonnes |
| Shaft Line | Intermediate shaft(s) + propeller shaft + stern-tube bearings | 10 – 60 tonnes |
| Thrust Bearing | White-metal / tilting-pad thrust bearing housing | 2 – 15 tonnes |
| Propeller | Fixed-pitch or controllable-pitch propeller (CPP) | 5 – 120 tonnes |
| Auxiliary Plant | Generators, purifiers, compressors, boilers, pumps | 0.5 – 30 tonnes each |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (First-time Learner Friendly):**
> Imagine you are assembling a giant Lego Technic racing car on a kitchen table, but the pieces weigh hundreds of kilograms and the slightest 0.10 mm misalignment will make the car vibrate itself to pieces at full speed. That is exactly what shipyard "main-machinery installation" engineers do — except their "table" is a flexing steel hull, the "car" weighs thousands of tonnes, and the engine must drive a propeller spinning at **80 – 120 rpm** through 60 – 80 metres of shafting that may deflect (sag) by several millimetres under its own weight.

In simpler words: **the ship is the chassis, the engine is the heart, the shaft is the spine, the propeller is the legs, and the alignment engineer is the orthopaedic surgeon** who ensures that every "joint" (bearing) carries its correct share of load.

### 1.3 Physical & Operational Constants (Bolded for Recall)

* Ship's **design service speed** typically = **15 – 25 knots** for merchant vessels, up to **30+ knots** for naval combatants.
* Propeller rotational speed for a 2-stroke MAN/B&W main engine = **80 – 120 rpm**.
* Shaft-line alignment accuracy routinely required = **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm** at the coupling flanges (depending on engine builder).
* Standard acceptance reference temperature for shaft alignment measurements = **20 °C (68 °F)** — the *reference datum* for all cold alignment offsets.
* Typical main-engine foundation bolt preload = specified by the maker; for large 2-stroke engines, foundation-resin chocking is preferred over traditional steel chocks for a uniform bearing pressure of **~1.0 N/mm²** to **2.5 N/mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The examiner expects the student to describe the *sequence* of operations, the *alignment philosophy* (cold vs. hot, optical vs. laser), the *role of foundation treatment*, and the *machinery outfitting sequence* — not the thermodynamic design of the engine itself.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Process — Logical Flow

The installation of main machinery is **not** a single act of "placing the engine". It is a meticulously sequenced campaign of work-packages that can be grouped into **seven logical phases**:

**Phase 1 — Engineering & Planning (Pre-Stage at the Drawing Board)**
* Define the *machinery arrangement plan* (longitudinal/transverse/elevational).
* Produce the *shaft alignment calculation sheet* (cold, hot, and half-power conditions).
* Decide the *erection sequence* (top-down / bottom-up / block method).
* Identify the *reference基准 (datum)* — usually a **theoretical shaft line** (TSL) drawn on the general arrangement.

**Phase 2 — Foundation Preparation**
* Shot-blast, grit-blast, and de-grease the tank-top / double-bottom plating under the engine seat.
* Check flatness with a precision straight-edge; tolerance typically **0.05 mm/m**.
* Drill and tap (or tap after welding studs) for foundation bolts.
* Apply epoxy-based or chockfast-type resin chocking where specified.

**Phase 3 — Heavy-Lift Engineering**
* Select the crane (gantry, level-luffing, or sheer-leg) based on the **SWL (Safe Working Load)** curve at the required radius.
* Rig the engine using certified slings and shackles, observing the maker's **centre-of-gravity (CoG)** mark.
* Lower, guide, and pin to the foundation using alignment jacks and lateral pushers.

**Phase 4 — Cold Alignment (Workshop Accuracy)**
* Set the main engine on the TSL using a **laser alignment system** (e.g., Easy-Laser, Prüftechnik) or **optical scope (theodolite + micrometer targets)**.
* Adjust the engine feet via jacking screws until the **offset (Δv)** and **angularity (Δα)** at each coupling fall within the maker's table.
* *Why cold?* Because the engine is not yet warm — no thermal growth to compensate for.

**Phase 5 — Hot Alignment / Sea-Trial Verification**
* After commissioning, the engine's seating grows vertically (thermal expansion of cast iron/steel bedplate ≈ **0.10 – 0.30 mm per metre**).
* The cold alignment is deliberately offset **downward** (or **upward**, per maker's instruction) by a pre-calculated *thermal growth value* so that at normal running temperature the shaft line is geometrically true.
* Final verification: a *crash-astern trial* and *measured-km/litre fuel consumption* check at the sea trial.

**Phase 6 — Connection & Outfitting**
* Bolt-up propeller shaft coupling, lock the thrust bearing, install lube-oil pipes, cooling-water lines, exhaust uptakes, fuel-supply rails, and control air lines.
* Tighten foundation bolts to design torque (commonly **M48 to M72 studs**, torqued to **~1,500 – 4,000 N·m** in stages using a cross-pattern).

**Phase 7 — Commissioning & Sea Trials**
* Turning gear test, chain-out test, dock trial (ahead/astern), and full-power sea trial.

### 2.2 Alignment Philosophy — The Dimensional Chain

> [!NOTE]
> The entire shaft line is treated as a single **dimensional chain** (size chain) in which the propeller position is the *closing link*. All other elements (engine, gear, intermediate shaft bearings, stern-tube bearing) must be positioned so that the cumulative error at the propeller flange lies within tolerance.

The shaft line is normally aligned using one of three industry methods:

| Method | Principle | Typical Accuracy | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical (Sweep-Target / Transit)** | Theodolite + optical micrometer on shaft targets | ±0.02 mm/m | Traditional large warships |
| **Laser (Shaft-Alignment Laser)** | Collimated laser + 2-axis PSD detectors | ±0.01 mm at 10 m | Modern merchant yards |
| **Reverse-Indicator / DTI (Dial Test Indicator)** | DTI on shaft, shaft rotated by half-turn | ±0.03 mm | Workshop bench work |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically standard. Memorise the units and the sign convention.** The reference temperature is **20 °C** unless otherwise stated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Beam (Sag) Deflection of a Shaft on Two Bearings} \\
&\delta \;=\; \frac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I} \quad \text{[mm]} \\
&\text{where } w = \text{weight per unit length (N/mm), } L = \text{span (mm), } E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^2\ (\text{steel}), I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}\ \text{mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(2) Thermal Vertical Growth of Engine Bedplate} \\
&\Delta h \;=\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{[mm]} \\
&\alpha_{\text{cast iron}} \approx 11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K},\quad \alpha_{\text{steel}} \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(3) Offset at Coupling (Angularity)} \\
&\Delta v \;=\; a \cdot \tan(\theta) \;\approx\; a \cdot \theta \quad \text{(for small }\theta\text{)} \\
&\theta \;=\; \frac{\Delta y}{L} \quad \text{[radians]}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(4) Total Permissible Shaft Misalignment (Maker's Empirical)} \\
&\delta_{\text{total,max}} \;\approx\; 0.10\ \text{mm per 1 m of coupling diameter} \quad \text{(typical 2-stroke engine rule-of-thumb)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(5) Foundation-Bolt Pre-load (Torque–Tension Relation)} \\
&T \;=\; k \cdot d \cdot F_{p} \quad \text{[N·m]} \\
&k \approx 0.20\ (\text{lubricated}) \text{ to } 0.30\ (\text{dry}),\ d = \text{nominal bolt diameter (m)},\ F_{p} = \text{preload (N)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(6) Bearing Reaction of a Simply-Supported Shaft (Two Bearings)} \\
&R_{A} \;=\; \frac{W\,(L - a)}{L}, \qquad R_{B} \;=\; \frac{W\,a}{L} \quad \text{[N]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Equation (1) tells the surveyor how much the shaft will *sag* between bearings when only gravity acts. If the shaft is overhung (e.g., propeller-end cantilever), the surveyor must lift the propeller end by exactly the computed deflection **before** tightening the coupling — otherwise, when the ship floats, the shaft will "relax" into a straight line and the alignment will be lost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Sag Deflection of a Horizontal Shaft (Classical Beam Theory)

**Given:** A solid forged-steel intermediate shaft, diameter $d = 0.40\ \text{m}$, span between two bearings $L = 6.0\ \text{m}$, weight per unit length $w = (7850\ \text{kg/m}^3) \times (\pi \times 0.4^2 / 4) \times 9.81 \approx 9{,}670\ \text{N/m}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the second moment of area.**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (0.40)^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 0.0256}{64} \;\approx\; 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert weight per unit length into N/mm for consistency.**

$$
w \;=\; 9{,}670\ \text{N/m} \;\times\; \frac{1\ \text{m}}{1000\ \text{mm}} \;=\; 9.67\ \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simply-supported, uniformly-loaded beam formula.**

$$
L \;=\; 6.0\ \text{m} \;=\; 6{,}000\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{5 \times 9.67 \times (6000)^{4}}{384 \times (2.1 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.2566 \times 10^{3})}
$$

(We must convert $I$ from m⁴ to mm⁴: $I = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{4} \times (1000)^{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{9}\ \text{mm}^{4}$.)

**Step 4 — Substitute numerically.**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{5 \times 9.67 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15}}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{9}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerator:**

$$
5 \times 9.67 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15} \;\approx\; 6.266 \times 10^{16}
$$

**Step 6 — Denominator:**

$$
384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{9} \;\approx\; 1.014 \times 10^{17}
$$

**Step 7 — Final division:**

$$
\delta \;\approx\; \frac{6.266 \times 10^{16}}{1.014 \times 10^{17}} \;\approx\; 0.618\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The intermediate shaft will sag by approximately **0.62 mm** between its bearings. The alignment engineer must therefore *lift* the after-end of the shaft by **0.62 mm** relative to the forward-end when cold-aligning on the berth, so that when the ship is water-borne (and the shaft "un-sags" to straight), the alignment is geometrically perfect.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Thermal Growth of the Engine Bedplate

**Given:** A 6-cylinder 2-stroke marine diesel engine, bedplate length $L = 8.0\ \text{m}$, made of cast iron ($\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$). Running temperature of bedplate = **60 °C** above ambient (assumed ambient = 20 °C).

**Step 1 — Temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 60\ - 20 \;=\; 40\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply linear thermal-expansion formula.**

$$
\Delta h \;=\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta h \;=\; 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 8000\ \text{mm} \times 40
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
\Delta h \;=\; 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 320{,}000 \;\approx\; 3.52\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The aft end of the engine will rise by **3.52 mm** when hot. Therefore, the *cold* alignment must be set **3.52 mm LOW** at the aft end (i.e., the engine is tilted slightly nose-up on the berth) so that it becomes perfectly level at full-load service temperature. This is the cornerstone of *hot-alignment compensation*.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Permissible Offset at a Coupling

**Given:** Coupling flange diameter $D = 0.80\ \text{m}$. Maker's rule: **0.10 mm offset per 1 m diameter**.

**Step 1 — Apply the empirical rule.**

$$
\delta_{\text{max}} \;=\; 0.10\ \text{mm/m} \times 0.80\ \text{m} \;=\; 0.08\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to micrometres for the surveyor.**

$$
\delta_{\text{max}} \;=\; 80\ \mu\text{m}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Engine-Type vs. Installation Strategy

| Engine Type | Typical RPM | Reduction Gear | Cold-Align Method | Foundation Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-speed 2-stroke diesel (e.g., MAN B&W G95) | 80 – 100 | None (direct drive) | Laser + engine-room jacking | Epoxy resin chock |
| Medium-speed 4-stroke diesel (e.g., Wärtsilä W46) | 500 – 720 | Yes (ratio 3:1 to 6:1) | Laser + gear jacking | Steel chock or resin |
| Geared gas turbine (e.g., LM2500) | 3,600 (turbine) | Yes (≈ 25:1) | Laser + high-precision shim | Special anti-vibration mount |
| Steam turbine (nuclear/conventional) | 3,000 – 3,600 | Yes (double reduction) | Optical (precision heritage) | Spring-mount + chock |

### 3.5 Engine-Room Outfitting Sequence (The "Build-Down" Method)

The order of *outfitting around* an already-set main engine follows a strict **inside-out, lower-upper** rule:

1. **Below-floor:** Sea-water cooling suction, overboard discharges, lube-oil sump tank.
2. **Floor level:** Lube-oil purifier foundations, fuel-oil booster pump bases.
3. **Engine platforms:** Starting-air receivers, control-air bottles, fuel-oil service tanks.
4. **Engine entablature:** Turbocharger, exhaust manifold, charge-air cooler.
5. **Above:** Exhaust uptakes, funnel gas uptakes, engine-room crane rails.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Listing the exhaust system before the turbocharger. The turbocharger sits *on* the engine and must be rigged *with* the engine or immediately after — it is not a separate outfitting item in the sequence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Master Installation Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block complies with the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (no `**`, no `*`, no HTML inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START Berth Preparation])
    a1[Phase 1 Engineering and Datum Definition]
    a2[Phase 2 Foundation Shotblast and Bolt Drilling]
    a3[Phase 3 Heavy Lift Plan and Crane Selection]
    a4[Phase 4A Engine Set on TSL]
    a5[Phase 4B Reduction Gear Set on TSL]
    a6[Phase 4C Intermediate Shaft and Bearings Set]
    a7[Phase 4D Propeller Shaft Set into Stern Tube]
    a8[Phase 4E Propeller Hoist and Key-on]
    b1[Phase 5 Cold Alignment by Laser]
    b2[Phase 5B Apply Sag and Thermal Offsets]
    c1[Phase 6 Foundation Bolt Torque and Lockwire]
    c2[Phase 6B Piping Electrical and Control Hookup]
    d1[Phase 7 Dock Trial Ahead Astern]
    d2[Phase 7B Sea Trial Performance Verification]
    endNode([END Handover to Owner])

    startNode --> a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4
    a3 --> a5
    a4 --> a6
    a5 --> a6
    a6 --> a7 --> a8
    a8 --> b1 --> b2 --> c1 --> c2 --> d1 --> d2 --> endNode
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shaft-Line Power Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainEng[MAIN ENGINE 2 STROKE DIESEL]
    thrustBrg[THRUST BEARING HOUSING]
    intmShaft[INTERMEDIATE SHAFT]
    sternTube[STERN TUBE BEARING]
    propShaft[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    propeller[PROPELLER FIXED OR CPP]
    coupling1[FLEXIBLE COUPLING 1]
    coupling2[FLEXIBLE COUPLING 2]
    seal1[STERN TUBE SEALING AFT]
    seal2[STERN TUBE SEALING FWD]
    gearbox[REDUCTION GEAR OPTIONAL]

    mainEng --> thrustBrg
    thrustBrg --> coupling1
    coupling1 --> gearbox
    gearbox --> coupling2
    coupling2 --> intmShaft
    intmShaft --> sternTube
    sternTube --> seal2
    seal2 --> propShaft
    propShaft --> seal1
    seal1 --> propeller
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Alignment-Compensation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    measuredCold[MEASURED COLD ALIGNMENT]
    sagDelta[SAG DEFLECTION EQ 1]
    thermalDelta[THERMAL GROWTH EQ 2]
    makerTol[MAKER TOLERANCE TABLE]

    measuredCold --> compCalc{COMPUTE COMPENSATION VECTOR}
    sagDelta --> compCalc
    thermalDelta --> compCalc
    makerTol --> compCalc

    compCalc --> applyOffset[APPLY VERTICAL AND HORIZONTAL OFFSETS TO ENGINE FEET JACKS]
    applyOffset --> relock[RELOCK FOUNDATION BOLTS]
    relock --> verify[FINAL LASER VERIFY WITHIN 0.05 MM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram interpretation tip for KTU exam:** The examiner may give a printed block diagram and ask you to *label the compensation vectors* or to *identify the closing link of the dimensional chain*. The closing link is **always the propeller position** in a single-screw ship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
Define the *theoretical shaft line* (TSL). Why is it used as the reference datum for machinery alignment?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Theoretical Shaft Line (TSL) is an imaginary straight line passing through the centres of the **main engine output flange, reduction-gear output flange (if fitted), all intermediate-shaft bearings, the stern-tube bearing, and the propeller flange bore**. It is drawn on the ship's general-arrangement plan and serves as the reference *closing line* of the shafting dimensional chain. The alignment of every component is measured *relative* to this line, never to the ship's plating or keel, because the hull flexes and twists under seaway loads whereas the shaft line must remain geometrically true. By positioning all components onto a common TSL, the surveyor guarantees that the cumulative error at the propeller end lies within the maker's tolerance.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
List **four** preparatory operations that must be completed on the engine foundation *before* the main engine is lifted in.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Cleaning and de-greasing** of the foundation plating (shot-blast to Sa 2.5).
> 2. **Flatness check** with a precision straight-edge — tolerance ≤ 0.05 mm/m.
> 3. **Drilling and tapping** of foundation bolt holes (or stud welding) per the maker's template.
> 4. **Dry-fit check** of chock plates and preparation of the **epoxy resin chocking** bed (if specified).

*(Each item carries 0.75 marks; a 5th valid point may be given as a bonus.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 pattern**, the student is given an internal choice. Both questions below are 14-mark problems with two 7-mark sub-parts.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2 Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **dimensional chain method** of aligning a marine shaft line. Explain the terms *closing link* and *tolerance stack-up*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply-supported intermediate shaft has the following data: length between bearings $L = 7.5\ \text{m}$, diameter $d = 0.42\ \text{m}$, material = forged steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^2$, density $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$). Calculate the **sag deflection** of the shaft at mid-span due to its own weight. What alignment offset must the surveyor apply to compensate? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to A(a):**

* **Dimensional chain (size chain)** — A dimensional chain is a series of individual dimensions of consecutive components (engine position, gear position, bearing heights, etc.) that, when added or subtracted, determine the position of the *closing link* — which, in a single-screw ship, is the **propeller-shaft flange centre**.
* The chain is closed when the sum of *increasing* links equals the sum of *decreasing* links within the prescribed tolerance.
* **Tolerance stack-up** is the statistical (or worst-case) accumulation of the individual tolerances of each link; classification societies limit the total stack-up to typically **0.10 – 0.20 mm** at the propeller.
* The block diagram should show: Engine → Coupling 1 → Gear → Coupling 2 → Int-Shaft 1 → Bearing 1 → Int-Shaft 2 → Bearing 2 → Stern-Tube Bearing → Propeller-Shaft → Propeller.

**Model Solution to A(b):**

*Step 1 —* Cross-section area of shaft:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.42)^{2}}{4} \;\approx\; 0.1385\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 2 —* Weight per unit length:

$$
w \;=\; \rho \cdot A \cdot g \;=\; 7850 \times 0.1385 \times 9.81 \;\approx\; 10{,}670\ \text{N/m} \;\approx\; 10.67\ \text{N/mm}
$$

*Step 3 —* Second moment of area:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.42)^{4}}{64} \;\approx\; 1.222 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{4} \;=\; 1.222 \times 10^{9}\ \text{mm}^{4}
$$

*Step 4 —* Convert length to mm:

$$
L \;=\; 7.5\ \text{m} \;=\; 7{,}500\ \text{mm}
$$

*Step 5 —* Apply the sag formula:

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10.67 \times (7500)^{4}}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1.222 \times 10^{9}}
$$

*Step 6 —* Numerator:

$$
5 \times 10.67 \times 3.164 \times 10^{15} \;\approx\; 1.688 \times 10^{17}
$$

*Step 7 —* Denominator:

$$
384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1.222 \times 10^{9} \;\approx\; 9.857 \times 10^{16}
$$

*Step 8 —* Final division:

$$
\delta \;\approx\; \frac{1.688 \times 10^{17}}{9.857 \times 10^{16}} \;\approx\; 1.71\ \text{mm}
$$

*Step 9 —* **Conclusion:** The surveyor must *raise the after end of the shaft by 1.71 mm* during cold alignment so that, in the water-borne condition (where gravity straightens the shaft), the alignment is true.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2 Module 2 - Alternative]`

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step technological sequence** of installing a low-speed two-stroke main propulsion diesel engine in a new-build merchant ship, from foundation preparation to sea trial. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast-iron main-engine bedplate has a length of **9.0 m**. The cold alignment is carried out at **20 °C**. At normal service load, the bedplate temperature rises to **65 °C**. Calculate the **vertical thermal growth** of the aft end of the bedplate. What *cold-offset* must the alignment engineer apply to ensure perfect alignment at service temperature? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to B(a):**

*Step 1 —* Foundation shot-blasting and flatness check.
*Step 2 —* Marking of the TSL and engine centre-line on the foundation.
*Step 3 —* Heavy-lift using the sheer-legs or gantry crane.
*Step 4 —* Engine set down on jacking screws.
*Step 5 —* Initial *rough* alignment using steel rules.
*Step 6 —* Final *cold* alignment using laser (Easy-Laser/Prüftechnik).
*Step 7 —* Foundation bolt tightening in cross-pattern to design torque.
*Step 8 —* Chock resin pouring (if epoxy chock) and curing.
*Step 9 —* Connection of exhaust, cooling, lube-oil, and fuel systems.
*Step 10 —* Turning-gear test, dock trial (ahead/astern), sea trial.

**Model Solution to B(b):**

*Step 1 —* Identify the coefficient of linear expansion of cast iron:

$$
\alpha \;=\; 11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}
$$

*Step 2 —* Compute the temperature difference:

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 65\ - 20 \;=\; 45\ \text{K}
$$

*Step 3 —* Apply thermal expansion:

$$
\Delta h \;=\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 9000\ \text{mm} \times 45
$$

*Step 4 —* Numerically:

$$
\Delta h \;=\; 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 405{,}000 \;\approx\; 4.46\ \text{mm}
$$

*Step 5 —* **Conclusion:** The bedplate's aft end will grow **4.46 mm** vertically when hot. Therefore, at cold alignment, the engine must be set **4.46 mm LOWER** at the aft end than its final hot position. In practice, the engine is jacked down at the aft end by this amount before the foundation bolts are locked.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reference temperature** (20 °C). Any alignment problem without a stated reference temperature is incomplete — the examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up mm and m** in the beam-deflection formula. Always convert **everything to mm** before substitution.
> 3. **Omitting the gravity straightening correction** — students often give only the thermal offset and forget the *sag* offset, losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Using the wrong α** — for *cast-iron* bedplate use $11 \times 10^{-6}$; for *steel* bedplate use $12 \times 10^{-6}$.
> 5. **Listing steps in the wrong order** in the installation sequence — e.g., saying "tighten bolts" *before* "align". Always align first, then lock.
> 6. **Confusing "cold alignment" with "hot alignment"** — cold alignment is performed on the berth *with* thermal/sag offsets deliberately added; hot alignment is the *verification* at running temperature.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module-2 Examination**

* [ ] **Main machinery** = prime mover + transmission + shaft line + propeller + auxiliaries.
* [ ] **Theoretical Shaft Line (TSL)** is the imaginary reference line connecting engine, gear, intermediate bearings, stern-tube, and propeller.
* [ ] **Closing link of the dimensional chain** is the *propeller position* (single-screw ship).
* [ ] **Installation phases (7):** Plan → Foundation → Lift → Set → Cold-align → Connect → Trial.
* [ ] **Sag deflection formula:** $\delta = 5 w L^{4} / (384 E I)$ — surveyor must *lift* the shaft by this amount.
* [ ] **Thermal growth formula:** $\Delta h = \alpha L \Delta T$ — surveyor must *set down* the engine by this amount.
* [ ] **Reference temperature** for all calculations = **20 °C**.
* [ ] **Cast-iron α** = $11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$; **Steel α** = $12 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$.
* [ ] **Typical alignment tolerance** = 0.05 – 0.15 mm at coupling; **0.10 mm/m of coupling diameter** is the empirical rule of thumb.
* [ ] **Foundation treatment:** flatness ≤ 0.05 mm/m; epoxy resin chock for large 2-stroke engines; steel chocks for medium-speed.
* [ ] **Alignment instruments:** laser (modern), optical transit (legacy), reverse-indicator (workshop).
* [ ] **Foundation-bolt tightening:** cross-pattern, multi-stage, to maker's specified torque (commonly $k = 0.20$ for lubricated).
* [ ] **Sea trial verifications:** turning-gear → dock trial → crash-astern → measured-mile fuel-rate check.
* [ ] **Outfitting sequence rule:** *inside-out, lower-upper*; turbocharger follows the engine, never precedes it.
* [ ] **Reduction gear** is required when the prime mover's rated speed is higher than the propeller's optimal speed (e.g., 4-stroke diesels, gas turbines).
* [ ] **Thrust bearing** absorbs the propeller's ahead/astern thrust; must be aligned within 0.05 mm of TSL.
* [ ] **Stern-tube bearings** are typically white-metal or composite; lubricated by seawater or oil.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation of Shafting and Propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Installation of Shafting and Propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Installation of Shafting — Formal Definition

**Shafting installation** refers to the complete engineering process of assembling, aligning, supporting, and sealing the marine propulsion shaft system that transmits power from the main engine (or reduction gear) to the propeller. The shaft line typically comprises the **thrust shaft**, **intermediate shaft(s)**, and **tail (propeller) shaft**, supported by bearings and passing through the **stern tube** to the propeller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Installation of shafting is a **Module 2** core topic under PENST632 — Shipbuilding Technology. Questions typically test alignment tolerances, bearing arrangements, and propeller fitting methods.

## 1.2 Installation of Propeller — Formal Definition

**Propeller installation** is the procedure of mounting the marine propeller on the tapered forward end of the **tail shaft** (also called the propeller shaft) using methods such as **keyed taper fit**, **keyless shrink fit**, or **flange coupling (bolted)**. The propeller is the rotating hydrofoil that converts engine torque into thrust.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spine and the Feet

Think of a ship as a human body:
- The **shafting** is the **spine** — a long, rigid load-bearing member that transmits power and supports rotation.
- The **propeller** is the **feet** — the final interface that pushes against the environment (water) to make the body move.
- The **bearings** are the **discs between vertebrae** — they hold alignment and reduce friction.
- The **stern tube** is the **pelvic girdle** — a sealed structural passage through which the spine exits the body.

Just as a misalignment of the spine causes pain and inefficiency, a misaligned shafting causes **vibration, bearing wear, and loss of propulsive efficiency**. The propeller must be fitted with the right **interference (press-fit)** to handle enormous torque, just as a hip joint must be tightly fitted to bear body weight.

## 1.4 Key Components at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Components of Marine Shafting & Propeller Assembly:**
> - **Thrust Shaft** — transmits engine thrust to the hull via a thrust bearing.
> - **Intermediate Shaft(s)** — connect thrust shaft to tail shaft via flange couplings.
> - **Tail (Propeller) Shaft** — supports the propeller; runs through the stern tube.
> - **Stern Tube** — watertight passage housing the after-most bearing (A-bracket / P-bracket / strut bearing).
> - **White Metal Bearings** — lined with Babbitt metal for low-friction support.
> - **Stuffing Box** — sealing arrangement using gland packing or lip seals.
> - **Propeller** — 3, 4, or 5-bladed fixed-pitch or controllable-pitch propeller (CPP).
> - **Coupling Flanges** — connect shaft sections, allow disassembly for maintenance.

**Standard metric used in classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, IRS):** The shaft diameter is determined by empirical formulas such as the **Baudouin / Schaffran formula** based on engine power and RPM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequence of Shafting Installation

The installation follows a strict sequence (from forward to aft):

1. **Foundation preparation** — chock beds (holding-down bolts) for the main engine and thrust block are aligned.
2. **Thrust bearing assembly** — fitted on the thrust shaft; absorbs axial thrust.
3. **Thrust shaft placement** — aligned with the engine crankshaft / gearbox output.
4. **Intermediate shaft coupling** — flange couplings bolted using fitted bolts (clearance or interference).
5. **Tail shaft insertion** — the propeller shaft is slid through the stern tube.
6. **Stern tube bearing lining** — white metal bearing is scraped to fit (lignum vitae, rubber, or synthetic).
7. **Stern tube sealing** — stuffing box gland packing or modern lip seals.
8. **Propeller fitting** — keyed taper, keyless shrink, or bolted flange.
9. **Alignment check** — using dial gauges, laser alignment, or feeler gauges; deflection checks cold and hot.
10. **Sea trials** — vibration, temperature, and bearing performance verified under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why alignment matters:** A misaligned shaft causes **.05 to 0.10 mm** deflection per meter can cause severe vibration, oil-whirl in bearings, and fatigue failure. Classification societies specify **cold alignment offset** to compensate for thermal expansion of the hull (which hogs/sags as the ship warms).

## 2.2 Propeller Fitting Methods — Detailed

### A. Keyed Taper Fit (Traditional)
- The propeller boss has a **tapered bore**, and the tail shaft has a matching **taper** (typically 1:10, 1:12, or 1:15).
- A **keyway** is cut in both, and a **key** transmits torque.
- Torque is also transmitted by **friction** from the press fit.

### B. Keyless Shrink Fit (Modern, High Torque)
- No key; torque transmitted entirely by **friction** from a very tight **interference (negative tolerance)** fit.
- Propeller is heated (typically 150°C to 200°C) and dropped on the cold shaft.
- Suitable for **CPP** and large fixed-pitch propellers where high torque density is needed.

### C. Bolted Flange Coupling
- Propeller is mounted on a **separate flange** bolted to the shaft.
- Easier to remove; used in smaller vessels and CPP systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taper $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | Standard taper on tail shaft | dimensionless |
| 2 | Standard Taper Ratios | 1:10 (small), 1:12 (medium), 1:15 (large) | — |
| 3 | Shrink Fit Pressure $p = \dfrac{\delta \cdot E}{2 D^2} \cdot (D^2 - d^2)$ | Lamé's equation (thick cylinder) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 4 | Torque Capacity (Friction) $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot L \cdot D^2$ | Torsional transmission by friction | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 5 | Interference $\delta$ = $D_{hub} - d_{shaft}$ | Difference between hub bore and shaft | $\text{mm}$ |
| 6 | Baudouin formula: $D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Tail shaft diameter | $\text{mm}$ |
| 7 | Hot-fit Temperature Rise $\Delta T = \dfrac{\delta}{\alpha \cdot D} + 50°\text{C}$ | Heating needed for shrink fit | °C |
| 8 | Coupling Bolt Torque $T_b = 0.2 \cdot F_{pre} \cdot d_b$ | Bolt tightening torque | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 9 | Bearing Clearance Ratio | $c/d = 0.001$ to $0.002$ | — |
| 10 | Stern Tube Bearing Length $L_b = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot D_{shaft}$ | Empirical rule | $\text{mm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $D$ = hub outer diameter, $d$ = shaft diameter, $L$ = taper length, $\delta$ = interference, $E$ = Young's modulus (≈ $2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for steel), $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (≈ 0.12–0.18 steel-on-bronze), $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient (≈ $11 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for steel), $P$ = power (kW), $N$ = RPM, $k$ = empirical constant.**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Alignment engineering** is critical in turbo-machinery beyond ships — turbines, pumps, gearboxes.
- **Shrink fit analysis** using Lamé's equations is used in flywheels, gear hubs, and railway wheels.
- **White metal bearing scraping** is a foundational skill in heavy rotating machinery maintenance.
- **Sealing technology** (stuffing box vs. lip seal vs. airseal) influences vessel efficiency and **pollution prevention** (IMOS — IMO regulations on shaft seal leakage).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shrink Fit Pressure (Lamé's Equation)

The interference fit creates a **contact pressure $p$** between the propeller hub (outer cylinder) and the shaft (inner cylinder). This pressure generates the **normal force** that produces friction and hence torque transmission.

### Given:
- $D$ = hub outer (or interface) diameter, $d$ = shaft diameter.
- Interference $\delta$ = $D_{bore} - d_{shaft}$.
- Shaft is solid, hub is a thick cylinder.

### Step 1 — Compatibility of Radial Displacement
For a **solid shaft** under external pressure $p$, the radial outward displacement of the shaft surface is:

$$
u_{shaft} = -\frac{p \cdot d}{E} \cdot (1 - \nu)
$$

For a **thick hub** under internal pressure $p$, the radial inward displacement of the hub bore is given by Lamé's equation:

$$
u_{hub} = -\frac{p \cdot d}{E} \cdot \left( \frac{D^2 + d^2}{D^2 - d^2} + \nu \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Interference Equation
The total interference equals the sum of the magnitude of radial displacements (inwards into the interface):

$$
\delta = -u_{shaft} - u_{hub} = \frac{p \cdot d}{E} \left[ (1 - \nu) + \frac{D^2 + d^2}{D^2 - d^2} + \nu \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Simplification
Since $(1 - \nu) + \nu = 1$, the equation simplifies to:

$$
\delta = \frac{p \cdot d}{E} \left[ 1 + \frac{D^2 + d^2}{D^2 - d^2} \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Contact Pressure
Solving for $p$:

$$
p = \frac{\delta \cdot E}{d \left[ 1 + \dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{D^2 - d^2} \right]}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(D^2 - d^2)$:

$$
p = \frac{\delta \cdot E \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{d \left[ (D^2 - d^2) + (D^2 + d^2) \right]} = \frac{\delta \cdot E \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{2 d \cdot D^2}
$$

### Final Working Equation:

$$
\boxed{\, p = \frac{\delta \cdot E \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{2 d \cdot D^2} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Larger interference $\delta$ → higher contact pressure $p$ → higher torque capacity but also higher **hub hoop stress**. If $p$ exceeds the yield strength of bronze, the hub cracks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Capacity of Shrink Fit

Once $p$ is known, the **axial (friction) force** on the conical interface is found by integrating the normal force over the conical surface. For a taper of slope $T = (D - d)/L$:

### Step 1 — Normal Force on Tapered Surface
The total normal force on the conical seat is:

$$
F_N = \int_0^L p \cdot \pi \cdot D(x) \cdot dx
$$

where $D(x) = d + (D - d) \cdot x / L$. Integrating:

$$
F_N = \pi \cdot p \cdot L \cdot \frac{(D + d)}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Friction Force (Axial Holding)
The friction force holding the propeller on the shaft is:

$$
F_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_N = \mu \cdot \pi \cdot p \cdot L \cdot \frac{D + d}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Torque Capacity
The torque is transmitted at the **mean radius** $R_m = (D + d)/4$:

$$
T_{cap} = F_{fric} \cdot R_m = \mu \cdot \pi \cdot p \cdot L \cdot \frac{D + d}{2} \cdot \frac{D + d}{4}
$$

### Final Working Equation:

$$
\boxed{\, T_{cap} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \mu \cdot \pi \cdot p \cdot L \cdot (D + d)^2 \,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Propeller Fitting Methods

| Parameter | Keyed Taper Fit | Keyless Shrink Fit | Bolted Flange |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torque transmission** | Key + friction | Friction only | Friction + bolt shear |
| **Interference** | Light (0.05–0.1 mm) | Heavy (0.2–0.4 mm) | None (clearance fit) |
| **Heating required** | No (or light) | Yes (150–200°C) | No |
| **Removal difficulty** | Moderate | High (requires hydraulic puller) | Easy (unbolt) |
| **Stress concentration** | At keyway | Smooth (no notch) | At bolt holes |
| **Application** | Small/medium vessels | Large tankers, naval | CPP, small craft |
| **Taper ratio** | 1:10, 1:12, 1:15 | 1:12, 1:15 | None |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A propeller hub has bore diameter $D = 0.500$ m, shaft diameter $d = 0.400$ m, length of taper $L = 1.2$ m, interference $\delta = 0.30$ mm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\mu = 0.15$.

### Step 1 — Convert to consistent units
$\delta = 0.30$ mm; $D = 500$ mm; $d = 400$ mm; $L = 1200$ mm.

### Step 2 — Calculate Shrink Pressure
$$
p = \frac{0.30 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (500^2 - 400^2)}{2 \times 400 \times 500^2}
$$

Computing the squares: $500^2 = 250000$, $400^2 = 160000$.

$$
p = \frac{0.30 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (90000)}{2 \times 400 \times 250000}
$$

$$
p = \frac{0.30 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 90000}{2 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
p = \frac{5.4 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^8} = 27 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Torque Capacity
$$
T_{cap} = \frac{1}{8} \times 0.15 \times \pi \times 27 \times 1200 \times (500 + 400)^2
$$

$$
T_{cap} = \frac{1}{8} \times 0.15 \times 3.1416 \times 27 \times 1200 \times 810000
$$

Step-by-step:
- $0.15 \times 3.1416 = 0.4712$
- $0.4712 \times 27 = 12.72$
- $12.72 \times 1200 = 15268$
- $15268 \times 810000 = 1.237 \times 10^{10}$
- Divide by 8: $T_{cap} = 1.546 \times 10^9$ N·mm = **1546 kN·m**

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **maximum** static torque the friction fit can transmit. The service torque is typically $1/3$ to $1/2$ of this value, providing a safety factor of 2–3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Marine Shafting Arrangement — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Engine] --> B[Thrust Bearing Block]
    B --> C[Thrust Shaft]
    C --> D[Coupling Flange 1]
    D --> E[Intermediate Shaft 1]
    E --> F[Coupling Flange 2]
    F --> G[Intermediate Shaft 2]
    G --> H[Coupling Flange 3]
    H --> I[Tail Shaft]
    I --> J[Stern Tube Bearing]
    I --> K[Stuffing Box Seal]
    I --> L[Propeller Taper]
    L --> M[Propeller Hub]
    M --> N[Propeller Blades]
    J --> O[After Peak Bulkhead]
    K --> P[Aft Sealant Gland]
```

## 4.2 Propeller Fitting Methods Comparison — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Propeller Installation] --> B{Vessel Size and Type}
    B -->|Small / Medium| C{Torque Demand}
    B -->|Large Tanker / Naval| D[Keyless Shrink Fit]
    C -->|Moderate| E[Keyed Taper Fit]
    C -->|High with CPP| F[Bolted Flange Coupling]
    E --> G[Cut Taper 1 to 10 on Shaft]
    G --> H[Machine Keyway]
    H --> I[Press or Light Heat Fit]
    I --> J[Insert Key and Secure Nut]
    D --> K[Heat Propeller 150 to 200 C]
    K --> L[Drop onto Cold Shaft]
    L --> M[Cool and Lock with Stop Nut]
    F --> N[Mount Flange on Tapered Shaft]
    N --> O[Bolt Propeller to Flange]
    O --> P[Torque Tighten Bolts]
```

## 4.3 Stern Tube Sealing Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sea Water Side] --> B[Stern Tube A Bracket Bearing]
    B --> C[White Metal Lining]
    C --> D[Stuffing Box Gland]
    D --> E[Gland Packing Rings]
    E --> F[Lip Seal Optional]
    F --> G[Inboard Seal]
    G --> H[Oil Lubrication Chamber]
    H --> I[Forward Seal]
    I --> J[Engine Room Side]
```

## 4.4 Alignment Compensation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cold Alignment Measurement] --> B{Deflection Value}
    B -->|Above Tolerance| C[Adjust Chock Shims]
    C --> D[Re-measure Cold Alignment]
    B -->|Within Tolerance| E[Apply Hot Offset Correction]
    E --> F[Verify with Dial Gauge or Laser]
    F --> G[Lock Foundation Bolts]
    G --> H[Run Sea Trials]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
