**Step 1.** Differential form:

$$
\frac{dL}{dT} \;=\; \alpha \cdot L
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate between $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
\int_{L_1}^{L_2} \frac{dL}{L} \;=\; \alpha \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT
$$

**Step 3.** Result:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right) \;=\; \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 4.** For small $\alpha \Delta T$, Taylor-expansion gives:

$$
L_2 \;\approx\; L_1 \cdot (1 + \alpha \Delta T)
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$
\Delta L \;=\; L_1 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Numerical example (KTU typical):**
- $L = 25$ m, $\alpha = 0.012$ mm/(m·°C) for steel, $\Delta T = 180$ °C (cold → hot steam line)

$$
\Delta L \;=\; 25 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 180 \;=\; 54\;\text{mm}
$$

A **54 mm expansion** must be absorbed — either by an expansion loop, an Omega-bend, or a **bellows / compensator**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Flow Rate and Velocity Check

Given pump discharge required: $Q = 30$ m³/h for fire-fighting main, pipe $D_{in} = 100$ mm.

**Step 1.** Convert units:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{30}{3600} \;=\; 0.00833\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2.** Cross-sectional area:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \;=\; 0.7854 \cdot (0.1)^{2} \;=\; 0.007854\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Velocity:

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{0.00833}{0.007854} \;\approx\; 1.06\;\text{m/s}
$$

This is acceptable for fire main (recommended 1.0 – 2.5 m/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity rule of thumb:** Velocity for sea-water: 1.5 – 2.5 m/s; for steam: 25 – 40 m/s; for lube oil: 1.0 – 2.0 m/s. Going below 0.5 m/s invites sediment; going above 3 m/s causes erosion in Cu-Ni.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Workflow — Marine Pipe Installation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipbuilding Contract and Class Rules] --> B[Piping Engineering Design]
    B --> B1[P and ID Drawing]
    B --> B2[Pipe Routing Layout]
    B --> B3[Material Take Off MTO]
    B1 --> C[Workshop Prefabrication]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Pipe Cutting and Beveling]
    C1 --> C2[Pipe Bending Mandrel or Induction]
    C2 --> C3[Spool Assembly and Tack Welding]
    C3 --> D[Onboard Erection]
    D --> D1[Lifting Spool to Position]
    D1 --> D2[Temporary Tack Welding or Clamping]
    D2 --> E[Final Alignment and Fit Up]
    E --> F[Final Welding or Bolting of Flanges]
    F --> G[Non Destructive Testing NDT]
    G --> G1[Hydrostatic Pressure Test 1.5 times Design]
    G1 --> H[Cleaning Flushing Blowing Through]
    H --> I[Painting and Lagging Insulation]
    I --> J[Commissioning and Sea Trials]
    J --> K[Class Surveyor Sign Off]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Design Input] --> EN[Engineering]
    EN --> PROC[Procurement]
    EN --> FAB[Fabrication Shop]
    PROC --> FAB
    FAB --> PRE[Prefabricated Spools]
    PRE --> SHIP[Ship Erection Stage]
    SHIP --> WELD[Final Welding and Flanging]
    WELD --> TEST[Pressure Testing]
    TEST --> QC[Quality Control and NDT]
    QC --> COM[Commissioning]
    COM --> DEL[Delivery to Owner]
```

### 4.3 Pipe Support Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Pipe size and temperature?}
    Q1 -->|Small bore, ambient| S1[Simple U Clamp and Guide]
    Q1 -->|Large bore, ambient| S2[Pipe Shoe on Saddle Support]
    Q1 -->|High temperature steam| S3[Spring Hanger or Constant Load Hanger]
    Q1 -->|Vibration prone near engine| S4[Flexible Bellows and Anti Vibration Mount]
    Q1 -->|At hull penetration| S5[Bulkhead Penetrating Piece with Welded Boss]
```

### 4.4 Penetration Through Watertight Bulkhead (Schematic Description)

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional flow of a watertight pipe penetration:**
> Hull side → Pipe flange → Welded boss (reinforcement pad) → Pipe spool → Bulkhead piece (welded to plate) → Far-side spool → Next flange.
> Reinforcement pads must be ≥ 1.5× the cut-out diameter; weld is full-penetration and 100% radiographed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define pipe class as used in marine piping practice. List any four classes with their typical services.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Pipe class is a classification system grouping pipes by **service, design pressure, and material**. (1 mark)
> *Examples:*
> - **Class I** — Main steam (high pressure, carbon steel) (0.5 mark)
> - **Class II** — Fuel oil transfer (medium-high pressure) (0.5 mark)
> - **Class III** — Sea water cooling (Cu-Ni 90/10) (0.5 mark)
> - **Class IV** — Bilge and freshwater (low pressure, GI) (0.5 mark)

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Why is a hydrostatic test pressure set to 1.5 times the design pressure? What medium is used?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Hydrostatic test pressure is set at **1.5 × design pressure** to verify the integrity of joints, weld seams, and material against any manufacturing defect, with a built-in safety margin. (2 marks) The medium used is **fresh water** (sometimes with corrosion inhibitor) — never sea water for steel pipes, to avoid chloride-induced cracking during the test. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q A.)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]`
> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for **prefabrication and erection of marine piping systems** onboard a ship. Discuss the role of **pipe hangers and supports**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A Cu-Ni 90/10 sea-water cooling line has an outside diameter of **114.3 mm**, design pressure **5 bar**, allowable stress **75 MPa**, weld efficiency **0.85**, and corrosion allowance **1.5 mm**. Calculate the **minimum wall thickness** and verify if a **Schedule 40 pipe (7.14 mm)** is acceptable. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Q A(a) — Prefabrication & Erection (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Drawing & MTO preparation (2 marks):**
- Pipe routing is finalized from P&ID and general arrangement drawings.
- A **Material Take-Off (MTO)** is generated listing each pipe, fitting, flange, and valve.
- Each spool is assigned a unique **spool number** (e.g., **SW-12-04** for sea-water line, frame 12, spool 4).

**Step 2 — Workshop prefabrication (2 marks):**
- Pipes are cut (oxy-fuel or plasma), beveled (30° V-groove for butt weld), and bent (cold mandrel or induction heating).
- Spools are welded in jigs to maintain tolerance, then **NDE-tested** (radiography or ultrasonic) at the shop.
- Each spool is fitted with temporary end-caps and **trial-assembled** to verify onboard fit.

**Step 3 — Onboard erection (2 marks):**
- Spools are lifted by chain blocks or cranes, positioned along the route, and tack-welded or clamped.
- Alignment is checked against **machinery foundations** before final welding.
- **Pipe hangers and supports** carry the pipe weight, absorb vibration, allow thermal expansion (sliding/saddle), and maintain slope for drainage.

**Step 4 — Supports selection (1 mark):**
- Small bore ambient → U-bolt clamp.
- Large bore → pipe shoe on I-beam saddle.
- High temperature → **spring hanger** (variable load) or **constant-load hanger**.
- At machinery → **flexible bellows**.

---

### Model Solution for Q A(b) — Wall Thickness (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 114.3$ mm = $0.1143$ m
- $P = 5$ bar = $500{,}000$ Pa
- $\sigma_{allow} = 75$ MPa = $75 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $c = 1.5$ mm = $0.0015$ m

**Step 1.** Apply the formula:

$$
t_{min} \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta} \;+\; c
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute:

$$
t_{min} \;=\; \dfrac{500{,}000 \cdot 0.1143}{2 \cdot 75 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.85} \;+\; 0.0015
$$

**Step 3.** Compute numerator:

$$
500{,}000 \cdot 0.1143 \;=\; 57{,}150
$$

**Step 4.** Compute denominator:

$$
2 \cdot 75 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.85 \;=\; 127{,}500{,}000
$$

**Step 5.** Division:

$$
\dfrac{57{,}150}{127{,}500{,}000} \;=\; 0.0004482\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 6.** Add corrosion allowance:

$$
t_{min} \;=\; 0.0004482 \;+\; 0.0015 \;=\; 0.001948\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 1.95\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 7.** Compare with Schedule 40 ($t = 7.14$ mm):

$$
7.14\;\text{mm} \;\gg\; 1.95\;\text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Schedule 40 is **acceptable** — in fact, the available thickness gives a very large safety margin (~3.7×), which is desirable because marine pipes must withstand **vibration, slug impact, and corrosion over 25-year service life**. (1 mark for concluding remark + safety margin discussion)

---

### 5.3 Alternative Question (Internal Choice B)

> **Q B.)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]`
> **(a)** Discuss the **types of pipe joints** used in shipboard piping with examples of their applications. Mention the standards for flanges. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A steel steam pipe of length **30 m** carries saturated steam at **200 °C**. The pipe was installed at an ambient temperature of **25 °C**. Calculate the **free thermal expansion** if the coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 0.012$ mm/(m·°C). Suggest a suitable method to absorb this expansion. *(7 marks)*

**Solution outline Q B(b):**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 200 - 25 \;=\; 175\;\text{°C}
$$

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 0.012 \cdot 30 \cdot 175
$$

$$
\Delta L \;=\; 63\;\text{mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance $c$** — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** (bar vs Pa, mm vs m) — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the conclusion or safety check** in numerical problems — costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing only formula, no derivation in Part B** — examiner awards only **1 of 7 marks** for raw equation in long answers.
> 5. **Confusing hoop stress with longitudinal stress** — remember factor of 2 difference.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Recall Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)**

- [x] Pipe installation = **planning → fabricate → erect → test → commission**.
- [x] Five pipe classes (I to V) — know at least the first three with materials.
- [x] **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.5 × design pressure** with **fresh water**.
- [x] Minimum bend radius (cold) = **5 × nominal diameter**.
- [x] Corrosion allowance (typical) = **1.5 mm** for sea-water systems.
- [x] Cu-Ni 90/10 used for sea-water; carbon steel ASTM A-106 for steam.
- [x] Thermal expansion formula: $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$, with $\alpha_{steel} \approx 0.012$ mm/(m·°C).
- [x] Hoop stress: $\sigma = PD/2t$ — used for wall thickness; longitudinal stress is half of hoop.
- [x] Recommended velocities: sea water **1.5 – 2.5 m/s**, steam **25 – 40 m/s**, lube oil **1 – 2 m/s**.
- [x] Welded joints for permanent/high-pressure; flanged at valves/pumps/machinery; threaded only for small bore low pressure.
- [x] Bulkhead penetration requires **welded boss + reinforcement pad**.
- [x] High-temp / hot lines need **expansion loops, bellows, or sliding supports**.
- [x] **Always NDT test welds** before final pressure test.
- [x] Slug impact, vibration, and hull deflection — extra thickness is normal; **never** design to theoretical minimum only.
- [x] Penalties in exam: wrong units > forgotten corrosion allowance > no conclusion remark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrical Installation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrical Installation in Ships** refers to the complete integrated network of power generation equipment, switchgear, distribution cabling, control panels, protective devices, lighting systems, navigation electronics, and emergency backup systems that collectively generate, distribute, regulate, and utilize electrical energy throughout a marine vessel, designed and installed in strict accordance with **Classification Society Rules** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) and **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) regulations. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Electrical Installation* is treated as a **sub-system of ship outfitting**, executed concurrently with hull fabrication and machinery installation, and finalized during the *Pre-Delivery Trials (PDT)* phase. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Ship is Like a Floating City** Think of a modern ship as a **miniature floating smart-city**. Just as a city needs power plants, transmission lines, substations, streetlights, hospitals, and emergency generators, a ship needs: - **Generators** → act like city power plants - **Main Switchboard** → like the city's grid control station - **Busbars & Cables** → like transmission lines and household wiring - **Emergency Generator** → like the backup generator at a hospital - **Navigation Electronics** → like the city's traffic control system The difference? On a ship, **everything is constantly vibrating**, exposed to **salt corrosion**, subject to **rolling and pitching motions**, and must operate in **compact, hazardous spaces** (engine rooms, pump rooms, paint stores). This is why marine electrical installation follows strict maritime standards rather than normal land-based electrical codes. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Marine Voltage Levels**: **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** (tankers, naval); **380 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** (merchant ships trading internationally) - **Lighting Voltage**: **220 V AC** (or **24 V DC** for emergency/battery-fed systems) - **Battery Voltage**: **24 V DC** (standard for emergency systems) - **Frequency Tolerance**: ±**5%** steady, ±**10%** transient (per IEC 60092) - **Insulation Resistance Minimum**: **1 MΩ per 1000 V** of rated voltage - **Ambient Design Temperature**: **45 °C** in engine rooms, **40 °C** in air-conditioned spaces ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know Topics for KTU University Exam (Module 2):** > 1. Ship's electrical power generation system architecture > 2. Main switchboard and emergency switchboard layout > 3. Types of cables used in marine applications (XLPE, EPR, PVC) > 4. Cable routing, penetration, and bunching rules > 5. Hazardous area classification (Zone 0, 1, 2) and Ex-proof equipment > 6. Earthing and bonding systems > 7. Emergency power and transitional source requirements > 8. Classification society rules — Lloyd's Register / DNV # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Ship's Electrical Power System The marine electrical installation follows a **hierarchical radial distribution architecture** with redundancy built in. The hierarchy is: ### Step 1 — Power Generation - **Main Generators (3 or 4 units)** driven by **auxiliary diesel engines** (typically 4-stroke, medium-speed) or **shaft generators** coupled to the main propulsion shaft. - Each generator is rated between **500 kW to 5000 kW** depending on ship size. - Generators run in **parallel** to share load, controlled by a **Power Management System (PMS)**. ### Step 2 — Main Switchboard (MSB) - The MSB is the **nerve center** of the ship's electrical system. - Houses **circuit breakers**, **busbars**, **protective relays**, **synchroscopes**, **AVRs (Automatic Voltage Regulators)**, **ammeters**, **voltmeters**, **power factor meters**, and **kw-hour meters**. - Located in the **engine control room (ECR)** for protection and accessibility. ### Step 3 — Distribution - **Main feeders** run from MSB to **sub-boards** (e.g., navigation deck sub-board, galley sub-board). - **Branch circuits** feed individual consumers. - **Bus-tie breakers** allow interconnection between sections or between MSB and Emergency Switchboard. ### Step 4 — Loads - **Propulsion auxiliaries** (pumps, compressors) - **Hotel loads** (lighting, HVAC, galley) - **Navigation & communication equipment** - **Cargo handling equipment** (cranes, winches) - **Safety & emergency systems** ## 2.2 Emergency Electrical System (SOLAS Mandated) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43**, every passenger and cargo ship must have: > - A **self-contained emergency source of power** (battery or emergency diesel generator) > - Capable of supplying **specified services for a minimum of 18 hours** for cargo ships of ≥500 GT > - Must start automatically upon **main power failure** and reach full load within **45 seconds** **Transitional Source of Power** (battery-fed) supplies critical services (e.g., navigation lights, communication, fire detection) **without delay** during the **45-second** start-up gap of the emergency generator. ## 2.3 Hazardous Area Classification Ship compartments containing flammable gases, vapors, or dust are classified under **IEC 60092-502** and **IEC 60079**: | Zone | Definition | Typical Ship Location | Equipment Allowed | |------|------------|----------------------|-------------------| | **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **continuously present** | — (rare in ships) | Ex 'ia' (intrinsically safe) | | **Zone 1** | Likely to occur in **normal operation** | Paint locker, battery locker | Ex 'd' (flameproof) or Ex 'e' (increased safety) | | **Zone 2** | Unlikely, only briefly | Open deck near vents | Ex 'n' (non-sparking) or ordinary enclosed | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **examination-essentials** for KTU Module 2 numericals. | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---------|-------------|-------|-------------| | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Three-phase full-load current | Amperes (A) | Cable sizing | | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Single-phase full-load current | Amperes (A) | Lighting circuits | | $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Voltage drop in 3-φ cable | Volts (V) | Voltage drop ≤ 6% (motor), ≤ 4% (lighting) | | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Conductor DC resistance | Ω | Cable resistance | | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{(R_s+R_c)^2 + (X_s+X_c)^2}}$ | Short-circuit current | kA | Protection discrimination | | $E = \dfrac{V}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi}$ | EMF equation (generator) | Volts | Generator sizing | | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out}+P_{losses}} \times 100$ | Generator efficiency | % | Power generation | | $P_{bat} = \dfrac{V_{bat} \cdot C_{ah}}{T}$ | Battery endurance | W | Emergency battery sizing | | $T = \dfrac{C_{ah}}{I_{disch}}$ | Discharge time | Hours | Battery backup | | $I_{trip} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_{inst}}$ | Discrimination check | — | Protection settings | > Symbols used: $I$ = current, $P$ = power, $V_L$ = line voltage, $\cos\phi$ = power factor, $L$ = cable length, $R$ = resistance, $X$ = reactance, $\rho$ = resistivity, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $f$ = frequency, $N$ = turns, $\Phi$ = flux. ## 2.5 Marine Cables — Types and Selection Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why marine cables differ from land cables?** They must resist **fire**, **oil**, **vibration**, **sea water**, **rodents**, and meet **low smoke zero halogen (LSZH)** or **low smoke fume emission** standards. **Why each step matters**: - **Conductor**: tinned copper (corrosion resistance) - **Insulation**: **XLPE** (cross-linked polyethylene) for power, **EPR** (ethylene propylene rubber) for flexibility - **Bedding**: special fillers for circular shape - **Inner Sheath**: PVC or LSZH - **Armour**: **Galvanized steel wire braid (GSWB)** — mandatory for exposed deck runs - **Outer Sheath**: **PCP** (polychloroprene) or **PVC** for oil/flame resistance ## 2.6 Cable Bunching and Derating Rule > [!WARNING] > When multiple cables are bunched in a single conduit or tray, each cable's **ampacity must be derated** using a **grouping factor** from IEC 60092-352 Table. The effective current-carrying capacity is: $$I_{effective} = I_{base} \times k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3$$ where: - $k_1$ = **grouping factor** (depends on number of bunched cables) - $k_2$ = **ambient temperature factor** (45 °C engine room requires ~0.87) - $k_3$ = **type of installation factor** (in duct vs. open tray) ## 2.7 Earthing and Bonding System A ship's earthing system uses **TN-S** or **IT (isolated)** configuration: - **Hull Return** is **PROHIBITED** except for very specific permitted systems (e.g., cathodic protection, limited DC zones). - **Earth-fault detection** is critical because the marine IT system can operate with a single earth fault without immediate disconnection — but alarms are triggered. - **Equipotential bonding** of all metallic enclosures prevents dangerous touch voltages. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility This entire electrical installation system ensures: 1. **Safety of crew and passengers** during normal and emergency operations. 2. **Continuity of propulsion and navigation** — vital for ocean-going ships that are 1000+ nautical miles from any port. 3. **Compliance** with class rules so the vessel can be insured, chartered, and operate internationally. 4. **Integration with modern automation** (Integrated Bridge Systems, Condition Monitoring). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Three-Phase Full-Load Current from Fundamental Power Equation **Problem Context**: Calculate the full-load current of a 3-phase motor drawing 150 kW at 440 V, 60 Hz, power factor 0.85 lagging. **Starting Principle**: The real power in a balanced three-phase system is given by: $$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$ where: - $P$ = real power in **watts** - $V_L$ = **line-to-line** RMS voltage - $I_L$ = line RMS current - $\cos\phi$ = power factor (lagging if inductive, leading if capacitive) **Step 1** — Rearrange for line current: $$I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$ **Step 2** — Substitute values (convert kW → W: $150 \text{ kW} = 150{,}000 \text{ W}$): $$I_L = \dfrac{150{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.85}$$ **Step 3** — Evaluate denominator: $$\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.85 = 1.732 \times 440 \times 0.85$$ $$1.732 \times 440 = 762.08$$ $$762.08 \times 0.85 = 647.77$$ **Step 4** — Final evaluation: $$I_L = \dfrac{150{,}000}{647.77} = 231.6 \text{ A}$$ **Result**: The motor draws approximately **231.6 A** at full load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams**: Cable selection for a motor starts here. A 240 A continuous current requires a **70 mm²** tinned copper XLPE cable (with derating). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Drop Across a Marine Power Cable **Problem Context**: A 3-phase motor at the bow thruster draws 200 A at 440 V, 0.85 pf lagging. The cable run is 80 m of 70 mm² copper XLPE cable. $R = 0.32 \text{ mΩ/m}$ and $X = 0.08 \text{ mΩ/m}$. Verify voltage drop. **Starting Equation** (voltage drop in 3-φ): $$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$ **Step 1** — Compute $\sin\phi$ from $\cos\phi = 0.85$: $$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} = 0.5268$$ **Step 2** — Compute $(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$: $$R\cos\phi = 0.32 \times 0.85 = 0.272 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ $$X\sin\phi = 0.08 \times 0.5268 = 0.04214 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ $$\text{Sum} = 0.272 + 0.04214 = 0.3141 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ **Step 3** — Multiply by length L = 80 m: $$0.3141 \times 80 = 25.13 \text{ mΩ}$$ **Step 4** — Apply $\sqrt{3} \cdot I$: $$\sqrt{3} \times 200 = 346.4 \text{ A}$$ **Step 5** — Final voltage drop: $$V_{drop} = 346.4 \times 25.13 \times 10^{-3} = 8.70 \text{ V}$$ **Step 6** — Percentage voltage drop: $$\% V_{drop} = \dfrac{8.70}{440} \times 100 = 1.98\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result**: 1.98% voltage drop is **well within** the 4% limit (IEC 60092) for motor circuits. The 70 mm² cable is acceptable. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Emergency Battery Sizing **Problem Context**: A ship's emergency battery (24 V DC) must supply navigation lights (300 W), GMDSS radio (200 W), and fire detection panel (100 W) for **18 hours** (per SOLAS). Calculate required battery capacity. **Step 1** — Total DC load: $$P_{total} = 300 + 200 + 100 = 600 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2** — Calculate current drawn at 24 V: $$I = \dfrac{P_{total}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{600}{24} = 25 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3** — Required capacity over 18 hours: $$C_{required} = I \times T = 25 \times 18 = 450 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 4** — Apply derating factor (batteries should not discharge below 80% of rated capacity, so derating factor = 1.25): $$C_{selected} = 450 \times 1.25 = 562.5 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 5** — Select next standard battery size (commercial batteries come in 100 Ah increments): $$C_{selected} = 600 \text{ Ah}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Battery type**: For marine emergency service, **vented lead-acid** or **Ni-Cd (Nickel-Cadmium)** batteries are preferred for their tolerance to overcharge and long service life. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Marine Generators | Parameter | Diesel Generator | Shaft Generator | Shaft Alternator (PTO) | |-----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------------| | **Prime Mover** | Auxiliary Diesel | Main Engine | Main Engine | | **Efficiency** | High (medium-speed) | Very High (uses waste) | Very High | | **Operating Speed** | 720 / 1800 rpm | Variable | Variable | | **Frequency Regulation** | Excellent (governor) | Requires PTO/PTI | Challenging | | **Use Case** | All ships | Cruise ships, tankers | Vessels with steady speed | | **Cost** | Moderate | High | High | ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Short-Circuit Current at Switchboard Busbar **Problem Context**: A 1500 kVA, 440 V, 3-phase generator has a sub-transient reactance $X_d'' = 0.12$ pu. Calculate the sub-transient short-circuit current at its terminals. **Step 1** — Base current: $$I_{base} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 440}$$ $$I_{base} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{762.08} = 1968.4 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2** — Sub-transient short-circuit current (per unit method): $$I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{base}}{X_d''} = \dfrac{1968.4}{0.12} = 16{,}403 \text{ A} \approx 16.4 \text{ kA}$$ **Step 3** — Note the **asymmetric peak** (DC offset) which is $\sqrt{2} \times 1.8 \times I_{sc}$ for circuit breaker selection: $$I_{peak} = 2.55 \times 16{,}403 = 41{,}827 \text{ A}$$ > [!WARNING] > The breaker must have a **breaking capacity** of at least **25 kA** (with adequate safety margin) and **making capacity** of at least **65 kA** for this generator. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ship's Electrical Power Distribution Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD GenA[Generator 1
Diesel Driven]:::gen GenB[Generator 2
Diesel Driven]:::gen GenC[Generator 3
Standby]:::gen SG[Shaft Generator
PTO from Main Engine]:::gen MSB[Main Switchboard
440V 3 Phase]:::msb ESB[Emergency Switchboard]:::esb EDG[Emergency Diesel Generator]:::gen BAT[24V DC Battery Bank]:::bat TS[Transitional Source
DC UPS]:::bat PNL1[Bow Thruster Motor]:::load PNL2[Navigation Deck Sub Board]:::load PNL3[Engine Room Sub Board]:::load PNL4[Cargo Crane Sub Board]:::load PNL5[Hotel Services Sub Board]:::load PNL6[Emergency Loads Panel]:::load NAV[Nav Lights GMDSS Radio]:::nav ALARM[Fire Detection Alarm Panel]:::nav GenA --> MSB GenB --> MSB GenC --> MSB SG --> MSB MSB --> PNL1 MSB --> PNL2 MSB --> PNL3 MSB --> PNL4 MSB --> PNL5 MSB --> EDG EDG --> ESB BAT --> TS TS --> ESB ESB --> PNL6 PNL6 --> NAV PNL6 --> ALARM classDef gen fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef msb fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef esb fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef bat fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nav fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Emergency Power Transition Logic Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Main Power OK]:::ok --> B[MSB Feeds Emergency Loads via Bus Tie]:::ok A --> C[EDG in Standby
Cooling Water Preheated]:::standby A --> D[Battery Float Charged]:::standby E[Main Power Fails]:::fail --> F[Bus Tie Breaker Opens]:::fail F --> G[EDG Auto Starts
Within 45 Seconds]:::fail F --> H[Transitional Source
Immediate Battery Feed]:::fail H --> I[Nav Lights Communication Fire Detection Active]:::fail G --> J[EDG Reaches Rated Speed and Voltage]:::fail J --> K[EDG Circuit Breaker Closes]:::fail K --> L[Emergency Loads Now Supplied by EDG]:::fail M[Main Power Returns]:::recover --> N[Manual or Auto Parallel Restoration]:::recover N --> O[EDG Returns to Standby]:::recover N --> P[Battery Recharges]:::recover classDef ok fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef standby fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef recover fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Cable Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start Cable Selection]:::start --> Q1{Load Type?}:::decision Q1 -->|Power Motor| MOTOR[Determine Full Load Current
Use 3 phase formula]:::process Q1 -->|Lighting| LIGHT[Determine Lighting Current
Use 1 phase formula]:::process Q1 -->|HVAC| HVAC[Determine Heating Load]:::process MOTOR --> DERATE[Apply Derating Factors
Grouping Temperature]:::process LIGHT --> DERATE HVAC --> DERATE DERATE --> VD[Check Voltage Drop
Less than 4 percent]:::check VD --> Q2{Voltage Drop OK?}:::decision Q2 -->|Yes| SEL[Select Standard Cable Size]:::final Q2 -->|No| INC[Increase Cable Cross Section]:::final INC --> VD SEL --> SC[Short Circuit Check
I squared t Capacity]:::check SC --> ARM{Exposed Run on Deck?}:::decision ARM -->|Yes| ARMR[Add Galvanized Steel Wire Braid Armour]:::process ARM -->|No| FIN[Final Cable Specification]:::final ARMR --> FIN classDef start fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef check fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Hazardous Zone Classification — Ship Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TANK[Tank Vessel Hazardous Zones] Z0[Zone 0
Inside Cargo Tank
Continuous Flammable Gas]:::zone0 Z1A[Zone 1
Cofferdam Pump Room
Gas Likely Normal]:::zone1 Z2A[Zone 2
Open Deck 3m from Vent]:::zone2 Z1B[Zone 1
Paint Locker]:::zone1 Z1C[Zone 1
Battery Compartment]:::zone1 end Z0 -.->|requires| EXIA[Ex ia Equipment
Intrinsically Safe]:::equip Z1A -.->|requires| EXD[Ex d Equipment
Flameproof Enclosure]:::equip Z2A -.->|requires| EXN[Ex n Equipment
Non Sparking]:::equip Z1B -.->|requires| EXD Z1C -.->|requires| EXD classDef zone0 fill:#ff1744,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef zone1 fill:#ff9100,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zone2 fill:#ffea00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef equip fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term "Transitional Source of Power" as applied to marine electrical installations. Why is it required?** **Model Answer**: > [!IMPORTANT] > A **transitional source of power** is a **battery-fed DC system** (or UPS) that automatically takes over the supply of critical safety services (navigation lights, GMDSS communication, fire detection, general alarm) the instant the main power fails, without any time delay. > > It is required because the **Emergency Diesel Generator (EDG)** takes up to **45 seconds** to start, accelerate, and reach rated voltage/frequency. SOLAS regulations forbid even a momentary loss of safety-critical services, hence the transitional source bridges this **45-second gap**. > > **Marks Distribution**: Definition (1), Purpose (1), SOLAS reference (1) = **3 marks** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2023] **List any six essential components of a ship's Main Switchboard (MSB).** **Model Answer**: > [!NOTE] > 1. **Main busbars** (copper, tinned, sectionalized) > 2. **Circuit breakers** (ACB - Air Circuit Breaker) for each generator and feeder > 3. **Synchronizing panel** with synchroscope and lamps > 4. **Protective relays** (overcurrent, earth fault, reverse power) > 5. **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** control > 6. **Measuring instruments** (ammeter, voltmeter, kW meter, power factor meter, frequency meter) > > **Marks Distribution**: 6 × 0.5 = **3 marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a ship's electrical power generation and distribution system**, including main generators, main switchboard, emergency switchboard, and bus-tie arrangements. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **SOLAS requirements for emergency power** on cargo ships. Calculate the **battery capacity** required to supply 750 W of emergency DC load for 18 hours at 24 V, including a 25% derating factor. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer for Question A #### (a) Architecture of Ship's Electrical Power System (7 marks) **Generation Layer**: Modern ships use **3 to 4 diesel-driven generators** (typically 500–5000 kW each) operating at **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz**. Generators run in parallel through the MSB. The system includes: - 2–3 **running generators** to share load (load sharing by governor droop) - 1 **standby generator** that auto-starts if a running unit fails - Optional **shaft generator** for fuel economy **Main Switchboard (MSB)**: Housed in the **Engine Control Room (ECR)** with IP-44 protection. Contains: - **Generator panels** with ACBs (Air Circuit Breakers) - **Bus-tie breaker** that can split the board into two halves (for fault isolation or maintenance) - **Synchronizing gear** - **Protective relays**: overcurrent (51), earth fault (51N), reverse power (32), undervoltage (27), overvoltage (59) **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)**: Normally supplied via bus-tie from MSB. On main power failure, **bus-tie opens** and **EDG feeds ESB** within 45 seconds. **Distribution**: From MSB, **main feeders** supply **section boards** (cargo deck, navigation deck, engine room). From section boards, **branch circuits** feed individual consumers. > [!NOTE] > **Why this layered architecture?** It provides **selectivity** (only the faulted part trips, not the whole ship), **redundancy** (multiple power paths), and **ease of maintenance**. **Marks**: Architecture sketch (2), Generation (2), MSB (2), Distribution (1) = **7 marks** #### (b) SOLAS Emergency Power Requirements and Battery Sizing (7 marks) **SOLAS Requirements (3 marks)**: > [!IMPORTANT] > Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43**: > 1. Every ship ≥ 500 GT must have an **independent emergency source of power**. > 2. For cargo ships, it must supply emergency loads for **minimum 18 hours**. > 3. The EDG must **start automatically** and reach full load within **45 seconds**. > 4. A **transitional source of power** (battery/UPS) must supply safety-critical loads without interruption during the 45-second gap. > 5. Emergency loads include: navigation lights, GMDSS, fire detection, emergency lighting, steering gear, watertight doors, public address. **Battery Sizing Calculation (4 marks)**: **Step 1**: Total emergency DC load: $$P_{total} = 750 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2**: Calculate current: $$I = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{750}{24} = 31.25 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3**: Required Ah for 18 hours: $$C_{raw} = 31.25 \times 18 = 562.5 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 4**: Apply derating factor of 1.25: $$C_{selected} = 562.5 \times 1.25 = 703.125 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 5**: Choose next standard size = **750 Ah battery bank**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Many students forget to apply the derating factor and lose 1 mark. Also, students must state the **standard battery size** selected (not just the calculated value). **Final Marks**: SOLAS (3) + Calculation (4) = **7 marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - June 2022] **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of hazardous zones on a tanker ship** as per IEC 60092-502. List suitable types of certified electrical equipment for each zone. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 440 V, 60 Hz motor draws 90 A at 0.85 pf lagging. The cable length from MSB to motor is 60 m, using XLPE 35 mm² cable with $R = 0.52 \text{ mΩ/m}$ and $X = 0.09 \text{ mΩ/m}$. Calculate the **percentage voltage drop** and verify if it complies with IEC 60092. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer for Question B #### (a) Hazardous Zone Classification on Tanker Ships (7 marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per **IEC 60092-502** and **IEC 60079**, hazardous zones on tankers are classified as follows: | Zone | Definition | Typical Location | Equipment Type | |------|------------|------------------|----------------| | **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **continuously or for long periods** | Inside cargo tanks, pipes | Ex 'ia' (Intrinsically Safe) | | **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere **likely during normal operation** | Pump rooms, cofferdams, paint lockers, battery rooms | Ex 'd' (Flameproof), Ex 'e' (Increased Safety) | | **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere **unlikely, briefly present** | Open deck within 3 m of tank vents, sounding pipes | Ex 'n' (Non-Sparking) | **Equipment Explanation (2 marks)**: - **Ex 'ia'**: Energy in any circuit limited to below ignition threshold — even with two faults. - **Ex 'd'**: Flameproof enclosure that contains any internal explosion and cools the gases before they exit. - **Ex 'e'**: Increased safety — no arcs, sparks, or hot surfaces during normal operation. - **Ex 'n'**: Non-sparking devices designed for zone 2 only. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters**: Using non-certified equipment in a hazardous zone can cause a **gas explosion** killing the entire crew. Classification Societies refuse to class such vessels. **Marks**: Zone 0 (1), Zone 1 (2), Zone 2 (2), Equipment types (2) = **7 marks** #### (b) Voltage Drop Calculation (7 marks) **Given**: $I = 90 \text{ A}$, $V = 440 \text{ V}$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $L = 60 \text{ m}$, $R = 0.52 \text{ mΩ/m}$, $X = 0.09 \text{ mΩ/m}$ **Step 1**: Compute $\sin\phi$: $$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{0.2775} = 0.5268$$ **Step 2**: Compute $(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$: $$R\cos\phi = 0.52 \times 0.85 = 0.442 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ $$X\sin\phi = 0.09 \times 0.5268 = 0.04741 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ $$\text{Sum} = 0.442 + 0.04741 = 0.4894 \text{ mΩ/m}$$ **Step 3**: Multiply by L = 60 m: $$0.4894 \times 60 = 29.36 \text{ mΩ}$$ **Step 4**: Apply $\sqrt{3} \cdot I$: $$\sqrt{3} \times 90 = 155.88 \text{ A}$$ **Step 5**: Final voltage drop: $$V_{drop} = 155.88 \times 29.36 \times 10^{-3} = 4.576 \text{ V}$$ **Step 6**: Percentage voltage drop: $$\% V_{drop} = \dfrac{4.576}{440} \times 100 = 1.04\%$$ **Verification**: > [!IMPORTANT] > Per **IEC 60092**, voltage drop for motor circuits must be ≤ **6%**. Our result of **1.04%** is **well within limits** — the cable is **acceptable**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students commonly make errors in: > 1. Forgetting the **$\sqrt{3}$ factor** for 3-phase (loses 2 marks) > 2. Not converting **mΩ to Ω** before multiplying (loses 1 mark) > 3. Not stating the **IEC limit explicitly** in the answer (loses 1 mark) **Marks**: Formula (2), Calculation steps (3), Percentage conversion (1), Verification statement (1) = **7 marks** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 - Electrical Installation** ### Architecture - Ships use **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** (or 380 V/50 Hz) AC systems - **3 to 4 generators** run in parallel; standby generator auto-starts on failure - **Main Switchboard (MSB)** in ECR; **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)** in separate compartment ### Emergency Power (SOLAS II-1 Reg 43) - **Transitional source** = immediate battery backup for safety services - **EDG** must reach full load within **45 seconds** - Minimum autonomy: **18 hours** for cargo ships ≥500 GT ### Hazardous Zones - **Zone 0**: Continuous gas (inside tanks) → Ex 'ia' - **Zone 1**: Likely gas (pump room) → Ex 'd' or Ex 'e' - **Zone 2**: Unlikely gas (open deck near vents) → Ex 'n' ### Cables - Marine cables: **tinned copper + XLPE/EPR insulation + LSZH sheath + GSWB armour** - Must apply **grouping, temperature, and installation derating factors** - Voltage drop limit: **4% (lighting)**, **6% (motor)** ### Earthing - **Hull return is PROHIBITED** (except specific permitted systems) - Marine systems use **IT (isolated) earthing** with earth-fault alarms - **Equipotential bonding** mandatory ### Key Formulas (Memorize!) - $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi}$ — 3-phase current - $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ — voltage drop - $C_{bat} = \dfrac{P \cdot T}{V} \times 1.25$ — battery capacity with derating - $I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{base}}{X_d''}$ — short-circuit current per unit ### Classification Society Rules - **Lloyd's Register (LR)**, **DNV**, **ABS**, **BV** — all prescribe minimum standards - Always cross-check with **IEC 60092 series** (Marine Electrical Installations) ### Common Student Mistakes to Avoid 1. ❌ Using land-based electrical codes instead of IEC 60092 2. ❌ Forgetting derating factors when cables are bunched 3. ❌ Not stating SOLAS chapter/regulation in emergency power answers 4. ❌ Confusing Zone 1 and Zone 2 requirements 5. ❌ Using ordinary switches instead of Ex-certified equipment in hazardous zones > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Examiner's Warning**: In 14-mark questions, students who fail to provide **standard references** (SOLAS Regulation number, IEC clause number) typically lose **1 to 2 marks** even when their calculations are correct. Always cite the regulation!

topics.title: Hull Installation Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hull Installation Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hull Installation Work** (also called **Hull Outfitting** or **Ship Outfitting**) is the comprehensive phase of ship construction that encompasses the installation, fitting, and integration of all machinery, equipment, piping, electrical systems, ventilation, insulation, joinery, deck fittings, and auxiliary components into the **erected hull structure** after the structural fabrication and block erection stages are complete.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST632), hull installation work is categorized into three principal classifications:

1. **Pre-outfitting (Pre-erection Outfitting):** All outfit work performed on hull blocks/sections **before** they are erected onto the slipway or in the building dock.
2. **Unit Outfitting:** Outfitting performed on pre-assembled **machinery units, pipe units, and ventilation units** in workshops before being lifted aboard.
3. **Post-outfitting (Post-erection Outfitting):** All outfit work performed **after** the hull blocks have been erected, joined, and the structural integrity is achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield):** Hull installation work transforms a bare welded steel structure (the hull) into a fully functional, habitable, and operational vessel ready for sea trials and delivery.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have just finished building the **bare skeleton (steel frame) of a house** — walls, roof, and floors are all in place, but there are no doors, windows, electrical wires, plumbing pipes, kitchen appliances, or furniture. The house is structurally complete but **not livable**.

Hull installation work is exactly this — the **"making-livable" stage** of ship construction:

| House Analogy | Ship Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Steel skeleton of house | Bare erected hull (steel plating + stiffeners) |
| Electrical wiring | Ship's electrical cabling & switchboards |
| Plumbing & water lines | Bilge, ballast, fire-fighting, fuel piping |
| Kitchen appliances | Galley equipment, refrigeration plants |
| HVAC ducts | Ship's ventilation & air-conditioning |
| Doors, windows, furniture | Joiner work, doors, hatches, deck coverings |
| Furniture & fixtures | Bridge equipment, accommodation fittings |
| Water heater, AC units | Main engine, auxiliaries, boilers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world shipyard parallel:** At Cochin Shipyard (CSL) or any modern Korean/European shipyard, approximately **60–70% of total outfitting work** is done **before block erection** (pre-outfitting) to reduce the costly "working at height" hours and improve productivity. This is a high-yield KTU exam point.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Standard outfitting accuracy tolerance:** $\pm$ **2 mm** for major alignment, $\pm$ **1 mm** for shaft alignment
- **Painting coverage standard:** **125–150 μm** DFT (Dry Film Thickness) for most marine applications
- **Insulation thickness (machinery spaces):** **50–100 mm** (mineral wool / rock wool)
- **Pipe support spacing standard:** Typically **3 m** maximum for steel pipes
- **Cable tray fill factor:** **Maximum 40%** by cross-sectional area
- **Outfitting productivity benchmark:** ~**10,000–15,000 man-hours** per ship for outfitting (varies by ship type)

> [!NOTE]
> The **shipbuilding outfitting-to-erection man-hour ratio** in modern yards is targeted at **60:40 or 70:30** (outfitting:erection), reflecting the shift toward pre-outfitting strategies.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Hull Installation Work

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) identifies the following sub-systems under hull installation:

1. **Main propulsion machinery installation** (Main engine, reduction gearbox, shafting, propellers)
2. **Auxiliary machinery installation** (Generators, compressors, pumps, purifiers, heat exchangers)
3. **Piping systems** (Bilge, ballast, fuel, lube oil, fire-fighting, sanitary, seawater, compressed air, steam)
4. **Electrical installation** (Generators, switchboards, motors, cabling, lighting, navigation electronics)
5. **HVAC & Ventilation** (Ducts, fans, AC plants, fire dampers)
6. **Insulation & lining** (Thermal insulation, acoustic insulation, refrigerated compartments)
7. **Joiner work & accommodation** (Cabins, galley, mess rooms, sanitary modules, furniture)
8. **Deck fittings & hull openings** (Hatches, doors, manholes, valves, sea chests, ports, windows)
9. **Coating & corrosion protection** (Painting, cathodic protection, anodes)
10. **Safety & lifesaving equipment** (Liferafts, fire-fighting outfits, EEBD, lifesaving appliances)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Approach to Hull Installation Work

The KTU 2024 curriculum emphasizes a **"sequence-aware" installation philosophy**. Theoretically, hull installation is governed by three engineering principles:

### Principle 1: The "Inverted Pyramid" of Dependencies

Outfitting must follow a strict dependency chain. You cannot install the main engine before the engine foundations are surveyed and aligned. You cannot lay electrical cables before the cable trays and penetrations are installed.

**Logical Hierarchy (Top-Down Installation Order):**

1. **Foundation Stage:** Engine bedplates, auxiliary foundations, mast foundations → surveyed and chocked with epoxy or steel chocks
2. **Heavy Machinery Stage:** Main engine, reduction gear, generators, boilers, main switchboard
3. **Piping Stage:** Large bore pipes (sea water, ballast) first, then small bore (instrumentation)
4. **HVAC Duct Stage:** Ducts and fans installed in machinery and accommodation spaces
5. **Electrical Stage:** Cable trays → cables → equipment → switchboards
6. **Insulation Stage:** Around hot pipes, engine exhausts, AC ducts, refrigerated compartments
7. **Joiner Stage:** Partitions, panelling, ceiling, doors, furniture
8. **Final Stage:** Deck covering, painting, safety equipment, commissioning

### Principle 2: The "Unit Pre-assembly" Strategy

Modern shipyards pre-assemble outfit **units** in workshop conditions (climate-controlled, ground-level access) and lift them aboard as complete modules. This reduces cost, improves quality, and shortens delivery time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "Unit Outfitting":**
> A typical modern ship uses **200–400 pre-assembled units** (pipe units, ventilation units, electrical units, galley units, sanitary units). Each unit is tested on the shop floor before installation — a concept borrowed from industrial assembly-line manufacturing.

### Principle 3: The "Working Environment" Optimization

Installation work is divided by **location and accessibility**:
- **Ground-level work** (pre-outfitting on blocks) → efficient, fast
- **Overhead work** (in double-bottom tanks, high decks) → costly, slower
- **Confined-space work** (inside tanks, cofferdams) → hazardous, requires permits

The strategic goal is to **minimize the percentage of "high and confined" work hours**.

---

## 2.2 Installation Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

The **"Why"** behind each step:

| Step | Operation | "Why" — Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hull blocks erected and welded | Structural integrity must exist before any equipment load is added |
| 2 | Major foundations surveyed & chocked | Distribute machinery loads to hull girder; tolerances must be ±1 mm |
| 3 | Heavy machinery lifted in via shipyard crane | Largest components installed first while access is open |
| 4 | Shafting, propellers, rudder fitted | Done in dry dock before launching for accurate alignment |
| 5 | Pipe units, ventilation units, electrical units lifted in | Modulated work installed in proximity to final location |
| 6 | Final pipe connections, cable terminations | After all major equipment is in place and aligned |
| 7 | Insulation and lagging applied | After hot systems are pressure-tested and certified |
| 8 | Joiner work, panelling, deck covering | Aesthetic and habitable finishing; near-end of build |
| 9 | Painting, marking, safety equipment | Final stage before sea trials |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly applicable** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numerical problems in KTU ESE examinations for Module 2.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application in Hull Installation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sling Tension** (Two-leg sling at angle θ) | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos \theta}$ | Crane lifting of machinery units; θ = sling angle from vertical |
| 2 | **Sling Angle Safety Check** | $\theta \leq 60°$ (typical yard limit) | Beyond 60°, sling tension rises sharply and crushing risk increases |
| 3 | **Number of Slings Required** | $n = \dfrac{W \cdot SF}{T_{single}}$ | SF = Safety Factor (typically 1.5–2.0) |
| 4 | **Centre of Gravity (Composite Unit)** | $X_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Used for machinery unit lifting and ship stability check |
| 5 | **Chock Grouting Volume** | $V = L \cdot B \cdot t$ | Epoxy/steel chock volume between bedplate and foundation |
| 6 | **Allowable Bearing Pressure on Chock** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_{engine}}{A_{contact}}$ | Must be < allowable chock strength (~25 N/mm² for epoxy) |
| 7 | **Pipe Support Spacing Rule** | $L_{max} = k \cdot \sqrt{D}$ (for steel pipes) | k ≈ 12 for carbon steel; D = pipe diameter (mm) |
| 8 | **Cable Tray Fill Factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum A_{cables}}{A_{tray}} \leq 0.4$ | 40% maximum to allow heat dissipation and future cabling |
| 9 | **Insulation Heat Loss (Flat Surface)** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $d$ = insulation thickness |
| 10 | **Painting DFT vs WFT** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{\% \text{ solids by volume}}$ | Wet film thickness required to achieve specified dry film |
| 11 | **Man-Hour Estimation (Outfitting)** | $MH = m \cdot LOA^2 \cdot B$ | Empirical rule for outfitting man-hours (m = ship-type factor) |
| 12 | **Crane Capacity Selection** | $C_{crane} \geq W_{load} \cdot SF_{dynamic}$ | $SF_{dynamic} = 1.25$ for shipyard gantry cranes |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU examination, **Formula #1, #2, #4, and #8** are the most frequently asked. Memorize the sling-tension geometry and the tray fill factor for guaranteed marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Concepts

| Concept | Where It's Used in Real Shipyards |
|---|---|
| Sling Tension Calculation | Every heavy lift at Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo |
| Centre of Gravity | Lifting plans, voyage stability, inclining experiment |
| Chock Bearing Pressure | Main engine alignment (lathe-aligned shafting system) |
| Cable Tray Fill Factor | Class-approved electrical drawings (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register) |
| Pipe Support Spacing | Pipe stress analysis, class-approved piping isometrics |
| Outfitting Man-Hour Estimation | Tender pricing, shipyard production planning, ERP systems |

---

## 2.5 Pre-Outfitting vs. Post-Outfitting — KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Pre-Outfitting | Post-Outfitting |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | At block fabrication stage (workshop) | After block erection (at quay/basin) |
| **Working Height** | Ground level (0–2 m) | High in air (10–30 m) |
| **Productivity** | **High** (1.5–2× faster) | Low (costly overhead work) |
| **Quality** | Better (workshop conditions) | Variable (weather-dependent) |
| **Safety** | High | Lower (fall risks, fatigue) |
| **Cost per man-hour** | Lower | Higher (~30–50% premium) |
| **Equipment Used** | Workshop cranes, jigs | Cherry pickers, scaffolding |
| **% in Modern Yards** | **60–70%** | 30–40% |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension for a Two-Leg Crane Lift (Most Asked KTU Numerical)

### Problem Statement
A main engine weighing **W = 80 tonnes (800 kN)** is to be lifted by a two-leg sling. The sling makes an angle **θ = 45°** with the vertical. Calculate:
1. Tension in each sling leg
2. Whether the angle is within safe yard limits
3. Vertical force component at the hook
4. Minimum sling diameter if allowable stress is **120 N/mm²**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup**
The load W is suspended by two identical slings. Each sling makes angle θ with the vertical. By symmetry, the tension T is equal in both legs.

**Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium Equation**
Summing vertical forces at the load attachment point:

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

The vertical component of each sling is $T \cos \theta$. Two slings support the weight:

$$
2 \cdot T \cos \theta - W = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Tension T**

$$
2 \cdot T \cos \theta = W
$$

$$
T = \frac{W}{2 \cos \theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Given: $W = 800 \text{ kN}$, $\theta = 45°$

$$
\cos(45°) = 0.7071
$$

$$
T = \frac{800}{2 \times 0.7071}
$$

$$
T = \frac{800}{1.4142}
$$

$$
T = 565.7 \text{ kN (per sling leg)}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety Check on Sling Angle**
The yard rule: $\theta \leq 60°$ for safe lifting.

Since $45° < 60°$, the lift is **SAFE** (✓).

If $\theta = 70°$ (beyond limit), then:

$$
T = \frac{800}{2 \cos(70°)} = \frac{800}{2 \times 0.3420} = 1169.6 \text{ kN}
$$

→ Tension **doubles** when angle increases from 45° to 70°. This demonstrates the **critical importance of keeping slings as vertical as possible**.

**Step 6 — Vertical Force at Hook**

$$
F_{vertical} = 2 \cdot T \cos \theta = W = 800 \text{ kN (verified, by equilibrium)}
$$

**Step 7 — Minimum Sling Diameter**
Allowable tensile stress: $\sigma_{allow} = 120 \text{ N/mm}^2$
Required cross-sectional area per leg:

$$
A_{required} = \frac{T}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{565{,}700 \text{ N}}{120 \text{ N/mm}^2} = 4714.2 \text{ mm}^2
$$

For a circular cross-section:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{required}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 4714.2}{3.1416}}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{6001.6} = 77.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply Safety Factor**
Adopt a sling factor of safety of **5.0** (standard for wire rope slings per ISO 7531):

$$
d_{selected} = 77.5 \times \sqrt{5.0} = 77.5 \times 2.236 = 173.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Rounded up: $d_{selected} = 180$ mm** (standard wire rope sling size).

### Final Answer Box
- Tension per leg: **T = 565.7 kN**
- Sling angle: **SAFE (45° < 60°)**
- Minimum diameter (with SF = 5): **d = 180 mm**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Centre of Gravity of an Outfit Unit

### Problem Statement
A pre-assembled machinery unit has three components:
- Pump A: $W_A = 2.0$ t, located at $x_A = 1.0$ m
- Motor B: $W_B = 0.8$ t, located at $x_B = 2.5$ m
- Base frame C: $W_C = 1.2$ t, located at $x_C = 1.8$ m

Find the centre of gravity (longitudinal position).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the Composite CG Formula**

$$
X_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Numerator (Σ Wᵢ · xᵢ)**

$$
\sum W_i \cdot x_i = (2.0)(1.0) + (0.8)(2.5) + (1.2)(1.8)
$$

$$
= 2.0 + 2.0 + 2.16 = 6.16 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Denominator (Σ Wᵢ)**

$$
\sum W_i = 2.0 + 0.8 + 1.2 = 4.0 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate X_cg**

$$
X_{cg} = \frac{6.16}{4.0} = 1.54 \text{ m}
$$

### Final Answer
**$X_{cg} = 1.54$ m** from the reference origin. This is the lifting point for balanced hoisting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pipe Support Spacing Calculation

### Problem Statement
A steel pipe of **nominal diameter DN = 100 mm** is to be supported. Using the rule $L_{max} = k\sqrt{D}$, with $k = 12$ for carbon steel, calculate the maximum allowable support spacing.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Substitute D and k**

$$
L_{max} = 12 \times \sqrt{100}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Square Root**

$$
\sqrt{100} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Final Result**

$$
L_{max} = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ cm} = 1.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**
A DN100 steel pipe carrying water (full of liquid) must be supported **every 1.2 m maximum**. This prevents sagging, vibration-induced fatigue, and water hammer damage.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Installation Productivity Comparison

| Installation Strategy | Productivity Index | Cost Index | Quality Index | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **100% Pre-outfitting on blocks** | 100 (baseline) | 100 | 100 | High |
| **Hybrid (60% pre + 40% post)** | 85 | 115 | 95 | Very High |
| **100% Post-outfitting (old method)** | 55 | 160 | 75 | High |
| **Unit pre-assembly (modular)** | 130 | 90 | 110 | High |
| **3D-printed pipe spools + unit outfitting** | 150 | 80 | 115 | Emerging |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Standard Numerical Problem Bank (Worked Solutions)

### Problem A: Bearing Pressure on Engine Chock

A main engine weighs **120 tonnes (1177 kN)**. The total contact area between the engine bedplate and the epoxy chocks is **3.2 m²**. Calculate the bearing pressure on the chocks and verify if it is safe for a chock material of allowable strength **25 N/mm²**.

**Solution:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{1{,}177{,}000 \text{ N}}{3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = 0.368 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Since $0.368 \ll 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$, the chock is **vastly under-stressed** — very safe (✓).

### Problem B: Cable Tray Fill Factor Check

A cable tray has a cross-section of **300 mm × 100 mm**. Six cables, each of **25 mm diameter**, are to be routed. Check if the fill factor is within the 40% rule.

**Solution:**

Area of tray:

$$
A_{tray} = 300 \times 100 = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Area of one cable (circular):

$$
A_{cable} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (25)^2}{4} = 490.9 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Total area of six cables:

$$
\sum A_{cables} = 6 \times 490.9 = 2945.2 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Fill factor:

$$
\eta = \frac{2945.2}{30{,}000} = 0.098 = 9.8\%
$$

Since $9.8\% \ll 40\%$, the layout is **approved** with significant spare capacity (✓).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part A questions in KTU ESE carry 3 marks each. Answers should be **precise (5–8 lines)** with diagrams where applicable. Memorize definitions and one-line classifications.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define hull installation work. List any four major activities performed under it.**

**Model Answer:**
Hull installation work is the phase of ship construction in which all machinery, piping, electrical, ventilation, insulation, joinery, and auxiliary systems are installed and integrated into the erected hull to make the vessel fully operational and habitable.

**Four major activities:**
1. Main engine and auxiliary machinery installation.
2. Piping system fabrication, erection, and testing.
3. Electrical cabling, switchboard, and lighting installation.
4. Insulation, joiner work, and accommodation outfitting.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between pre-outfitting and post-outfitting. Give two advantages of pre-outfitting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pre-Outfitting | Post-Outfitting |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | On block, before erection | After block erection |
| **Working height** | Ground level | Elevated / over-head |
| **Productivity** | High | Low |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

**Two advantages of pre-outfitting:**
1. **Higher productivity** — work done at ground level is 1.5–2× faster.
2. **Improved quality** — workshop-controlled environment (lighting, no weather dependency) and easier access for QA inspection.
