# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of DFP in Shipbuilding

### Pillar 1 — Standardization

**Definition:** Use of components, materials, sections, and processes that conform to recognized standards (IACS, ISO, BIS, AWS).

**Why it matters in shipbuilding:**
- Reduces drawing office workload
- Allows bulk procurement (economies of scale)
- Reduces inventory of spares
- Facilitates yard-wide repeatability

**How it is applied:**
- Rolled sections (angles, T-bars, bulb plates, flat bars) — adopt standard mill sizes
- Standard plate thicknesses: 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 25 mm
- Standard pipe sizes (DN 50, 65, 80, 100, 150, 200, etc.)
- Standard valve, pump, and machinery makes/models
- Standard weld preparations (e.g., single-V 60° included angle for butt welds up to 16 mm)

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb used in Indian shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, MDL, GRSE):** "If a non-standard size is needed, the designer must justify it in writing on the drawing — otherwise the procurement department reverts to the nearest standard."

---

### Pillar 2 — Simplification

Simplification means reducing the **variety** of components and the **complexity** of operations. It draws from *value engineering* principles.

**Application areas:**
- Reducing the number of different plate thicknesses on a hull (e.g., from 14 grades down to 5–6).
- Eliminating compound curves where flat development is possible (within scantling tolerance).
- Using single-V butt welds instead of double-U for medium-thickness plates.
- Symmetrical hull form to share moulds and fixtures port-starboard.

**Hindley's simplification principle (synthesized):**

$$N_{savings} = N_{original} - N_{simplified}$$

where $N$ is the count of distinct part numbers. The **Simplification Index (SI)** used in some yards is:

$$SI = \frac{\text{Number of unique parts}}{\text{Total parts in assembly}} \times 100\%$$

A lower SI indicates higher simplification. **KTU expects:** ship designers to target $SI < 8\%$ for primary hull structure.

---

### Pillar 3 — Modularization and Block Construction

A ship is divided into **units → blocks → grand blocks → sections → the whole ship**. This is the single most important DFP strategy in modern yards.

**Hierarchy:**

$$\text{Hull} \to \text{Grand Block} \to \text{Block} \to \text{Sub-block} \to \text{Panel/Piece}$$

**Block weight limits (typical):**
- Yard crane capacity: **100 T to 1200 T** (goliath cranes in mega-yards).
- Block weight target: **≤ 60% of crane capacity** (for safety and rigging).

**Block size considerations:**

$$L_{block}^{max} = \text{Lofted length that fits through workshops and onto slipway}$$
$$W_{block}^{max} = \text{Limited by workshop bay width (typically 30 – 50 m)}$$
$$H_{block}^{max} = \text{Limited by gantry clearance (typically 8 – 12 m)}$$

**Why modularize?**
- Parallel work in different shops (steel, outfit, paint)
- Reduces critical path duration
- Improves welding quality (down-hand welding in panels)
- Easier inspection and NDT

---

### Pillar 4 — Design for Welding (DFW)

Welding represents **30–40% of total labor hours** in a steel ship. DFW aims to make welding **easy, accessible, and defect-free**.

**Key DFW rules:**
1. **Weld accessibility:** All groove welds must be reachable by a welder with a 300 mm minimum throat clearance (per IACS UR W).
2. **Welding position preference:** Flat (1G) > Horizontal (2G) > Vertical (3G) > Overhead (4G). **Cost ratio is approximately 1 : 2 : 3 : 5.**
3. **Joint design minimization:** Use single-V up to 16 mm plate thickness.
4. **Backing and backing bars:** Use ceramic or copper backing for one-sided welds.
5. **Weld volume minimization:**

$$V_{weld} = \frac{t^2}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot L \quad \text{(for single-V groove)}$$

where $t$ = plate thickness, $\theta$ = included angle (typically 60°), $L$ = weld length.

6. **Avoid intersecting welds:** Cross welds create stress concentration zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld cost weighting (KTU Board favorite):**
> Cost per metre of weld ≈ $k \cdot V_{weld} \cdot p$
> where $k$ = deposition efficiency factor (typically 0.6–0.8 for GMAW), and $p$ = cost per unit weld metal volume.

---

### Pillar 5 — Design for Assembly, Inspection, and Maintenance (DFA/DFI/DFMt)

**Design for Assembly (DFA):**
- Minimize the number of parts (Boothroyd's principle).
- Use self-locating features (dowels, tapers, alignment marks).
- Provide generous tolerances at mating interfaces.

**Design for Inspection (DFI):**
- Welds must be visible for NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI).
- Brackets, stiffeners, and equipment should not block access.
- Internal compartments must have adequate manhole access (**minimum 600 × 600 mm** as per SOLAS Ch. II-1).

**Design for Maintenance (DFMt):**
- 1 m clearance around major machinery (class rule of thumb).
- Piping routed with removable sections.
- Coatings specified for ease of re-blasting and re-painting.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept / Quantity** | **Formula / Relation** | **Units** | **Remarks / Typical Values** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--:|:--|
| 1 | Block weight estimation | $W_{block} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel}$ | T (tonnes) | $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V$ = block volume |
| 2 | Plate flatness tolerance | $\Delta_f = \pm\,2 \text{ mm/m}$ | mm/m | Class rule for primary hull plating |
| 3 | Hull module length | $L_{mod} = L_{wl} / n_{blocks}$ | m | Typically $n_{blocks} = 20$ to 80 depending on vessel size |
| 4 | Weld volume (single-V) | $V = (t^2 / 2) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot L$ | mm³ | $\theta = 60°$ preferred; $t$ in mm, $L$ in mm |
| 5 | Weld cost ratio by position | $1G : 2G : 3G : 4G \approx 1 : 2 : 3 : 5$ | ratio | Used for production planning |
| 6 | Simplification Index | $SI = (N_{unique} / N_{total}) \times 100\%$ | % | Target $SI < 8\%$ for primary hull |
| 7 | Block centre of gravity | $z_{cg} = \Sigma(w_i z_i) / \Sigma w_i$ | m | Needed for crane lift planning |
| 8 | Tolerance stack-up (n parts) | $T_{stack} = \sqrt{\Sigma t_i^2}$ (statistical) | mm | More realistic than arithmetic sum |
| 9 | Modular man-hours saved | $MH_{saved} = MH_{conv} - MH_{mod}$ | hours | Quantified using work breakdown structure |
| 10 | Crane utilization factor | $\eta = W_{block} / W_{crane}$ | ratio | Design limit: $\eta \leq 0.6$ to 0.7 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Block Weight and Crane Suitability Analysis

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> A flat-bottomed rectangular ship block has the following dimensions: length $L = 24$ m, breadth $B = 16$ m, depth $D = 6$ m. The block contains $180 \text{ m}^3$ of fabricated steel volume. The yard has a goliath crane of capacity **600 T**.
> *(a) Compute the weight of the block and check whether it is within the design lift limit (assume 60% utilization).*
> *(b) If over-limit, propose a DFP solution and re-evaluate.*

### Step 1: Identify known quantities

We are given:
- $L = 24$ m, $B = 16$ m, $D = 6$ m
- $V_{steel} = 180 \text{ m}^3$ (this is the actual steel volume inside the block, not the bounding box volume)
- $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 7.85 \text{ T/m}^3$
- $W_{crane} = 600$ T
- $\eta_{design} = 0.60$

### Step 2: Compute mass of the block

$$m = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel}$$

Substituting:

$$m = 7.85 \, \frac{\text{T}}{\text{m}^3} \times 180 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$m = 1413 \, \text{T}$$

### Step 3: Compute design lift capacity of the crane

$$W_{lift,design} = \eta_{design} \times W_{crane}$$

$$W_{lift,design} = 0.60 \times 600 = 360 \, \text{T}$$

### Step 4: Compare and conclude

$$m = 1413 \, \text{T} \quad \gg \quad W_{lift,design} = 360 \, \text{T}$$

**Conclusion:** The block is **grossly over the lift limit** — approximately **3.9 times** the safe capacity. A DFP redesign is mandatory.

### Step 5: Apply DFP solution — sub-divide the block

**Solution:** Sub-divide the $24 \text{ m} \times 16 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m}$ grand block into smaller blocks.

Let us split along the length into **n** sub-blocks of equal steel volume (assuming uniform distribution):

$$V_{sub} = \frac{V_{steel}}{n}$$

For safety, target $W_{sub} = 360$ T (the design lift limit). Thus:

$$m_{sub} = 360 \text{ T} = 7.85 \times V_{sub}$$

$$V_{sub} = \frac{360}{7.85} = 45.86 \text{ m}^3$$

Therefore, the required number of sub-blocks:

$$n = \frac{V_{steel}}{V_{sub}} = \frac{180}{45.86} = 3.92 \implies n = 4 \text{ sub-blocks (rounded up)}$$

### Step 6: Check final sub-block weight

$$W_{sub,actual} = \frac{1413}{4} = 353.25 \, \text{T} \le 360 \, \text{T} \quad \checkmark$$

**DFP Result:** Split the grand block into **4 sub-blocks**, each weighing approximately **353 T**, all within the 60% crane utilization limit. The new sub-block length becomes:

$$L_{sub} = \frac{L}{n} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \, \text{m}$$

This is a clean modular dimension that also aligns with the **standard sectional steel stock length of 6 m** — a beautiful DFP synergy!

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Weld Volume and Cost Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
> A ship hull has a 14 mm thick plate, butt-welded with a single-V groove of included angle $\theta = 60°$. The total weld length is $L = 80$ m.
> *(a) Compute the theoretical weld metal volume.*
> *(b) If the deposition efficiency is 0.70 and the cost of weld metal + gas is ₹450 per kg, compute the cost. Take steel density 7850 kg/m³.*

### Step 1: Theoretical weld cross-sectional area

For a single-V groove of depth $t$ and included angle $\theta$, the cross-sectional area is:

$$A = \frac{t^2}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Substituting $t = 14$ mm, $\theta = 60°$:

$$\tan\left(\frac{60°}{2}\right) = \tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$$

$$A = \frac{14^2}{2} \times 0.5774 = \frac{196}{2} \times 0.5774$$

$$A = 98 \times 0.5774 = 56.58 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2: Theoretical weld volume

$$V_{weld} = A \times L = 56.58 \, \text{mm}^2 \times 80{,}000 \, \text{mm}$$

$$V_{weld} = 4{,}526{,}400 \text{ mm}^3 = 4.5264 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3$$

Convert to $\text{m}^3$:

$$V_{weld} = 4.5264 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-9} = 4.5264 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$$

### Step 3: Mass of weld metal required (accounting for efficiency)

The actual mass needed is the theoretical mass divided by deposition efficiency:

$$m_{weld,actual} = \frac{\rho_{steel} \cdot V_{weld}}{\eta_{dep}}$$

$$m_{weld,actual} = \frac{7850 \times 4.5264 \times 10^{-3}}{0.70}$$

$$m_{weld,actual} = \frac{35.53}{0.70} = 50.76 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4: Total cost

$$C = m_{weld,actual} \times p = 50.76 \times 450$$

$$C = ₹22{,}842$$

### Step 5: DFP insight

A DFP-minded designer would consider:
- Using **double-V (X-groove)** for $t = 14$ mm to reduce weld volume by ~50%, since the original code allows it for plate thickness > 12 mm.
- Calculating new volume:

$$A_{X-groove} = 2 \times \frac{(t/2)^2}{2} \cdot \tan(30°) = 2 \times \frac{7^2}{2} \times 0.5774 = 28.29 \text{ mm}^2$$

Savings in cost: **~50%**, i.e., saving **~₹11,400** on this single joint — multiplied across thousands of joints, this is a massive DFP win.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Tolerance Stack-Up in Block Assembly

**Problem Statement:**
> A 30 m long hull section is assembled from 6 sequential blocks, each with a length tolerance of $\pm 4$ mm. Compute (a) arithmetic stack-up, (b) statistical (RSS) stack-up, and (c) determine if class tolerance of $\pm 20$ mm on overall length is met.

### Step 1: Arithmetic (worst-case) stack-up

$$T_{arith} = \Sigma \vert t_i \vert = 6 \times 4 = \pm 24 \text{ mm}$$

This **exceeds** the class tolerance of $\pm 20$ mm.

### Step 2: Statistical (Root-Sum-Square) stack-up

$$T_{RSS} = \sqrt{\Sigma t_i^2} = \sqrt{6 \times 4^2} = \sqrt{6 \times 16} = \sqrt{96}$$

$$T_{RSS} = 9.80 \text{ mm}$$

This is well within class tolerance.

### Step 3: Conclusion

The **statistical stack-up of ±9.80 mm** is the realistic figure. The arithmetic figure of ±24 mm is an over-design. DFP recommends using **statistical tolerance analysis** to optimize machining and assembly accuracy without over-constraining the production.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** For KTU examinations, both arithmetic and statistical stack-ups may be asked. Always state assumptions (e.g., "assuming independent and normally distributed tolerances") for the statistical method.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define Design for Production (DFP) in shipbuilding. List any four key principles.**

**Model Answer:**
Design for Production (DFP) is a ship design philosophy that integrates manufacturing, fabrication, assembly, inspection, and maintenance considerations into the design phase itself, to ensure the vessel can be built economically, safely, and to the required quality within the project schedule.

The four key principles are:
1. **Standardization** of materials, sections, and components
2. **Simplification** of forms, joints, and processes
3. **Modularization** into buildable units and blocks
4. **Design for Welding (DFW)** ensuring accessible, efficient joints

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Explain the "Weld Position Cost Ratio" of 1 : 2 : 3 : 5 used in ship production planning.**

**Model Answer:**
The cost ratio represents the relative expense of depositing weld metal in the four standard positions:
- **1G (flat) → 1** — easiest, highest deposition rate, lowest rework
- **2G (horizontal) → 2** — slightly more difficult
- **3G (vertical) → 3** — gravity-influenced, requires weave technique
- **4G (overhead) → 5** — most difficult, lowest deposition, highest defect risk

It is used in DFP to:
- Drive designers to orient joints for 1G/2G welds
- Quantify cost savings from position improvement
- Optimize block orientation during assembly planning

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Module 3 Internal Choice Option

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the **hierarchy of modular construction** in modern shipbuilding from "plate" to "complete ship." Discuss the role of each level in DFP.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A shipyard has a **goliath crane of 800 T capacity**. A grand block has been designed with the following data: length 30 m, breadth 18 m, depth 7 m, fabricated steel volume 250 m³. Density of steel = 7850 kg/m³. **Apply the 60% utilization rule** to check the block's suitability for a single lift, and if unsuitable, propose a DFP-based solution with calculations.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The hierarchy of modular construction in shipbuilding is:

1. **Plate / Section (Piece Level):** Raw steel plates and rolled sections are the smallest units. They are cut, shaped, and edge-prepared.
2. **Panel / Sub-block (Smallest Assembly):** A flat plate with attached stiffeners — built on a panel line.
3. **Block (3D Unit):** A three-dimensional assembly of panels, typically 6 m × 6 m × 6 m, with outfit items partially fitted.
4. **Grand Block (Mega Unit):** A logical grouping of blocks, weighing 200–1200 T, lifted by heavy cranes.
5. **Section (Erection Unit):** A complete cross-section of the ship (e.g., engine room section).
6. **Ship (Complete Assembly):** The fully erected and outfitted vessel.

**Role in DFP:**
- Allows **parallel work** on different units
- Maximizes **down-hand welding** (1G/2G)
- Permits **NDT and blasting/painting** before final erection
- Reduces critical path and improves quality control

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Compute block mass.

$$m = 7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 250 \, \text{m}^3 = 1{,}962{,}500 \, \text{kg} = 1962.5 \, \text{T}$$

**Step 2:** Compute design lift limit.

$$W_{lift,design} = 0.60 \times 800 = 480 \, \text{T}$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

$$1962.5 \, \text{T} \gg 480 \, \text{T} \quad \Rightarrow \text{NOT suitable for single lift}$$

**Step 4:** Sub-divide.

For $W_{sub} = 480$ T:

$$V_{sub} = \frac{480}{7.85} = 61.15 \text{ m}^3$$

$$n = \frac{250}{61.15} = 4.09 \implies n = 5 \text{ sub-blocks}$$

**Step 5:** Verify.

$$W_{sub,actual} = \frac{1962.5}{5} = 392.5 \, \text{T} \le 480 \, \text{T} \quad \checkmark$$

**DFP Recommendation:** Split the 30 m long grand block into **5 sub-blocks of 6 m length each**. The 6 m dimension also matches standard steel mill stock length, enabling efficient plate nesting. Each sub-block weighs 392.5 T, well within the 60% crane utilization limit.

---

### Question B — Module 3 Internal Choice Option (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principles of **Design for Welding (DFW)** in shipbuilding. Discuss the weld position cost ratio and its impact on design decisions.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A ship hull contains a butt weld in 16 mm thick plate, total weld length 120 m, single-V 60° groove, deposition efficiency 0.75, cost of weld metal ₹500 per kg. Compute the **theoretical weld volume, actual mass of weld metal consumed, and total cost**. Suggest one DFP measure to reduce cost.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Design for Welding (DFW)** principles:

1. **Joint Accessibility:** Minimum 300 mm clearance for welder and electrode.
2. **Position Preference:** Always design for 1G/2G welding.
3. **Joint Type Selection:** Single-V for 6–12 mm; double-V (X-groove) for 12–25 mm.
4. **Avoid Tri-axial Welds:** No three welds meeting at a point.
5. **Backing Strategy:** Use ceramic backing for one-sided welding.
6. **Material Weldability:** Use ABS-A, B, D, E grade steels with CE ≤ 0.45.

**Weld Position Cost Ratio (1G:2G:3G:4G = 1:2:3:5):**
- Encourages designers to orient blocks for down-hand welding
- Rotating blocks during assembly (e.g., turning a sub-block to expose 1G welds) is a classic DFP technique
- Drives the use of jigs and positioners

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of single-V groove.

$$A = \frac{16^2}{2} \times \tan(30°) = 128 \times 0.5774 = 73.91 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical weld volume.

$$V = 73.91 \times (120 \times 1000) = 8.869 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3 = 8.869 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Mass of weld metal.

$$m_{theoretical} = 7850 \times 8.869 \times 10^{-3} = 69.62 \, \text{kg}$$

$$m_{actual} = \frac{69.62}{0.75} = 92.83 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4:** Total cost.

$$C = 92.83 \times 500 = ₹46{,}415$$

**Step 5:** DFP measure — **Use double-V (X-groove) for 16 mm plate.**

New cross-section:

$$A_X = 2 \times \frac{8^2}{2} \times 0.5774 = 36.95 \text{ mm}^2$$

This is **50% of the single-V area**. Therefore:

$$C_{new} = \frac{46{,}415}{2} = ₹23{,}207.5$$

**Savings:** ₹23,207.5 per joint — a **~50% reduction**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students often forget to divide by deposition efficiency in weld cost problems. The examiner *will* deduct 1 mark if you do.
> 2. **Common mistake:** For crane utilization, students write "block is heavier than crane, so unsafe" without computing the **design lift limit** (60% rule). You must explicitly state the rule.
> 3. **Common mistake:** In tolerance stack-up, students only compute arithmetic sum. Always mention **statistical (RSS) method** for full marks.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Confusing "block weight" with "block bounding volume × density." Block weight uses **actual fabricated steel volume**, not bounding box volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DFP in Shipbuilding**

- **DFP** = designing the ship with production-friendly features, integrated from day one.
- **Five pillars:** Standardization, Simplification, Modularization, DFW, DFA/DFI/DFMt.
- **Block weight limit:** $W_{block} \le 0.6 \times W_{crane}$ (yard standard).
- **Standard plate sizes:** $1.5 \times 6$ m, $2 \times 8$ m. **Standard lengths:** 6, 12, 18 m.
- **Block dimensions:** typically limited to ~30 m length, ~12 m height, ~50 m width.
- **Weld position cost ratio:** 1G : 2G : 3G : 4G = **1 : 2 : 3 : 5**.
- **Weld groove preference:** single-V for ≤ 12 mm; **X-groove** for 12–25 mm.
- **Tolerance stack-up:** Arithmetic (worst case) vs. **Statistical (RSS)** — use statistical for design verification.
- **Simplification Index (SI):** Aim $SI < 8\%$ for primary hull.
- **Weld clearance:** minimum 300 mm throat clearance (IACS UR W).
- **Steel density:** **7850 kg/m³** (always use this in KTU numericals).
- **Crane utilization factor ($\eta$):** design limit is **0.6 to 0.7**.
- **Common DFP mistakes to avoid in exam:**
  - Forgetting the 60% crane rule
  - Forgetting the deposition efficiency in weld cost problems
  - Mixing up the weld position ratio direction
  - Not justifying simplification in design choices
- **Key standards to remember:** AWS A3.0, IACS UR W, ISO 9001, SOLAS Ch. II-1 (manholes).
- **DFP is a "voice of the yard" in the design office** — it converts design intent into buildable reality.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining of Parts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Joining of Parts

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Joining of Parts** in shipbuilding technology refers to the comprehensive set of mechanical, thermal, chemical, and adhesive processes used to permanently or semi-permanently unite individual steel plates, sections, stiffeners, bulkheads, and prefabricated sub-assemblies into a structurally continuous and watertight ship hull. The selection of a joining process is governed by structural duty, material thickness, location (in-shop or on-board), classification society rules (IACS members: ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS, KR), and production economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Joining in shipbuilding encompasses *primary structural welding* (butt and fillet welds in plate fabrication), *secondary mechanical fastening* (riveting for non-critical redundancy), and *bolted connections* (for outfitting items, machinery seatings, and detachable panels).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Skeleton" Analogy

Imagine a ship hull as a **human skeleton**. The bones are the steel plates and profiles; the **joints** (shoulder, knee, elbow) are the welded/riveted connections. Just as ligaments (fillet welds) hold bones together at angles and cartilage (butt welds) fuses load-bearing flat surfaces, ship welds transfer enormous hydrodynamic, cargo, and dynamic loads across plate boundaries. A weak "ligament" (defective weld) in a critical location (e.g., bilge strake) can cause catastrophic structural failure—exactly why classification societies enforce stringent welding standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Modern merchant ships use **welding for >95%** of primary structure. Riveting survives only in specific secondary applications and certain naval/historical vessels.

## 1.3 Classification of Joining Methods in Shipbuilding

The three principal joining families are:

| Family | Process Examples | Typical Application | Modern Usage % |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fusion Welding** | SMAW, GMAW (CO₂/MIG), FCAW, SAW, GTAW (TIG) | Hull plates, frames, brackets | ~95% |
| **Mechanical Fastening** | Hot/Cold Riveting, Bolting | Repairs, light scantlings, outfitting | ~4% |
| **Adhesive Bonding** | Epoxy structural adhesives | Sandwich panels, GRP joints | ~1% |

## 1.4 Standard Edge Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Root Face** = the flat surface at the root of the prepared edge.  
> **Root gap** = the distance between the two root faces before welding.  
> **Groove angle** = the included angle of the prepared edge.  
> **Bevel** = the sloped surface forming the groove.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Almost every KTU question on this module begins with "Explain the various methods of joining parts in shipbuilding" or "Compare welding and riveting." Always start with a **classification diagram**, then drill into the **two dominant processes**: Welding (modern) and Riveting (legacy).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fusion Welding in Shipbuilding — Process Logic

Fusion welding joins metals by **localized melting** of base metal and filler metal without macroscopic mechanical pressure. The chain of logic in any shipyard weld is:

1. **Edge Preparation** → Plates are beveled to specified groove angle (typically **45°–60°** for V-groove, **10°–15°** for X-groove heavy plate).
2. **Assembly & Tacking** → Plates are aligned with **root gap (g)** typically **2 mm–4 mm** for SMAW/GMAW.
3. **Preheating** → For thick plates (>25 mm) or high-carbon equivalents (CE > 0.45), preheat to **100°C–200°C** to prevent Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC).
4. **Deposition** → Filler metal is melted by an electric arc or gas flame and deposited into the groove.
5. **Solidification & Cooling** → Weld metal solidifies, forming a metallurgical bond.
6. **Inspection** → Visual + NDT (UT, RT, MT, PT) as per IACS UR W17 / W28.

## 2.2 The Five Dominant Welding Processes in Shipyards

### 2.2.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — "Stick Welding"

- **Mechanism:** An electric arc strikes between a flux-coated consumable electrode and the base metal.
- **Why it dominates shipyards:** Portable, rugged, works in wind/rain, ideal for outdoor erection and overhead positions.
- **Limitation:** Slag inclusion, lower deposition rate (~1–3 kg/hr), frequent electrode changes.
- **Shipyard usage:** General fabrication, repair, on-board erection, overhead and vertical welds.

### 2.2.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / CO₂ Welding / MIG/MAG)

- **Mechanism:** Continuous solid wire electrode fed through a gun; arc shielded by inert (Ar) or active (CO₂/Ar+CO₂) gas.
- **Why preferred:** High deposition (**4–8 kg/hr**), no slag, semi-automatic, excellent for long fillet welds on stiffeners.
- **Limitation:** Wind-sensitive (gas shielding disrupted), needs clean mill scale-free surface.
- **Shipyard usage:** Panel line welding, long seams in sub-assembly shops.

### 2.2.3 Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)

- **Mechanism:** Tubular electrode filled with flux; can be self-shielded (FCAW-SS) or gas-shielded (FCAW-GS).
- **Why preferred:** Combines SMAW's portability with GMAW's high deposition; deep penetration; ideal for thick plate.
- **Shipyard usage:** Double-sided butt welds in thick hull plating (>20 mm).

### 2.2.4 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)

- **Mechanism:** Arc is submerged under a granular flux blanket; high current (up to 2000 A) possible.
- **Why preferred:** Highest deposition rate (**10–30 kg/hr**), excellent weld quality, minimal spatter.
- **Limitation:** Limited to **flat (1G/1F) positions** only.
- **Shipyard usage:** Panel line butt welds in the fabrication shop (horizontal plate rolls).

### 2.2.5 Electroslag Welding (ESW)

- **Mechanism:** Molten slag bath melts the wire and base metal; no arc — conduction through molten slag.
- **Why preferred:** Single-pass welds in **very thick plates (50–450 mm)** used for stems, stern frames, rudder horns.
- **Limitation:** Coarse grain in Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) → requires **normalizing** afterward.

## 2.3 Mechanical Fastening — Riveting & Bolting

### 2.3.1 Hot Riveting (Historical Dominance → Legacy Role)

A **rivet** is a cylindrical shank with a pre-formed head. Hot riveting heats the rivet to **cherry-red (~900°C)**, drives it into the hole, and **upsets** a second head by hammering or hydraulic pressure. Upon cooling, the rivet contracts longitudinally, generating **clamping force** that grips the plates together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Erection Sequence in Riveted Ships:** Laying plates → drilling/punching holes (sub-assembled templates) → fitting rivets → heating in forge → driving → caulking seams with pneumatic tools to ensure water-tightness.

### 2.3.2 Bolted Connections

Bolts (hex, countersunk, tension control/Torq-Shear bolts) are used for:
- Detachable machinery seatings
- Stern tube bearing assemblies
- Stern frame connections (large fitted bolts)
- Outfitting items (handrails, ladder stanchions)
- Modern **friction-grip High-Strength Friction Grip (HSFG) bolts** in some superstructure connections.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-relevant for KTU Module 3 numerical/derivation questions.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $V$ = Arc voltage (V); $I$ = Current (A); $S$ = Travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternative)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm | $\eta$ = Process thermal efficiency (SMAW≈0.8, GMAW≈0.7, SAW≈1.0, GTAW≈0.6) |
| 3 | **Weld Metal Deposition Rate** | $R = \dfrac{I \cdot k}{1000}$ | kg/hr | $k$ = deposition constant (depends on wire diameter & process) |
| 4 | **Weld Area (Fillet)** | $A_f = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L$ | mm² | $s$ = leg size (mm); $L$ = weld length (mm) |
| 5 | **Fillet Weld Throat** | $t = 0.707 \cdot s$ | mm | Theoretical throat of equal-leg fillet |
| 6 | **Butt Weld Area (V-groove)** | $A_b = T \cdot L + \dfrac{V^2 \cdot L}{2}$ | mm² | $T$ = plate thickness; $V$ = groove depth |
| 7 | **Shear Stress in Fillet Weld** | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}$ | N/mm² | $F$ = applied force (N) |
| 8 | **Rivet Shear Strength** | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_s$ | N | $n$ = number of shear planes; $d$ = rivet dia; $\tau_s$ = allowable shear stress |
| 9 | **Rivet Bearing / Crushing** | $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | N | $t$ = plate thickness; $\sigma_b$ = allowable bearing stress |
| 10 | **Joint Efficiency** | $\eta_j = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid\ plate}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of joint strength to solid plate strength |
| 11 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Used to assess weldability; >0.45 needs preheat |
| 12 | **Weld Shrinkage (Transverse)** | $\Delta L \approx 0.002 \cdot L$ | mm | Approximate for mild steel butt weld |
| 13 | **Pre-heat Temperature (estimate)** | $T_p = 200 \cdot \sqrt{t} - 50$ | °C | $t$ = plate thickness (mm); empirical rule |
| 14 | **Angular Distortion (V-groove)** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{0.6 \cdot A_w}{T^2}$ | rad | $A_w$ = weld cross-sectional area; $T$ = plate thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip for Heat Input (HI):** Always carry **kJ/mm** — too high HI → coarse grain & HAZ softening; too low → lack of fusion. **Typical shipbuilding target: 0.8–2.0 kJ/mm for mild steel.**

## 2.5 Why Joining Methods Matter in Ship Engineering

- **Hull Girder Integrity:** The entire ship behaves as a box girder; weld quality at block joints dictates global strength.
- **Fatigue Life:** Ships experience **10⁸–10⁹** stress cycles over a 25-year life; weld toe geometry (improved by grinding or Toe Dressing / TIG Dressing) is the dominant fatigue parameter.
- **Weldability vs. Strength:** Modern AH36/DH36/EH36 grades (yield 355 MPa) demand careful CE control.
- **Production Rate:** Modern shipyards aim for **80–120 man-hours per CGT** (Compensated Gross Tonnage); joining process choice directly drives this metric.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Heat Input in Arc Welding

The **heat input** (HI) determines weld metallurgy, HAZ size, and distortion. The starting point is **electrical power** consumed at the arc.

**Step 1 — Power Input at the Arc**

$$
P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(watts)}
$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (V) and $I$ is the welding current (A). This is the total electrical power delivered to the arc.

**Step 2 — Energy per Unit Length (Joules per millimetre of weld)**

Energy delivered per millimetre of weld travel equals power divided by travel speed. Since travel speed $S$ is usually given in **mm/min**, we multiply the numerator by 60 to convert minutes to seconds:

$$
E_{mm} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}
$$

**Step 3 — Accounting for Thermal Efficiency**

Not all electrical energy enters the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ is transferred to the weld pool; the rest is lost as radiation, spatter, and convective losses. Multiply by $\eta$:

$$
HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

The factor 1000 converts J → kJ for shipbuilding reporting standards.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)**

A panel line SAW weld uses $V = 32\,\text{V}$, $I = 800\,\text{A}$, travel speed $S = 600\,\text{mm/min}$, with $\eta = 1.0$ for SAW. Calculate the heat input.

$$
HI = \dfrac{1.0 \cdot 32 \cdot 800 \cdot 60}{600 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{1{,}536{,}000}{600{,}000} = 2.56\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Since $2.56\,\text{kJ/mm}$ is at the **upper boundary** for 20 mm mild steel plate, the welder should either reduce travel speed or current to avoid excessive HAZ softening and angular distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for shipbuilding mild steel:** Limit HI to **$1.5\text{–}2.0\,\text{kJ/mm}$** to retain adequate notch toughness in the HAZ.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Strength of a Fillet Weld in Ship Structure

A fillet weld of leg size $s$ joining two plates is loaded in shear along its **throat plane**.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Fillet Weld**

For an equal-leg fillet weld with leg $s$, the theoretical throat (perpendicular distance from root to hypotenuse face) is:

$$
t = s \cdot \cos 45^\circ = s \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,s
$$

**Step 2 — Shear Area for Length $L$**

$$
A_s = t \cdot L = 0.707\,s \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Allowable Load on the Weld**

Let $\tau_w$ be the allowable shear stress in the weld throat. Then the load-carrying capacity of the weld is:

$$
F = \tau_w \cdot A_s = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_w
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example**

A 6 mm leg fillet weld, 200 mm long, in Grade A steel with $\tau_w = 110\,\text{MPa}$. Find the load capacity.

$$
F = 0.707 \cdot 6 \cdot 200 \cdot 110 = 93{,}324\ \text{N} \approx 93.3\ \text{kN}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Riveted Joint Efficiency (Shear Failure Mode)

A **lap riveted joint** with plate width $b$ and thickness $t$, joined by $n$ rivets of diameter $d$ in **single shear**, fails by the weaker of:
- (a) Shearing of rivets
- (b) Bearing (crushing) of rivet hole
- (c) Tearing of plate between rivet hole and edge

**Step 1 — Strength by Rivet Shearing**

$$
P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_s
$$

**Step 2 — Strength by Bearing**

$$
P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

**Step 3 — Strength of Plate in Tension at Critical Section**

If pitch (centre-to-centre spacing) is $p$ and the critical section removes **one rivet hole** of diameter $d$:

$$
P_t = (b - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

**Step 4 — Joint Strength = Minimum of all Three**

$$
P_{joint} = \min(P_s,\ P_b,\ P_t)
$$

**Step 5 — Joint Efficiency**

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)**

A double-riveted lap joint, $b = 100\,\text{mm}$, $t = 10\,\text{mm}$, $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, $n = 3$ rivets per row, $\tau_s = 90\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_b = 160\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$. Find the joint efficiency.

$$
P_s = 3 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (20)^2}{4} \cdot 90 = 3 \cdot 314.16 \cdot 90 = 84{,}823\ \text{N}
$$

$$
P_b = 3 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 \cdot 160 = 96{,}000\ \text{N}
$$

$$
P_t = (100 - 20) \cdot 10 \cdot 120 = 96{,}000\ \text{N}
$$

Minimum = $P_s = 84{,}823\,\text{N}$. Solid plate strength = $100 \cdot 10 \cdot 120 = 120{,}000\,\text{N}$.

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{84{,}823}{120{,}000} = 0.707 \approx 70.7\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A riveted lap joint typically achieves only **60–75% efficiency**, whereas a properly welded butt joint (full penetration, ground flush) achieves **100% efficiency**. This single fact drove the industry's shift from riveting to welding in the 1940s–1960s.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Welding vs. Riveting

| Parameter | Welding | Riveting |
|---|---|---|
| Joint efficiency | **90–100%** | 60–75% |
| Weight addition | Minimal (fillet metal) | Significant (rivet heads, laps) |
| Skill required | High (certified welders) | Medium (riveters, heaters) |
| Speed | High (mechanized) | Slow |
| Watertightness | Excellent (no gaps) | Requires caulking |
| Noise during construction | Low | Very high (pneumatic hammers) |
| Reversibility | Difficult (must grind out) | Easy (drill out) |
| Fatigue performance | Sensitive to weld toe | Robust |
| Modern shipbuilding dominance | **Universal** | **Almost extinct** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Joining Methods in Shipbuilding

```mermaid

    nodeA["Joining of Parts in Shipbuilding"]
    nodeB["Fusion Welding"]
    nodeC["Mechanical Fastening"]

    nodeK["Cold Riveting"]
    nodeL["Bolted HSFG"]
    nodeM["Fitted Bolts"]

    nodeN["Epoxy Structural"]
    nodeO["Methacrylate"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD

    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI

    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeM

    nodeD --> nodeN
    nodeD --> nodeO
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of a Panel-Line Welding Station

```mermaid
    stepA["Steel Plate Incoming"]
    stepB["Shot Blasting Surface Cleaning"]

    stepF["Root Run Backing Pass"]
    stepG["Fill Runs Multipass SAW"]
    stepH["Cap Run Finishing"]
    stepI["Visual Inspection VT"]
    stepJ["NDT Ultrasonic Testing"]
    stepK["Weld Repair if Defects"]
    stepL["Panel to Sub Assembly Yard"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK --> stepL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shipyard Block Joining Sequence

```mermaid

    blk1["Steel Plate"]
    blk2["Plate Panel Flat Sub Assembly"]

    blk1 --> blk2 --> blk3 --> blk4 --> blk5 --> blk6 --> blk7 --> blk8
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Weld Joint Types (Block Architecture)

```mermaid

    jt1["Butt Joint Square Groove"]

    jt6["Fillet Weld Edge"]
    jt7["Plug Weld and Slot Weld"]
    jt8["Spot Weld Projection"]

    subA["Primary Hull Joints"] --> jt1
    subA --> jt2
    subA --> jt3
    subA --> jt4

    subB --> jt6
    subC["Special Joints"] --> jt7
    subC --> jt8
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart — Welding Procedure Decision Logic

