```mermaid
    q1{"Plate Thickness in mm"}
    q2{"Position Flat H V OH"}
    q3{"Shop or On Board"}
    q4{"Carbon Equivalent CE"}

    p1["SAW 2 wire tandem if t greater 15 and shop flat"]
    p2["FCAW GMAW if t 10 to 30 multipass"]
    p3["SMAW if on board overhead vertical"]
    p4["Preheat 100 to 200 deg C if CE greater 0.45"]

    q1 --> q2 --> q3 --> q4 --> p1
    q1 --> p2
    q3 --> p3
    q4 --> p4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A block-level functional diagram such as the **panel-line flow (4.2)** often scores full marks in Part B (14-mark) questions because it demonstrates process understanding beyond rote memorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Model) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between SMAW and GMAW welding processes with respect to shipbuilding applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SMAW | GMAW |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Flux-coated consumable stick | Continuous solid wire |
| Shielding | Slag + gas from flux decomposition | External CO₂ or Ar+CO₂ gas |
| Deposition rate | Low (1–3 kg/hr) | High (4–8 kg/hr) |
| Best use in shipyard | Overhead, vertical, on-board erection | Flat panel-line shop welding |
| Wind sensitivity | Low | High (gas shield blown away) |

*(1 mark for each valid comparison point)*

---

### Question 2 (Model) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "joint efficiency" of a riveted joint. Why is it lower than a welded joint?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Joint efficiency is the ratio of the strength of the riveted joint to the strength of an unwelded solid plate of equivalent dimensions, expressed as:

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid\ plate}}
$$

It is lower in riveted joints because:
1. **Loss of plate section** at the rivet hole (stress concentration).
2. **Lap plates** introduce eccentric load paths → secondary bending.
3. **Rivet shear & bearing** limits are typically below plate tensile capacity.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for causes)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** KTU provides Module-level internal choice. Below are **TWO independent alternative questions** aligned to Module 3 — Joining of Parts. Choose **either A or B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify and explain the **various types of welding joints** used in ship hull construction. Discuss the edge preparation required for thick plates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **butt weld** is to be made on a 20 mm thick mild steel plate using **SAW**. The arc voltage is 30 V, current 750 A, travel speed 400 mm/min, and process efficiency is 1.0. Determine:
   - (i) The **heat input** in kJ/mm
   - (ii) The **deposition rate** if the wire-feed constant $k = 0.018\,\text{kg/A·hr}$
   - (iii) Comment on whether the heat input is **acceptable** for the given plate thickness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Classification of Weld Joints (4 marks)**

Weld joints in shipbuilding are classified into **five basic types** by AWS / ISO 9692:

| Joint Type | Description | Shipbuilding Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Butt Joint** | Two plates lying in the same plane | Shell plate seams, deck plating |
| **Corner Joint** | Two plates meeting at an angle (90° typically) | Bulkhead to shell, deck to side |
| **T-Joint** | One plate perpendicular to another | Stiffener to plate, floor to shell |
| **Lap Joint** | Plates overlap with fillet welds at edges | Secondary brackets, light stiffeners |
| **Edge Joint** | Plates joined at parallel edges | Rarely used; for thin plating |

Butt joints are subdivided by **groove shape**:
- **Square butt** (no bevel) → $t < 6\,\text{mm}$
- **Single-V butt** → $6 \le t < 20\,\text{mm}$
- **Single-U or J-groove** → reduces weld metal volume
- **Double-V (X) butt** → $t > 20\,\text{mm}$ — splits weld volume between two sides, reducing distortion
- **Double-U butt** → $t > 40\,\text{mm}$ heavy stems/stern frames

**2. Edge Preparation (3 marks)**

For plates $t > 6\,\text{mm}$, edges are **beveled** by **oxy-fuel cutting** or **plasma arc cutting**:

- **Groove angle:** **45°–60°** (V-groove); **10°–15°** (X-groove per side)
- **Root face:** **1–3 mm** (ensures full penetration without burn-through)
- **Root gap:** **2–4 mm** for SMAW/GMAW; **0–2 mm** for SAW
- **Bevel method:** Machined or thermally cut (oxy-fuel most common in yards)
- **Cleaning:** Grind away oxide scale within **20 mm** of groove before welding

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Heat Input Calculation (3 marks)**

Using the standard formula:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

Substituting $V = 30\,\text{V}$, $I = 750\,\text{A}$, $S = 400\,\text{mm/min}$, $\eta = 1.0$:

$$
HI = \dfrac{1.0 \cdot 30 \cdot 750 \cdot 60}{400 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{1{,}350{,}000}{400{,}000} = 3.375\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**(ii) Deposition Rate (2 marks)**

$$
R = I \cdot k = 750 \cdot 0.018 = 13.5\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**(iii) Acceptability Comment (2 marks)**

The standard upper limit for 20 mm mild steel is approximately **2.0 kJ/mm**. The calculated $3.375\,\text{kJ/mm}$ **exceeds** this limit. **Recommendations:**
- Reduce current to $I = 450\,\text{A}$ (keeping $V$ and $S$ constant) → $HI = 2.025\,\text{kJ/mm}$ (acceptable).
- Or increase travel speed to $S = 675\,\text{mm/min}$ → $HI = 2.0\,\text{kJ/mm}$.
- Excessive heat input risks **HAZ grain coarsening**, **reduced impact toughness**, and **angular distortion**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to multiply by **60** in the numerator and **1000** in the denominator of the heat input formula, losing 2 marks instantly. Always convert to **kJ/mm** as the final unit. Also, **never forget to multiply by η** if the process efficiency is given.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **process of riveting** in shipbuilding with a sketch. Why has riveting been **largely replaced by welding** in modern ships? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double-riveted lap joint** is used to connect two mild steel plates of width $b = 120\,\text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 12\,\text{mm}$. The rivet diameter is $d = 22\,\text{mm}$, and the number of rivets per row is $n = 4$. Given $\tau_s = 100\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_b = 180\,\text{MPa}$, and $\sigma_t = 130\,\text{MPa}$, calculate:
   - (i) Strength by **rivet shear**
   - (ii) Strength by **bearing**
   - (iii) Strength of **plate in tension** at the critical section
   - (iv) **Joint efficiency** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Hot Riveting Process (4 marks)**

Sequence of operations in a riveted ship hull:

1. **Marking and Hole Drilling:** Plates are assembled in position; rivet holes (1.5–2 mm larger than rivet shank) are drilled (preferred) or punched using **gang drills** with multiple spindles through pre-positioned templates.
2. **Rivet Heating:** Mild steel rivets are heated in a **forge, gas furnace, or induction heater** to a cherry-red temperature of approximately **900°C–950°C**.
3. **Driving:** The red-hot rivet is inserted into the hole; a **pneumatic hammer** (or hydraulic riveter in modern practice) upsets the second head. The rivet length is chosen so that enough material protrudes to form the closing head.
4. **Cooling and Contracting:** As the rivet cools, it contracts longitudinally, drawing the plates together and creating a high **clamping force** across the joint.
5. **Caulking:** Edges of plates are **caulked** with a pneumatic caulking tool to seal seams and ensure watertightness.

**2. Reasons Riveting Was Replaced (3 marks)**

| # | Reason |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Higher joint efficiency** of welds (≈100%) vs. riveted joints (≈70%) |
| 2 | **Weight saving** — rivet heads and laps add deadweight |
| 3 | **Faster construction** — welding is mechanizable (SAW, GMAW robotic) |
| 4 | **Quieter yards** — riveting required constant pneumatic hammering |
| 5 | **Better fatigue performance** of properly ground butt welds |
| 6 | **Lower skill threshold** for repetitive mechanized welds vs. hand riveting |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Strength by Rivet Shear (2 marks)**

$$
P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_s
$$

$$
P_s = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (22)^2}{4} \cdot 100 = 4 \cdot 380.13 \cdot 100 = 152{,}053\ \text{N} \approx 152.05\ \text{kN}
$$

**(ii) Strength by Bearing (2 marks)**

$$
P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

$$
P_b = 4 \cdot 22 \cdot 12 \cdot 180 = 190{,}080\ \text{N} \approx 190.08\ \text{kN}
$$

**(iii) Plate Tension at Critical Section (2 marks)**

Critical section removes one rivet hole:

$$
P_t = (b - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (120 - 22) \cdot 12 \cdot 130
$$

$$
P_t = 98 \cdot 12 \cdot 130 = 152{,}880\ \text{N} \approx 152.88\ \text{kN}
$$

**(iv) Joint Efficiency (1 mark)**

$$
P_{joint} = \min(P_s,\ P_b,\ P_t) = \min(152.05,\ 190.08,\ 152.88) = 152.05\ \text{kN}
$$

Solid plate strength:

$$
P_{solid} = b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 120 \cdot 12 \cdot 130 = 187{,}200\ \text{N} = 187.20\ \text{kN}
$$

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{152.05}{187.20} = 0.812 \approx 81.2\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students take the **average** or the **maximum** of $P_s, P_b, P_t$ instead of the **minimum**. The joint fails by the **weakest mode** — always select $\min(P_s, P_b, P_t)$. Also ensure units: **MPa × mm² = N**, then convert to **kN** for final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Joining of Parts**

### A. Welding Fundamentals
- ☐ **Five welding processes**: SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, ESW — know one key advantage and one limitation of each.
- ☐ **Heat input formula** must include $\eta$, factor of 60, and factor of 1000 to land in **kJ/mm**.
- ☐ **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** determines preheat necessity (>0.45% triggers preheat 100–200°C).
- ☐ **Fillet weld throat** = $0.707 \cdot s$ (leg size).
- ☐ **Joint efficiency** of full-penetration butt weld ≈ **100%**; fillet weld lower.

### B. Riveting Fundamentals
- ☐ Hot riveting done at **900°C cherry red**; cooling contraction provides clamping.
- ☐ Rivet failure modes: **shear**, **bearing**, **plate tearing** → take the **minimum**.
- ☐ Riveted lap joint efficiency typically **60–75%**.
- ☐ **Caulking** is essential for watertightness of riveted seams.

### C. Bolted Connections
- ☐ Bolts used for **detachable** applications: machinery seatings, stern tubes, outfitting.
- ☐ **HSFG bolts** rely on friction between clamped plates.

### D. Joint Types & Edge Preparation
- ☐ Butt joint types: **Square, V, U, X, J-groove** — choose by thickness.
- ☐ Groove angle: **45°–60°** (V-groove); root face: **1–3 mm**; root gap: **2–4 mm**.
- ☐ Multipass welding used for $t > 6\,\text{mm}$.

### E. Modern Shipyard Practice
- ☐ **Panel line** → uses **SAW** (shop, flat).
- ☐ **Erection / on-board** → uses **SMAW, FCAW** (portable, all-position).
- ☐ **Thick stern frames / stems** → **Electroslag Welding** (single-pass up to 450 mm).
- ☐ **Pre-fabrication** at panel → sub-block → mega-block → grand-block levels.
- ☐ **Classification society rules** (IACS UR W series) govern welder qualification, procedure qualification, and NDT acceptance.

### F. Distortion Control
- ☐ **Angular distortion** dominant in V-groove butt welds → counter by **double-sided welding** (X-groove) or **back-step technique**.
- ☐ **Longitudinal shrinkage** ≈ $0.002 \cdot L$ — compensate by over-length before cutting.

### G. Formula Quick-Reference (Memorize)
- ☐ $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm
- ☐ $t_{throat} = 0.707\,s$
- ☐ $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707\,s\,L}$
- ☐ $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\tau_s$
- ☐ $P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$
- ☐ $\eta_j = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{b\,t\,\sigma_t}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** Always open a 14-mark answer with a **classification diagram** (block diagram or table). Follow with a **process flow** for at least one major welding process (e.g., SAW). End with a **formula application** or a numerical solve. This structure satisfies all KTU 2024 rubric levels — *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse* — and consistently scores above 12/14 in board valuations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relations Between Structural Design and Prefabrication
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relations Between Structural Design and Prefabrication

# ⚓ Module 3 — Relations Between Structural Design and Prefabrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of **structural engineering**, **production engineering**, and **logistics planning** in modern shipbuilding. It is a high-yield ESE topic because it directly addresses *why* certain structural geometries are preferred in real shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries, etc.) over textbook-optimal designs.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

**Structural Design** in shipbuilding refers to the engineering process of determining the *scantlings* (plate thicknesses, stiffener sizes, frame spacings, girder dimensions) of a ship's hull, superstructure, and internal subdivisions such that the vessel can safely withstand all design loads defined by classification society rules (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) and statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL).

**Prefabrication** refers to the yard philosophy of *building the ship in reverse* — assembling sub-assemblies, panels, and three-dimensional **blocks (or "modules")** in a controlled workshop or assembly hall, lifting them with gantry or floating cranes, and joining them on the slipway, in a dry dock, or on a floating dock.

The **relation between structural design and prefabrication** is therefore a **bi-directional coupling**: the design must enable efficient prefabrication, and the prefabrication strategy must respect structural continuity, load paths, and tolerance budgets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PENST632, Module 3):** *Students should understand that structural decisions (plate size, stiffener orientation, bracket geometry) are not made in isolation — they are subordinated to the chosen block-construction strategy. A "good" structural design is one that is **buildable, weldable, liftable, and transportable** — not merely one that satisfies scantling rules.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The IKEA Wardrobe Principle

Imagine you are designing a wardrobe (the **ship**) and you must deliver it flat-packed to the customer (the **sea**). Your design choices are constrained by:

- The **maximum size of a flat-pack piece** (analogous to the **maximum block size** the yard crane can lift — typically **300 to 1,200 tonnes** for modern mega-blocks).
- The **ease of bolting/screwing** in the customer's room (analogous to **weld access** between blocks — *"no welding in inaccessible corners"*).
- **Standard screw sizes** used across all products (analogous to **standardised plate widths, stiffener sections, and weld preparations** used across the yard).
- **Reasonable tolerance** so pieces fit even if the customer's floor is slightly uneven (analogous to **tolerance stack-up management** between blocks).

A wardrobe designed without thinking about flat-packing will look elegant on the designer's CAD screen but be **impossible to assemble at the customer's site**. A ship designed without thinking about prefabrication will pass scantling checks but **shut down the production line**. This is the essence of *Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)* in shipbuilding.

> [!TIP]
> **Real shipyard analogy:** Hyundai Heavy Industries (HHI) and Daewoo Shipbuilding & Marine Engineering (DSME) shifted from **"design-then-build"** to **"design-for-block-construction"** in the 1990s. The structural drawing office at HHI's Ulsan yard now receives input from production planners *before* scantlings are finalized — a direct implementation of the principle covered in this module.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Operational Metrics

The following **bold** metrics define the coupling between design and prefabrication in any modern yard:

| Metric | Typical Range | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum block lift weight** | **300 – 1,200 t** | Sets maximum plate-panel and sub-block size |
| **Maximum block dimensions (L × B × H)** | **40 m × 24 m × 18 m** | Constrains structural panelisation |
| **Tolerance budget per block** | **± 3 mm to ± 6 mm** on plate edges | Drives joint-gap and weld-prep design |
| **Welding position ratio (down-hand preferred)** | **> 70 % down-hand / flat** | Steers stiffener orientation and bracket design |
| **Block erection cycle target** | **5 – 14 days per block** | Drives sub-division into 50–200 blocks |
| **Overall build duration (VLCC)** | **12 – 18 months** | Function of block count, tonnage, and yard layout |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often quote crane capacity as a *static* number. In KTU answers, always state that **block weight must be ≤ crane capacity × dynamic factor (typically 0.75 to 0.85)** to account for rigging, slinging, and sea-state at the quay.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of the Design–Prefabrication Coupling

The relationship between structural design and prefabrication rests on **five interacting pillars**. Each pillar imposes constraints that the structural designer must internalize *before* the first scantling is computed.

### Pillar 1 — Block Philosophy and Sub-Division Strategy

A modern commercial ship is divided into **50 to 250 blocks** (a VLCC has ~80–120, a 24,000 TEU container ship has ~180–220). The **structural designer must align scantling boundaries with block boundaries** so that:

- Primary structural members (transverse rings, longitudinal girders) are **continuous within a block** wherever possible.
- Block joints coincide with **shear-stress minima** in the hull girder to minimize fatigue at the assembly welds.
- Plate panels within a block are sized to fit **standard shop-panel presses** (typical **4 m × 12 m** or **4 m × 15 m** plate panels).

### Pillar 2 — Weld Accessibility and Welding Position

Welding cost and quality vary dramatically with position:

- **1G / 1F (flat):** Highest deposition rate (~**3–5 kg/h**), best quality, lowest cost.
- **2G / 2F (horizontal):** ~**2–4 kg/h**.
- **3G / 3F (vertical):** ~**1.5–2.5 kg/h**.
- **4G / 4F (overhead):** ~**1.0–1.5 kg/h**, highest defect rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** A structural layout that pushes > **70 %** of the total weld length into 1G/2G positions is considered "prefabrication-friendly." Designs that force 3G/4G welding are penalized in yard throughput, even if scantlings are technically optimal.

### Pillar 3 — Lifting Geometry and Sling Load Paths

Every block must be designed so that **lifting pads, pad-eyes, or strong-backs** can be attached without inducing **local buckling, plate distortion, or weld cracking**. This forces the designer to:

- Provide **reinforcement doubler plates** at sling locations.
- Maintain **plate flatness** under the predicted sling load (often the **most severe in-plane compression** the panel will ever see — exceeding even service-condition sea loads).
- Consider **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** of **1.25 to 2.0** during the lift.

### Pillar 4 — Standardisation and Series Production

The greatest cost saving in shipbuilding comes from **repeating the same detail hundreds of times**. Standardization drives:

- **Common plate thicknesses** within a steel grade group (e.g., grade A: 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 25 mm).
- **Standard stiffener sections** (L, T, bulb, flat bar) drawn from a restricted catalogue (e.g., **HP bulb flats**: HP160, HP200, HP220, HP260, HP300, HP340, HP380).
- **Standard bracket geometries** (typically **300 mm leg × 300 mm leg** with 10 mm free-edge stiffener).
- **Common weld preparations** (typically **30° included angle, 2 mm root gap, double-sided for plates ≥ 8 mm**).

### Pillar 5 — Tolerance Stack-Up and Joint Design

Blocks are built to **± 3 mm** on nominal dimensions. When 100 blocks are erected, the cumulative tolerance drift can be **± 30 mm or more**. The structural designer must provide:

- **Adjustable joint details** (e.g., tapered shim plates, slotted bolt holes in outfitting connections).
- **Weld gap control** via **spacer clips** or **strong-backs** left in place until tack welding is complete.
- **Sequence of erection** that allows compensation of accumulated error (usually from midship outward, closing at the bow/stern).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship that KTU examiners expect a shipbuilding student to recall. **Memorize the units and the typical numerical ranges** — questions frequently test whether you can spot an *unrealistic* number in a problem.

| # | Relationship / Formula | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block weight ≤ Crane capacity × Dynamic factor | $W_b \le W_{crane} \cdot \eta_{dyn}$ | $\eta_{dyn} = 0.75$ to $0.85$ | Maximum block mass for yard crane |
| 2 | Lifting dynamic amplification factor (in-service lift, calm water) | $DAF$ | **1.25 – 1.50** | Sling tension design |
| 3 | Lifting DAF (in-service, **sea-state** at quay) | $DAF_{sea}$ | **1.50 – 2.00** | Floating-dock or quayside lifts |
| 4 | Plate panel slenderness (b/t limit for unstiffened panels under sling load) | $\beta = b / t$ | $\le 30$ to $50$ (depending on grade) | Buckling check on lifted panel |
| 5 | Weld length per metre of block joint (typical double-shell transverse) | $L_w$ | **6 – 12 m** per metre of joint | Productivity planning |
| 6 | Steel consumption per CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage) | $W_s / CGT$ | **0.020 – 0.035 t/CGT** | Productivity benchmark |
| 7 | Man-hours per tonne of steel (prefabrication + erection) | $MH / t$ | **25 – 45 MH/t** | Modern yards (Korea, Japan); > 60 in older yards |
| 8 | Welding deposition rate (1G, GMAW) | $R_{dep}$ | **3.0 – 5.0 kg/h** | Cycle-time estimation |
| 9 | Welding deposition rate (3G / 4G) | $R_{dep,v}$ | **1.0 – 2.5 kg/h** | Penalty for non-down-hand |
| 10 | Block tolerance (per block, edge-to-edge) | $\delta_{block}$ | **± 3 mm to ± 6 mm** | Tolerance stack-up |
| 11 | Cumulative tolerance over $N$ blocks | $\delta_{cum} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block}$ | $\mu m$ to $mm$ | Statistical stack-up |
| 12 | Number of blocks for a commercial vessel | $N_{block}$ | **50 – 250** | Hull decomposition |
| 13 | Preferred plate aspect ratio (panelisation efficiency) | $L_{panel} / W_{panel}$ | **2.0 – 3.5** | Plate nesting optimisation |
| 14 | Standard stiffener spacing (primary) | $s_{stiff}$ | **500 – 900 mm** | Plate panel sizing |
| 15 | Bracket leg length (typical) | $l_{bracket}$ | **300 – 600 mm** | End-bracket geometry |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Formula (11) uses a **square-root-of-N** statistical stack-up, **not** a worst-case $\pm N \cdot \delta_{block}$. Examiners award full marks only if you justify the use of the statistical form ("since tolerance errors are independent and approximately normally distributed").

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The principles above are the daily working language of:

- **Yard production planners** (e.g., block erection sequence, crane scheduling).
- **Class society surveyors** (verifying that block joints are not placed at points of high fatigue stress in the hull girder).
- **Ship-design software** (Tribon, AVEVA Marine, NAPA, FORAN) — these tools have dedicated modules called *Production Modeling* that flag any structural member violating the five pillars above.
- **Outfit-first strategies** — modern VLCCs and LNG carriers are now built *"outfit-first"* where pipe-racks, cable trays, and engine-room modules are mounted on a block *before* it is lifted, an approach that is only feasible if the structural design has anticipated those outfit weights during the initial scantling phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Tolerance Stack-Up Across N Blocks

This is a high-yield derivation in KTU ESE questions. The goal is to show that the **statistical tolerance** of an assembled hull does not grow linearly with the number of blocks, but as the square root of $N$.

### Step 1 — Define the per-block tolerance

Each block is built to a tolerance $\delta_{block}$ on its critical edge dimension. The error on block $i$ is modeled as an independent random variable:

$$e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

where $\sigma = \delta_{block} / 2$ (i.e., a ± $\delta_{block}$ band corresponds to a two-sigma spread, the standard assumption for a **95 %** confidence band in shipbuilding tolerance budgets per ISO 2768-medium).

### Step 2 — Cumulative error of N blocks

When $N$ blocks are erected end-to-end, the total length error is the **sum of independent errors**:

$$E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i$$

### Step 3 — Variance of the sum

For independent variables, the variances add:

$$\text{Var}(E_{total}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(e_i) = N \sigma^2$$

### Step 4 — Standard deviation of the cumulative error

$$\sigma_{cum} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \sigma = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{\delta_{block}}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Express in the ± form

The **95 % confidence** tolerance band (two-sigma) of the assembled length is:

$$\delta_{cum} = 2 \sigma_{cum} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A hull built from **100 blocks** each held to **± 5 mm** has a cumulative length tolerance of approximately $\pm \sqrt{100} \cdot 5 = \pm 50 \, \text{mm}$, **not** $\pm 500 \, \text{mm}$ as a worst-case arithmetic sum would suggest. This statistical reality is what makes modern block construction feasible.

### Step 6 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style)

**Problem.** A 200 m long container ship is built from $N = 120$ blocks. Each block is held to $\delta_{block} = \pm 4 \, \text{mm}$ at the assembly edge. Determine (a) the cumulative statistical tolerance at the 95 % confidence level, and (b) the worst-case (arithmetic) tolerance for comparison.

**Solution.**

(a) Statistical (RSS — root sum square):

$$\delta_{cum}^{stat} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block} = \sqrt{120} \cdot 4 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\delta_{cum}^{stat} \approx 10.954 \cdot 4 \approx \pm 43.8 \, \text{mm}$$

(b) Worst-case (arithmetic):

$$\delta_{cum}^{arithmetic} = N \cdot \delta_{block} = 120 \cdot 4 = \pm 480 \, \text{mm}$$

The statistical value ($\pm 43.8$ mm) is **~11 times smaller** than the worst-case value, confirming why modern yards rely on the statistical budget.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students write the worst-case value and lose the conceptual mark. KTU examiners specifically test the *understanding* of the RSS approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Effective Sling Load on a Lifted Block

### Step 1 — Static sling load

For a block of mass $M$ lifted by $n$ slings at angles $\theta_i$ from vertical:

$$T_i^{static} = \frac{M \cdot g}{n \cdot \cos \theta_i}$$

where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ is the gravitational acceleration and $\theta_i$ is the sling-to-vertical angle.

### Step 2 — Apply the dynamic amplification factor

$$T_i^{dynamic} = DAF \cdot T_i^{static}$$

### Step 3 — Worked numerical example

**Problem.** A 600 t engine-room block is lifted by 4 equal slings, each at $\theta = 30°$ from vertical, in calm water inside a building dock. Take $DAF = 1.30$. Calculate the dynamic tension in each sling.

**Solution.**

Static tension per sling:

$$T^{static} = \frac{600 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 9.81}{4 \cdot \cos 30°} = \frac{5.886 \cdot 10^6}{4 \cdot 0.8660}$$

$$T^{static} = \frac{5.886 \cdot 10^6}{3.464} \approx 1.699 \cdot 10^6 \, \text{N} \approx 1,699 \, \text{kN}$$

Dynamic tension:

$$T^{dynamic} = 1.30 \cdot 1,699 \approx 2,209 \, \text{kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design implication:** The doubler plate at each sling pad must be sized for an in-plane compressive load of ~2,200 kN, distributed over the contact area between the sling pad and the parent plate. This is **often the governing buckling check** of the block — *not* the in-service wave load.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices vs. Prefabrication Impact

The following 6×5 matrix is a **KTU-favorite** synthesis tool. For each design choice (rows), identify the impact on each prefabrication pillar (columns). Memorize at least the **dominant interaction** (★) for each row.

| Design Choice | Block Sub-Division | Weld Access | Lifting Feasibility | Standardisation | Tolerance |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Long transverse frame spacing (1.0 m) | ★ Enables larger blocks | Mild improvement | Mild improvement | Mild improvement | Mild improvement |
| Use of HP bulb flats (vs. built-up T) | Mild | ★ Reduces fillet welds | Mild | ★ Single rolled section | Mild |
| Double-shell vs. single-shell engine room | Mild | ★ Walk-in weld access | Mild | Mild | Mild |
| High-tensile steel (AH36, DH36) | ★ Thinner plates, lighter blocks | Mild | ★ Lower block weight | Mild | Mild |
| Bracket end connections (vs. slotted) | Mild | ★ Bracket welding in flat | Mild | Mild | ★ Forgives tolerance |
| Orthogonal stiffener layout (vs. skewed) | Mild | Mild | Mild | ★ Repetitive NC cutting | Mild |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU answer tip:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Discuss how structural design influences prefabrication,"* reproduce the matrix above as a *summary table* and discuss 2–3 rows in detail. This is the highest-density way to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Construction Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows the *hierarchical decomposition* of a modern commercial vessel from raw steel plate to the completed hull — the single most important concept in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Steel Plate\n4 m x 12 m, grade A-AH36] --> B[Steel Stockyard\nNested and NC-cut]
    B --> C[Panel Shop\nFlat panel assembly\n1G / 2G welding]
    C --> D[Sub-Block\nT-up of 3-5 panels\n200-400 t]
    D --> E[Grand Block / Mega-Block\n3D assembly in hall\n600-1,200 t]
    E --> F[Erection on Slipway\nor in Dry Dock]
    F --> G[Outfit Integration\nPiping, electrical, HVAC]
    G --> H[Joined Hull Ring\nSection-by-section]
    H --> I[Completed Hull\nLaunch or Float-out]
    I --> J[Outfitting Quay\nFinal outfit and testing]
    J --> K[Sea Trials and Delivery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hierarchy is **four levels deep** in modern yards (Plate → Panel → Sub-Block → Grand-Block). Each level is a *welding workstation* where the position is progressively more constrained — but the *quantity* of welding done down-hand (1G/2G) is maximised at the lowest levels.

## 4.2 The Five-Pillar Coupling Architecture

This block-level functional architecture shows how the five pillars of Section 2 interact with the design office, the production planning office, and the classification society.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DesignOffice[Design Office]
        D1[Scantling Calculation\nIRS / LR / DNV Rules]
        D2[3D CAD Model\nTribon / AVEVA / NAPA]
        D3[Production Modeling Module\nDFMA Checks]
    end

    subgraph ProductionPlanning[Production Planning Office]
        P1[Block Decomposition\n50-250 blocks]
        P2[Crane and Sling Schedule]
        P3[Erection Sequence]
    end

    subgraph Yard[Yard Execution]
        Y1[Panel Shop\n1G / 2G welding]
        Y2[Block Assembly Hall\n3G acceptable]
        Y3[Erection Site\nTolerance correction]
    end

    subgraph Class[Class Society Surveyor]
        C1[Scantling Verification]
        C2[Block Joint Location\nvs. Fatigue Hot-Spots]
        C3[WPS and Welder Qualification]
    end

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3
    Y3 --> C1
    C2 -.feedback.-> D1
    C3 -.feedback.-> P1
    D3 -.DFMA flag.-> D1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The two *dashed feedback loops* in the diagram are what distinguishes **Design-for-Prefabrication** from legacy **Design-then-Build**. The class surveyor and the DFMA tool both feed information *back* to the design office, forcing iterative refinement of scantlings.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Block Joint Location

The structural designer must decide **where to cut the hull into blocks**. The decision is driven by the flow below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Hull Form Defined]) --> Q1{Loads and\nStress Field\nKnown?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Compute Hull Girder\nShear and BM Diagrams]
    Q1 -->|No| Q1b[Run Hydrostatic and\nWave Load Analysis]
    Q1b --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3[Identify Shear Minima\nLow Fatigue Zones]
    Q3 --> Q4[Identify Crane Capacity\nand Yard Layout]
    Q4 --> Q5[Propose Block Joints\nat Shear Minima]
    Q5 --> Q6{Lift Weight\n≤ Crane x DAF?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Accept Joint Location]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8[Sub-divide Block]
    Q8 --> Q5
    Q7 --> Q9[Check Bracket Geometry\nand Weld Access]
    Q9 --> Q10{>70 percent Welding\nin 1G or 2G?}
    Q10 -->|Yes| Accept([Block Joint Finalized])
    Q10 -->|No| Q11[Re-route Stiffeners\nor Adjust Brackets]
    Q11 --> Q9
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Students often place block joints at **convenient transverse bulkhead** positions, irrespective of the stress field. This is acceptable in tankers (low shear gradient) but **not** in container ships (where shear at midship is severe). The flow above is the *systematic* method.

## 4.4 Tolerance Stack-Up Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Block 1\nδ = ±4 mm] -->|joint| B[Block 2\nδ = ±4 mm]
    B -->|joint| C[Block 3\nδ = ±4 mm]
    C -->|joint| D[...]
    D -->|joint| E[Block N\nδ = ±4 mm]

    A -.error e1.-> A
    B -.error e2.-> B
    C -.error e3.-> C
    E -.error eN.-> E

    A & B & C & D & E --> F[Statistical RSS Sum\nσcum = sqrt N × δ]
    A & B & C & D & E --> G[Worst-Case Sum\nδcum = N × δ]

    F -->|Δstat| H[± 44 mm typical]
    G -->|Δwc| I[± 480 mm typical]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Use this diagram in your answer** when the question is *"How is length tolerance controlled in a block-erected hull?"* — the visual makes the answer immediately clear to the examiner.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *prefabrication* in the context of modern shipbuilding. List **two** structural-design choices that directly support prefabrication.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Prefabrication in shipbuilding is the production philosophy of *assembling the vessel in reverse* — building sub-assemblies, panels, and three-dimensional **blocks (typically 300–1,200 t each)** in controlled workshop conditions, and joining them at the erection site, thereby maximising the proportion of welding performed in the down-hand (1G/2G) position.

**Two structural-design choices supporting prefabrication:**

1. **Use of standard rolled sections (e.g., HP bulb flats)** instead of built-up T-sections, because rolled sections require only single-pass fillet welds to the parent plate and reduce total weld length by 30–40 %.
2. **Alignment of block joints with shear-stress minima** in the hull girder, which minimises the fatigue penalty at assembly welds and allows the joint to be located in a region of structurally lower demand.

> [!TIP]
> A crisp, structured 3-mark answer should contain the definition, *and* two clearly numbered design choices. Avoid vague terms like *"good design"* — always name the specific feature.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State the formula for the **statistical tolerance** of an assembled hull built from $N$ blocks. Why is the statistical form preferred over the arithmetic worst-case form?

**Model Answer:**

$$\delta_{cum}^{stat} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block}$$

**Why statistical is preferred:** Because the per-block errors are independent and approximately normally distributed, their variances (not their magnitudes) add. The square-root-of-N growth is therefore far more realistic than the linear worst-case $\delta_{cum}^{wc} = N \cdot \delta_{block}$. For a 100-block hull with $\delta_{block} = \pm 5$ mm, the statistical tolerance is only **± 50 mm**, while the worst-case value would be an unworkable **± 500 mm**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each module carries a compulsory question of 14 marks with internal choice (A or B). The 14 marks are typically split as **7 + 7** between two sub-parts. Every sub-part below carries a complete, model solution.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Recommended Choice for Most Students*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain, with a suitable sketch or block diagram, how the **structural design of a ship hull** is influenced by the **prefabrication philosophy** adopted by a modern shipyard. Discuss at least **four** design decisions that are subordinated to the chosen block-construction strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VLCC is to be built in **96 blocks**. Each block is held to a length tolerance of $\pm 4 \, \text{mm}$ at the assembly edge. Compute (i) the cumulative statistical tolerance at the 95 % confidence level, and (ii) the worst-case tolerance. Comment on the practical significance of the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The structural design of a modern commercial ship is no longer an independent activity performed *before* production planning. Instead, the design office works in a tight loop with the production planning office and the classification society, in what is termed **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**.

**Four key design decisions subordinated to block construction:**

1. **Plate panel sizing** is chosen to fit the **standard shop plate** (typically 4 m × 12 m or 4 m × 15 m) and the **press capacity** of the panel shop. Plates smaller than 1.5 m² are avoided because the cutting-to-welding ratio becomes uneconomical.

2. **Stiffener orientation** is dictated by the **preferred welding position** within a block. In a flat-panel workstation (1G/2G), stiffeners are run longitudinally so the fillet welds are deposited down-hand. Skewed stiffeners that force 3G/4G welding are rejected even if they provide slightly better scantlings.

3. **Block joint locations** are placed at **shear-stress minima** in the hull girder to minimise fatigue at the assembly welds. For a VLCC, the shear minima typically occur at the engine-room forward bulkhead and the after-peak forward bulkhead.

4. **Sling-pad reinforcement** is built into the structure at the design stage. The designer must verify the local plate buckling strength under a sling load amplified by a **DAF of 1.25 to 2.0** — often the most severe in-plane compressive load the panel ever experiences.

A block diagram showing the **Design Office → Production Planning → Yard Execution → Class Surveyor** feedback loop (as in Section 4.2) should accompany this answer to secure full marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N = 96$ blocks, $\delta_{block} = \pm 4 \, \text{mm}$.

**(i) Statistical (RSS) tolerance at 95 % confidence:**

$$\delta_{cum}^{stat} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block} = \sqrt{96} \cdot 4 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\delta_{cum}^{stat} = 9.7979 \cdot 4 \approx \pm 39.2 \, \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Worst-case (arithmetic) tolerance:**

$$\delta_{cum}^{wc} = N \cdot \delta_{block} = 96 \cdot 4 = \pm 384 \, \text{mm}$$

**Comment:** The statistical tolerance ($\pm 39.2$ mm) is **less than 11 %** of the worst-case value. The ~10× reduction reflects the independence of per-block errors and is what makes the construction of a 300 m+ VLCC feasible within tight overall-length tolerances required by the classification society. If the yard had to work to the worst-case budget, the joint-design and shimming problem would become intractable.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **five pillars** of the relationship between structural design and prefabrication. For each pillar, give **one specific numerical metric** that a designer would use. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 750 t engine-room block is to be lifted by **four equal slings**, each making an angle of **35°** with the vertical, in a building dock. The crane operator specifies a **dynamic amplification factor of 1.40**. Calculate the **dynamic tension in each sling** and the **maximum in-plane compressive load on the parent plate** at the sling-pad location if the contact area is **600 mm × 600 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| # | Pillar | Specific Numerical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Block philosophy and sub-division** | Number of blocks: **50–250** for a commercial vessel |
| 2 | **Weld accessibility and position** | Fraction of welding in 1G/2G: target **> 70 %** |
| 3 | **Lifting geometry and sling loads** | Maximum block weight: **≤ 1,200 t**; DAF **1.25–2.0** |
| 4 | **Standardisation and series production** | Restricted catalogue of stiffener sections (e.g., **HP160 to HP380**) |
| 5 | **Tolerance stack-up and joint design** | Per-block tolerance: **± 3 to ± 6 mm**; statistical RSS budget |

(Other acceptable metrics: plate aspect ratio 2.0–3.5, man-hours per tonne 25–45 MH/t, steel consumption 0.020–0.035 t/CGT.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $M = 750 \times 10^3 \, \text{kg}$, $n = 4$ slings, $\theta = 35°$, $DAF = 1.40$, contact area $A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \, \text{m}^2$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Static tension per sling:**

$$T^{static} = \frac{M g}{n \cos \theta} = \frac{750 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 9.81}{4 \cdot \cos 35°} = \frac{7.3575 \cdot 10^6}{4 \cdot 0.8192}$$

$$T^{static} = \frac{7.3575 \cdot 10^6}{3.2767} \approx 2.245 \cdot 10^6 \, \text{N} \approx 2,245 \, \text{kN}$$

**Dynamic tension per sling:**

$$T^{dynamic} = DAF \cdot T^{static} = 1.40 \cdot 2,245 \approx 3,143 \, \text{kN}$$

**In-plane compressive load on parent plate (each sling pad):**

$$\sigma_{pad} = \frac{T^{dynamic}}{A} = \frac{3,143 \cdot 10^3}{0.36} \approx 8.73 \cdot 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 8.73 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Buckling check reminder:** The 750 t block at four sling pads carries $\sim$8.7 MPa compression on each pad. With $t = 22$ mm mild-steel plate and an unsupported doubler dimension of $\sim$300 mm, the slenderness $\beta \approx 300/22 \approx 13.6$, well below the typical limit of 30. Hence the doubler plate does **not** buckle elastically — but a **yielding check** at the pad edge (stress concentration factor $\sim$3) gives a peak of $\sim$26 MPa, still below the yield of 235 MPa for grade A steel, with a comfortable safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Quoting crane capacity as a static number** without multiplying by the dynamic factor 0.75–0.85. Always subtract rigging losses.
> 2. **Using the arithmetic worst-case tolerance** ($\delta_{cum} = N \cdot \delta_{block}$) instead of the statistical form ($\delta_{cum} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block}$). The examiner awards the *conceptual* mark only if you justify the square-root form.
> 3. **Confusing welding position numbers** — students often state *"3G is the best position"*. Memorise: 1G/1F (flat) is the best, 4G/4F (overhead) is the worst.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in sling-tension calculations. The answer must be in **N** or **kN** — never in kg-force or tonnes-force without an explicit conversion.
> 5. **Drawing block joints at convenient bulkhead locations** without referring to the **shear-stress field** of the hull girder.
> 6. **Skipping the justification** in design questions. A bare list of "advantages" scores 4–5 marks; a justified argument with a sketch and metric scores 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Definition:** Prefabrication = build the ship in *reverse* — blocks (300–1,200 t, 50–250 per ship) are assembled in workshops and erected at the site.
- ☐ **DFMA loop:** Design Office ↔ Production Planning ↔ Class Surveyor (this is the *defining feature* of modern shipbuilding).
- ☐ **Five Pillars:** (1) Block philosophy, (2) Weld access, (3) Lifting geometry, (4) Standardisation, (5) Tolerance.
- ☐ **Golden metric:** **> 70 % of welding in 1G/2G position.**
- ☐ **Sling rule:** $W_b \le W_{crane} \cdot \eta_{dyn}$ with $\eta_{dyn} = 0.75 - 0.85$ **and** $T_i = DAF \cdot Mg / (n \cos \theta)$ with $DAF = 1.25 - 2.0$.
- ☐ **Tolerance:** $\delta_{cum} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \delta_{block}$ (statistical, RSS), **not** $N \cdot \delta_{block}$ (arithmetic).
- ☐ **Plate panel size:** 4 m × 12 m or 4 m × 15 m standard; aspect ratio 2.0–3.5.
- ☐ **Standard stiffeners:** HP bulb flats catalogue (HP160 to HP380); avoid built-up T-sections when a rolled equivalent exists.
- ☐ **Block joint placement:** Always at **shear minima** in the hull girder to minimise fatigue penalty.
- ☐ **Productivity benchmarks:** 25–45 MH/t of steel; 0.020–0.035 t of steel per CGT.
- ☐ **Welding deposition rates to remember:** 1G ≈ 3–5 kg/h; 3G/4G ≈ 1.0–2.5 kg/h.
- ☐ **Trick question warning:** A "block count" of **less than 30** for a VLCC or **more than 300** for a container ship is *red-flag unrealistic* — always sanity-check.
- ☐ **Examiner's hot-button term:** *"Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)"* — use it at least once in every 14-mark answer to signal awareness of modern practice.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-mile revision tip (1 hour before exam):** Draw the *five-pillar block diagram* (Section 4.2) and the *tolerance stack-up* (Section 4.4) on a single A4 sheet. These two diagrams cover ~40 % of typical 14-mark questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplifications in Structural Design (Design for Welding)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simplifications in Structural Design (Design for Welding)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Simplifications in Structural Design (Design for Welding)** refers to the deliberate, rule-based modification of ship structural geometry, member intersections, plate thickness transitions, and joint configurations during the *design phase itself*, to make the structure amenable to efficient, defect-free, and economical welding fabrication. The simplifications are codified by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) and follow the principle of *Fabrication-Oriented Design (FOD)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Design simplifications are pre-emptive geometric and dimensional rationalizations incorporated at the drawing-board stage, intended to eliminate weld-induced defects, reduce fabrication man-hours, ensure weld accessibility, and minimize residual stresses, while preserving — or even enhancing — the structural strength of the ship."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

Imagine you are stitching two pieces of thick denim cloth by hand using a curved needle. If both pieces are *perfectly flat*, *aligned edge-to-edge*, and have *uniform thickness*, the stitch is fast, straight, and strong.

Now imagine the same two pieces — but one has a curved edge, a step in thickness, and a fold. The needle will skip, the thread will bunch, and the joint will tear easily.

**Welding is the needle-and-thread of shipbuilding.** The plate edges are the "cloth," and the welding torch is the "needle."

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Welding Design:**
> *"If the design is complicated, the weld is complicated. If the weld is complicated, the weld is weak."*

**Design for welding** therefore enforces three cardinal simplifications:
1. **Flatness** — avoid curved, twisted, or non-coplanar plates where possible.
2. **Symmetry** — make loads and members symmetric about a centerline.
3. **Accessibility** — leave enough space (typically **150 mm minimum**, but **50 mm** for tack welds) for the welder and electrode to physically reach the joint.

### 1.3 Why Simplifications Are a Board-Favorite Topic

| Reason | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| Reduces weld defects (porosity, undercut) | Higher fatigue life |
| Standardizes weld sizes | Lower fabrication cost (less NDT, less rework) |
| Allows mechanized/robotic welding | Faster throughput in the shipyard |
| Distributes stress uniformly | Eliminates triaxial stress concentrations |
| Simplifies fit-up and jigging | Reduces skilled-labour dependency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Shipyards:** A typical *minimum weld leg length* in primary hull structure is **6 mm**, while heavy plate butt welds (thickness $> 25$ mm) require **double-V or double-U** preparations to reduce weld volume. Plate thickness is standardized in **2 mm increments** up to 25 mm and **5 mm increments** beyond.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Simplification Process

**Step 1 — Identify the structural function.**
Every member in a ship has a *load path*. The designer must trace how forces (shear, bending, hydrostatic) travel from one member to the next.

**Step 2 — Check weldability of the proposed joint.**
Ask three questions:
- *Is the joint reachable by a welding torch?* (Accessibility Rule)
- *Will the heat from welding cause distortion?* (Distortion Rule)
- *Are the two plate edges of compatible metallurgy and thickness?* (Material Rule)

**Step 3 — Apply the simplification hierarchy.**
Classify the joint in descending order of preference:

$$\text{Butt Weld} \;\; > \;\; \text{Fillet Weld (T-joint)} \;\; > \;\; \text{Fillet Weld (Lap joint)} \;\; > \;\; \text{Plug/Slot Weld}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce transitions (scallops, tapers, brackets).**
Where abrupt changes in stiffness or geometry occur, the designer inserts *transitional elements* such as:
- **Sniped ends** on stiffeners
- **Scarf joints** (tapered transitions) for plate thickness changes
- **Soft-toe brackets** at the end of stiffener runs
- **Weld access holes** at intersecting members

**Step 5 — Validate using classification rules.**
Run the simplified design past the rule checks of the chosen classification society (e.g., IRS Rules for Hull, Chapter 5 — Welding).

### 2.2 The Six Pillars of Design Simplification for Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six principles appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question on this topic. Memorize them.

1. **Avoidance of Multi-Planar Intersections (The "Three-Plate Corner" Rule)**
   *Where three or more plates meet at a single point, design a weld access hole or a scallop of radius $\ge 50$ mm.*

2. **Plate Edge Preparation Standardization**
   *Use only IRS/DNV-approved edge preparations: Square, Single-V, Single-U, Double-V, Double-U, J-groove, or combination.*

3. **Symmetry of Weld Group About Neutral Axis**
   *Place welds symmetrically about the centroid to avoid transverse shrinkage-induced distortion.*

4. **Minimization of Weld Volume**
   *Choose a smaller groove angle (e.g., $50^{\circ}$ instead of $60^{\circ}$) wherever penetration allows. Less weld metal $\Rightarrow$ less heat input $\Rightarrow$ less distortion.*

5. **Use of Stiffener Snipes and Tapered Ends**
   *Replace square-cut stiffener ends with a $30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$ snipe over a length of $\ge 2 \times$ web depth.*

6. **Avoidance of Overlapping Welds and Crossovers**
   *Cross-welds form brittle heat-affected zone (HAZ) junctions. Stagger them by a minimum of $100$ mm.*

### 2.3 The Heat Input Equation (Critical for Distortion Control)

Heat input is the single most important parameter controlled by simplification choices:

$$H = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

Where:
- $H$ = heat input per unit length (kJ/mm)
- $V$ = arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $k$ = thermal efficiency factor (dimensionless):
  - $k = 0.6$ for **manual metal arc (MMA / SMAW)**
  - $k = 0.8$ for **submerged arc welding (SAW)**
  - $k = 0.7$ for **gas metal arc welding (GMAW / MIG/MAG)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplification Link:** By reducing weld volume (smaller groove, less weld metal), the required heat input drops. The designer indirectly controls $H$ by choosing *where* welds go and *how big* they are.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat input | $H = \dfrac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $k$ depends on process |
| 2 | Weld throat (fillet) | $t = 0.707 \cdot z$ | mm | $z$ = leg length |
| 3 | Minimum leg length | $z \ge 0.25 \cdot t_{\text{thinner}}$ | mm | For plating |
| 4 | Plate thickness transition | $\text{Taper} \le 1:4$ | ratio | Overlap $\ge 4 \times$ thickness diff. |
| 5 | Scallop radius | $r \ge 50$ mm | mm | At multi-plate corners |
| 6 | Snipe length | $L \ge 2 \cdot d$ | mm | $d$ = stiffener web depth |
| 7 | Snipe angle | $\theta = 30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$ | degrees | From horizontal |
| 8 | Min. weld access hole radius | $r \ge 25$ mm | mm | For plate $\le 12$ mm |
| 9 | Weld pitch (staggered) | $p \le 200$ mm or $15 \cdot t$ | mm | Whichever is smaller |
| 10 | Heat input limit (high-strength steel) | $H \le 2.0$ kJ/mm | kJ/mm | For EH36, AH40 grades |
| 11 | Preheating temperature | $T_{p} \ge 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C | For CE $\ge 0.45$ |
| 12 | Distortion angular change | $\Delta\theta \propto \dfrac{H \cdot t^2}{L^2}$ | rad | Empirical relation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $|H|$ or use vertical pipes for absolute value. In KTU answer sheets, the *vertical bar* `|` is fine, but in **this markdown table** I have written formulas without absolute-value symbols to preserve table syntax. In your exam copy, write `$H$` cleanly.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

These simplifications are not academic. They govern every ship ever built. For example:
- In the **Forrestal-class aircraft carrier** (US Navy), a single design simplification of eliminating one tri-planar intersection saved an estimated **2,400 man-hours** of welding.
- Modern **double-hull tankers** (post-MARPOL) use standardized **bracket-less** connections that rely entirely on simplified, symmetric fillet welds.
- **LNG carriers** (moss-type and membrane-type) cannot tolerate weld defects because of cryogenic service; their designs are the *epitome* of simplification philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Fillet Welds Are Simplified to Symmetric Double-Sided Welds

**Statement:** Show that a symmetric double-sided fillet weld causes zero net angular distortion, while a single-sided fillet weld causes angular distortion proportional to heat input and plate thickness squared.

**Starting Premise:**
Welding shrinkage force acts transverse to the weld axis. The arc-heated zone contracts as it cools, generating a contraction strain $\varepsilon_s$ in the weld metal.

$$\varepsilon_s = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion ($\approx 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for steel)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature drop from solidus to ambient

**Step 1 — Compute the contraction force in a single-sided fillet weld.**

The contraction force $F$ in a weld of throat $t$, length $L$, and elastic modulus $E$ is:

$$F = E \cdot \varepsilon_s \cdot A = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Compute the moment arm causing angular distortion.**

For a single-sided fillet weld on a plate of width $b$ and thickness $T$, the moment arm about the plate's neutral axis is approximately $b/2$ (for an edge weld) or $b/6$ (for a centered weld). Taking the conservative case:

$$M = F \cdot \dfrac{b}{2} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate to elastic bending moment.**

$$M = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot \Delta\theta}{L}$$

Where the second moment of area of the plate section is:

$$I = \dfrac{T^{3} \cdot L}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for angular distortion $\Delta\theta$.**

Equating Step 2 and Step 3:

$$E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{b}{2} = \dfrac{E \cdot T^{3} \cdot L \cdot \Delta\theta}{12 \cdot L}$$

Canceling $E$ and $L$:

$$\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{b}{2} = \dfrac{T^{3} \cdot \Delta\theta}{12}$$

Solving for $\Delta\theta$:

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{12 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot b}{2 \cdot T^{3}} = \dfrac{6 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot b}{T^{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting numerical values for typical mild steel.**

For mild steel:
- $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $\Delta T = 800$ °C (approximate drop from $800^{\circ}$C to ambient $20^{\circ}$C)
- $t = 6$ mm (fillet throat)
- $b = 500$ mm (plate width)
- $T = 12$ mm (plate thickness)

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{6 \cdot (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 800 \cdot 6 \cdot 500}{12^{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 6 \cdot (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 800 \cdot 6 \cdot 500$$

$$= 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 800 \cdot 6 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 800 \cdot 3000 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 2{,}400{,}000 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 2.4 = 167.04$$

So numerator $= 167.04 \times 10^{0} = 167.04$ (in consistent units).

**Step 7 — Compute the denominator.**

$$12^{3} = 1728$$

**Step 8 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{167.04}{1728} \approx 0.0967 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\Delta\theta \approx 0.0967 \times \dfrac{180^{\circ}}{\pi} \approx 5.54^{\circ}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result.**

A single-sided fillet weld of leg $6$ mm on a $12$ mm plate causes approximately **$5.5^{\circ}$** of angular distortion — this is *unacceptable* in a ship's flat plate panel.

**Step 10 — Apply the simplification: Use double-sided symmetric fillet welds.**

If two fillet welds are placed symmetrically (one each side of the plate), the moments from the two welds cancel:

$$M_{\text{net}} = M_{1} - M_{2} = 0 \quad \text{(when welds are identical and symmetric)}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{symmetric}} = 0 \quad \text{(theoretically)}$$

In practice, residual distortion is reduced by **80–90%**, which is the *engineering justification* for the design simplification rule of symmetric welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:**
> The dominant term is $T^{3}$ in the denominator. This means **plate thickness is the most powerful design lever** — a $20\%$ increase in thickness reduces distortion by a factor of roughly $1.7$. This justifies the design rule of *using slightly thicker plates to permit thinner welds*, an important fabrication trade-off in ship design.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Simplification vs. Risk Avoided

| Simplification Action | Risk Avoided | Defect Prevented | Classification Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weld access hole at tri-planar corner | Cracking at weld intersection | Lamellar tearing, crater cracks | IRS Pt 3, Ch 5 |
| Stiffener snipe (30°) | Stress concentration at cut-off | Fatigue crack initiation | Lloyd's Ch 4, Sec 3 |
| Scarf transition (1:4) | Notch effect at thickness change | Brittle fracture | DNV Pt 3, Ch 4 |
| Symmetric double fillet | Angular distortion | Plate buckling, alignment error | ABS Pt 2, Ch 3 |
| Staggered weld pitch (p ≤ 200 mm) | Heat accumulation | HAZ softening, grain growth | BV Pt B, Ch 5 |
| Avoid lap joints in primary | Crevice corrosion, fatigue | Plate thinning, cracking | IRS Pt 3, Ch 5 |
| Standardized edge prep (V/J) | Incomplete fusion | Lack of penetration | All major societies |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Decision Tree for Joint Simplification (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Joint Required] --> B{Plates Coplanar?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Equal Thickness?}
    B -- No --> D[Add Scallop or Bracket]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Square Butt Weld]
    C -- No --> F[Taper 1 colon 4 over 4x diff]
    F --> G[Use Double V Groove]
    A --> H{Load Path Symmetric?}
    H -- No --> I[Add Doubling Plate]
    H -- Yes --> J[Use Symmetric Fillet Welds]
    A --> K{3+ Plates Meet?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Cut Weld Access Hole radius 50mm]
    K -- No --> M[Proceed with Standard Weld]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every box represents a decision node. The arrows are routed to the *simplest possible* weld configuration. This is the *fabrication-oriented design* philosophy in graphical form.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Welding Design Simplification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Structural Drawing] --> B[Identify Joints]
    B --> C{Joint Type}
    C --> D[Butt Joint]
    C --> E[Fillet Joint]
    C --> F[Lap Joint]
    D --> D1[Square Prep]
    D --> D2[Single V Prep]
    D --> D3[Double V Prep]
    E --> E1[T Joint symmetric]
    E --> E2[Corner Joint]
    F --> F1[Reject in Primary]
    F --> F2[Allow in Secondary]
    D1 --> G[Apply Heat Input H formula]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{Within H limit}
    H -- Yes --> I[Approve Design]
    H -- No --> J[Modify Groove Angle]
    J --> G
    I --> K[Generate WPS and PQR]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Cross-Section of Stiffener Snipe (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cross_Section_View
        nodeA[Main Plate 12mm]
        nodeB[Stiffener Web 8mm x 200mm]
        nodeC[Sniped End 30 deg cut]
        nodeD[Weld Fillet 6mm]
        nodeE[Soft Toe Region]
    end
    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The sniped end of the stiffener is cut at $30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$ over a length $\ge 2d$ (twice the web depth). The fillet weld terminates at a "soft toe" — a region of gradually reducing stress concentration. The triangular zone labelled "Soft Toe Region" is the *destination of the simplification*: stress that would have peaked at a square corner now ramps down smoothly.

### 4.4 Diagram D — Multi-Plate Corner Showing Weld Access Hole

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Shell Plate] --> nodeB[Corner Intersection]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Deck Plate]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Bulkhead Plate]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Bracket Plate]
    nodeF[Weld Access Hole r 50mm]
    nodeB --> nodeF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Hole?** The hole removes the four-plate intersection point. The welder can now lay continuous fillet welds along *each pair* of plates, eliminating the notorious "weld crossover" defect zone. After welding, the hole is *not* closed — it remains as a stress-relief feature and is permitted by all major classification societies.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define "Design for Welding" in the context of ship structural design. List any two simplifications commonly adopted.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
"Design for Welding" is the practice of modifying the geometry, layout, and detailing of ship structural components during the design stage itself, to facilitate efficient, defect-free, and economical welded fabrication. It is also termed *Fabrication-Oriented Design (FOD)*.

**Two Simplifications (1.5 marks):**
1. **Weld access holes** at multi-plate corners to eliminate weld crossovers.
2. **Stiffener snipes** at cut-off points to reduce stress concentration and fatigue crack initiation.
3. (Optional) **Symmetric double fillet welds** to cancel angular distortion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "simplification means *simpler drawings*." Examiner expects *fabrication-level* simplifications. Avoid the generic answer; use ship-specific terms like *snipe, scallop, access hole, scarf joint, bracket knee*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the heat input formula for arc welding. What is the value of the thermal efficiency factor $k$ for submerged arc welding (SAW)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$H = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes
- $v$ = travel speed in mm/min
- $k$ = thermal efficiency factor

**SAW value (1 mark):** $k = 0.8$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Module-3 Choice Question:**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **six key simplifications** adopted in ship structural design to facilitate welding. State the corresponding classification rule (or geometry parameter) for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-sided fillet weld of leg length **$z = 7$ mm** is deposited on a **$T = 14$ mm** thick plate of width **$b = 600$ mm**. The plate material is mild steel with $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C and $\Delta T = 800$ °C. Calculate the angular distortion $\Delta\theta$ in degrees. If the designer replaces this with **symmetric double-sided fillet welds**, what is the *theoretical* new distortion? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
*(Allocate 1 mark per simplification with rule. Sixth simplification carries 1 mark for grouping.)*

1. **Weld access holes** at multi-plate corners — radius $\ge 50$ mm. (IRS Pt 3, Ch 5)
2. **Stiffener snipe** at cut-offs — angle $30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$, length $\ge 2d$. (Lloyd's Ch 4, Sec 3)
3. **Scarf transition** for thickness changes — taper $\le 1:4$. (DNV Pt 3, Ch 4)
4. **Symmetric fillet welds** on both sides of plate. (ABS Pt 2, Ch 3)
5. **Staggered intermittent welds** — pitch $p \le 200$ mm. (BV Pt B, Ch 5)
6. **Avoidance of lap joints** in primary hull structure. (All societies, primary scantlings)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute throat $t$ from leg $z$:

$$t = 0.707 \cdot z = 0.707 \cdot 7 = 4.949 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the angular distortion formula derived in Section 3:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{6 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \cdot b}{T^{3}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{6 \cdot (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 800 \cdot 4.949 \cdot 600}{14^{3}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator:

$$6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 800 \cdot 4.949 \cdot 600 = 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 800 \cdot 2969.4$$
$$= 6 \cdot 11.6 \cdot 2{,}375{,}520 = 6 \cdot 27{,}556{,}032 = 165{,}336{,}192$$

So numerator $\approx 1.653 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{-6} = 165.336$.

**Step 5:** Denominator:

$$14^{3} = 2744$$

**Step 6:** Result:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{165.336}{2744} \approx 0.0603 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to degrees:

$$\Delta\theta \approx 0.0603 \times \frac{180^{\circ}}{\pi} \approx 0.0603 \times 57.296 \approx 3.45^{\circ}$$

**Step 8:** For symmetric double-sided fillet welds of equal leg length, the moments cancel, giving:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{symmetric}} = 0 \;\; \text{(theoretically)}$$

**Step 9:** Conclusion:
The single-sided weld causes $\approx 3.45^{\circ}$ of distortion, which exceeds the IRS-allowable $2^{\circ}$ tolerance. The design simplification of using symmetric double-sided welds is therefore **mandatory** for this plate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, explain the concept of a **weld access hole** and a **stiffener snipe**. State the standard dimensional limits. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A butt weld in a $20$ mm thick EH36 grade plate is to be deposited by SAW at $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 400$ mm/min. Given $k = 0.8$, compute the heat input $H$. Is it within the recommended $2.0$ kJ/mm limit for EH36? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Weld Access Hole (3.5 marks):**
A *weld access hole* (also called *scallop*) is a circular cut-out made at the intersection of three or more plates to:
- Remove the tri-planar stress concentration point.
- Provide physical access for the welding electrode.
- Eliminate weld crossover defects.

**Dimensional limit:** Radius $r \ge 50$ mm; for plate $\le 12$ mm, $r \ge 25$ mm is acceptable.

**Stiffener Snipe (3.5 marks):**
A *sniped end* is the tapered (cut at an angle) termination of a stiffener where it meets a primary member (e.g., a deck plate or shell plate). It:
- Reduces the stress concentration factor from $\sim 3.0$ (square cut) to $\sim 1.5$ (tapered cut).
- Provides a "soft toe" for the terminating fillet weld.

**Dimensional limits:**
- Snipe angle: $30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$ from horizontal.
- Snipe length $L \ge 2d$, where $d$ = stiffener web depth.

*(Sketch should show: a) shell plate, b) intersecting stiffener, c) triangular sniped region, d) terminating fillet weld labelled with leg length.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify the formula:

$$H = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
- $k = 0.8$
- $V = 32$ V
- $I = 600$ A
- $v = 400$ mm/min

$$H = \frac{0.8 \cdot 32 \cdot 600}{400 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$0.8 \cdot 32 \cdot 600 = 25.6 \cdot 600 = 15{,}360$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$400 \times 1000 = 400{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Compute ratio:

$$H = \frac{15{,}360}{400{,}000} = 0.0384 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 6:** Compare to limit $H_{\max} = 2.0$ kJ/mm:

$$H = 0.0384 \;\; < \;\; 2.0 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 7:** Conclusion:
**Yes, the heat input is well within the limit.** In fact, it is significantly *under* the limit, indicating either a fast travel speed or low energy setting. The designer may need to verify that adequate fusion is being achieved at such low heat input.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Unit error:** Forgetting the factor of $1000$ in the denominator is the most common mistake — it gives an answer off by a factor of $1000$.
> 2. **Process confusion:** $k = 0.6$ for MMAW, **$0.8$ for SAW**, $0.7$ for MIG/MAG. Mixing up gives incorrect heat input.
> 3. **Heat input limit:** The $2.0$ kJ/mm cap applies *only* to high-strength quenched and tempered steels (EH36, AH40). For ordinary mild steel (Grade A), the limit is typically $4.0$–$5.0$ kJ/mm.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch:** In Part (a), marks are reserved *specifically* for the neat sketch. A 3.5-mark answer without a labelled sketch loses 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Box.**

- **Definition:** Simplifications in structural design for welding = *pre-emptive geometric rationalizations* to enable defect-free, economical welded fabrication.
- **Six Key Simplifications:** (1) Weld access holes, (2) Sniped stiffener ends, (3) Scarf thickness transitions, (4) Symmetric double fillet welds, (5) Staggered intermittent welds, (6) Avoidance of lap joints in primary structure.
- **Standard Dimensions:** Scallop $r \ge 50$ mm; Snipe $L \ge 2d$, $\theta = 30^{\circ}$–$45^{\circ}$; Plate transition taper $\le 1:4$.
- **Heat Input Formula:** $H = \dfrac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm.
- **Thermal Efficiency:** MMAW $k = 0.6$, GMAW $k = 0.7$, SAW $k = 0.8$.
- **Fillet Throat:** $t = 0.707 \cdot z$.
- **Heat Input Limit (HSLA/EH36):** $H \le 2.0$ kJ/mm.
- **Distortion Relation:** $\Delta\theta \propto \dfrac{t \cdot b}{T^{3}}$ — plate thickness cubed is the dominant parameter.
- **Why Symmetric Welds Work:** Equal and opposite moments cancel, giving $\Delta\theta \approx 0$.
- **Weld Hierarchy (Best to Worst):** Butt $>$ Fillet T $>$ Fillet Lap $>$ Plug/Slot.
- **Cross-Weld Rule:** Never let two welds cross; stagger by $\ge 100$ mm.
- **Minimum Fillet Leg:** $z \ge 0.25 \cdot t_{\text{thinner}}$.
- **Common Examiners:** IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV — all enforce nearly identical simplification rules.
- **Real-World Impact:** Symmetric welding cuts distortion by 80–90%; access holes eliminate the most common weld defect (crossover cracks).
- **Always draw a sketch** in Part B — it is *weighted marks*, not optional.
- **Always carry the $1000$ in the denominator** of the heat input equation.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Simplifications in Structural Design (Design for Welding)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
