topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC)** in shipbuilding is the operational, engineering, and managerial system of planned activities, inspection procedures, tests, and evaluations employed at every stage of design, material procurement, fabrication, assembly, launching, and sea trials to ensure that the constructed vessel conforms to the specified **technical specifications, classification society rules, statutory regulations, and customer contract requirements**.

In the context of the **2024 KTU Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** for *PENST632 – Shipbuilding Technology*, Quality Control is not a single activity but a **continuous chain of verified gates** that a ship must pass through, from the moment a steel plate enters the stockyard until the vessel is delivered to the owner. It is the practical arm of the broader **Quality Assurance (QA)** philosophy.

The governing reference documents for any shipbuilding QC program are:

- **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, Indian Register of Shipping — IRS)
- **ISO 9001:2015** — Quality Management Systems
- **ISO 17025** — Competence of Testing and Calibration Laboratories
- **AWS D1.1 / AWS D1.6** — Structural Welding Codes
- **SOLAS, MARPOL, IACS** — Statutory and unified requirements

## 1.2 The Distinction: Quality Assurance (QA) vs Quality Control (QC)

| Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | System / Process oriented | Product / Output oriented |
| Nature | Preventive | Detective / Corrective |
| Scope | Entire organization / project lifecycle | Specific stages of production |
| Tools | Quality manuals, audits, procedures | Inspection, testing, gauges |
| Question Asked | *"Are we building it the right way?"* | *"Did we build it right?"* |
| Documentation | Quality Plan, ITP, manuals | Inspection reports, NDT reports, test certificates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** QA = **A**udit-driven (process); QC = **C**heck-driven (product).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Patient" Intuition

Imagine building a ship as analogous to **treating a patient in a hospital**:

- The patient walks in (raw steel arrives) → **Blood tests and X-rays** are done (**Incoming material QC** — MTC verification, dimensional check).
- During the operation (welding/fabrication) → **Vitals are continuously monitored** (**In-process QC** — visual, NDT during construction).
- After surgery (block assembly/erection) → **Post-operative checks** (**Final QC** — dimensional survey, NDT, hydrostatic test).
- Before discharge (launching/sea trials) → **Final fitness certificate** (**Commissioning QC** — inclining experiment, dock/sea trials).

If any stage is skipped or the readings are abnormal, the doctor (QC Inspector / Classification Surveyor) raises a **NCR (Non-Conformance Report)** — analogous to flagging a defect for rework.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Tolerances (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical QC Metrics in Shipbuilding:**
> - **Dimensional Tolerance for Plate Flatness:** typically $\pm 2 \text{ mm}$ per meter length on critical hull plates.
> - **Weld Bead Throat Thickness:** as per AWS D1.1, minimum throat $\geq 0.707 \times \text{leg size}$.
> - **NDT Acceptance Level:** Class A, B, C defects as per **IACS UR Z17 / Z23**.
> - **Statistical Process Control Limits:** $\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$, $\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ (the famous **"Three-Sigma"** rule).

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Shipbuilding QC

```
                ┌─────────────────────────┐
                │   QUALITY  CONTROL      │
                │   (In Shipbuilding)     │
                └────────────┬────────────┘
                             │
       ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
       │                     │                     │
┌──────▼──────┐       ┌──────▼──────┐       ┌──────▼──────┐
│  PILLAR 1   │       │  PILLAR 2   │       │  PILLAR 3   │
│  Material   │       │  Process    │       │  Product    │
│  QC         │       │  QC         │       │  QC         │
│ (Incoming)  │       │(Fabrication)│       │ (Final)     │
└─────────────┘       └─────────────┘       └─────────────┘
```

This tri-pillar structure will be expanded in subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shipbuilding QC Workflow — Logic Layered

Quality Control in shipbuilding is implemented as a **stage-gated workflow**. Each stage has defined **Inspection and Test Plans (ITP)** and **Hold Points** that must be cleared by the classification surveyor before proceeding.

### Stage 1: Design QC (Pre-Construction)
- Approval of **lines plan, general arrangement, structural drawings** by classification society.
- **Rule compliance check** — scantlings (plate thickness, stiffener sizes) verified against CSR (Common Structural Rules) for tankers/bulk carriers, or respective society rules.
- **Fatigue and FEA analysis** review.

### Stage 2: Material Procurement QC (Incoming)
- Verification of **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** — chemical composition, mechanical properties (yield strength, tensile strength, elongation, Charpy impact).
- **Dimensional check** on plates and sections.
- **Surface condition** — absence of excessive rust, laminations, scabs.

### Stage 3: Fabrication QC (In-Process)
- **Cutting QC:** Edge preparation, bevel angle (typically **30° ± 5°**), land thickness.
- **Fit-up QC:** Root gap, misalignment, tack weld quality.
- **Welding QC:** WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) compliance, PQR (Procedure Qualification Record), Welder Qualification.
- **NDT during fabrication:** Visual Testing (VT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MT), Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT).

### Stage 4: Assembly / Block Construction QC
- **Dimensional survey** — block flatness, squareness, twist.
- **NDT of welded joints** — full or percentage basis (often **10–20%** of butt welds are RT'd, **100%** UT of critical seams).
- **Coating QC** — surface preparation (Sa 2.5 blast), DFT (Dry Film Thickness) check, holiday detection.

### Stage 5: Erection / Outfitting QC
- Hull alignment, block-to-block joint inspection.
- **Pipe line hydrostatic / pneumatic testing**.
- **Machinery bedplate alignment**, shaft alignment.
- **Electrical continuity and insulation resistance** checks.

### Stage 6: Pre-Launching QC
- **Hull integrity survey**.
- **Tank air / hydrostatic tests** for tightness.
- **Stability booklet verification**, inclining experiment preparation.

### Stage 7: Launching and Sea Trials QC
- **Launching strain measurements**.
- **Sea trial performance**: speed, maneuverability, vibration, noise, fuel consumption.
- **Final NDT and as-built documentation**.

## 2.2 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — Theory of Control Charts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern shipyards, especially those with **Series Production (e.g., bulk carriers, container ships)**, SQC is applied to repetitive processes such as plate cutting, welding parameters, and coating thickness.

The **Shewhart Control Chart** is the foundational tool. Two types:
1. **Variables Charts** (for measurable data): $\bar{X}$–chart, R-chart.
2. **Attributes Charts** (for pass/fail data): p-chart, c-chart, np-chart.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper Control Limit for X-bar chart | Welding bead width monitoring |
| 2 | $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower Control Limit for X-bar chart | Welding bead width monitoring |
| 3 | $\text{UCL}_{R} = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper Control Limit for Range chart | Plate thickness variation |
| 4 | $\text{LCL}_{R} = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower Control Limit for Range chart | Plate thickness variation |
| 5 | $\text{UCL}_{p} = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Proportion defective upper limit | Weld defect rate per 100 joints |
| 6 | $\text{UCL}_{c} = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Defects per unit upper limit | Coating pinholes per m² |
| 7 | $\text{Cp} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability index | Plate length cutting accuracy |
| 8 | $\text{Cpk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (centered) | Hull plate flatness vs spec |
| 9 | $\text{LTPD} = \text{AQL} \cdot \dfrac{1 - P_a}{P_a - \alpha}$ | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | Sampling plan design for NDT |
| 10 | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_d \cdot N \cdot p}{N - (N-1)p}$ | Average Outgoing Quality | Final delivery defect rate |
| 11 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Sample standard deviation | NDT signal processing |
| 12 | $A_2, D_3, D_4$ | Constants from control chart table | Based on sample size $n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants for $n = 5$ (most common in shipyard sampling):**
> $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

## 2.3 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — Engineering Utility

| NDT Method | Physical Principle | Detects | Used For (Ship) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VT (Visual Testing)** | Eye + aids | Surface defects | All welds, initial check |
| **MT (Magnetic Particle)** | Magnetic flux leakage | Surface/near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic | Welded joints, castings |
| **PT / DPT (Dye Penetrant)** | Capillary action | Surface-open defects | Non-magnetic alloys (SS, Al) |
| **UT (Ultrasonic Testing)** | High-frequency sound reflection | Internal defects, laminations | Butt welds, plate quality |
| **RT (Radiographic Testing)** | X-ray / γ-ray absorption | Internal volumetric defects | Critical butt welds |
| **ET (Eddy Current)** | Electromagnetic induction | Surface defects in conductive mat. | Heat exchanger tubes |
| **ACFM (Alternating Current Field Measurement)** | Electromagnetic | Surface cracks underwater | Hull in-service inspection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quality Control in shipbuilding directly determines:
1. **Vessel safety at sea** — a single missed crack in a hatch coaming can cause catastrophic failure.
2. **Insurance premiums** — class-approved vessels get better coverage.
3. **Cargo worthiness** — oil tankers require IMO-class approved welding.
4. **Shipyard reputation and re-order rate.**
5. **Compliance with MARPOL Annex VI** (e.g., coating compliance reduces friction emissions).

---

## 2.5 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Three-Sigma Rule

**Why $3\sigma$?** — In a normal distribution, $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ captures **99.73%** of the population. The probability of a point falling outside these limits purely by chance is only **0.27%** — extremely rare. So, if a measurement crosses these limits, it is almost certainly due to an **assignable cause** (tool wear, wrong setting, bad material) — triggering investigation.

**How it's used:** Every welding station records bead width every hour. The QC engineer plots these on an $\bar{X}$-chart. If a point exceeds UCL, the line is stopped, the cause investigated, and only restarted after corrective action.

> [!TIP]
> A **Cp ≥ 1.33** is generally accepted as "capable process" in shipbuilding; **Cpk ≥ 1.33** means the process is also **centered**. Class societies often require evidence of Cpk for series production approval.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Control Limits for the X-bar Chart (Variables Data)

### Given
A shipyard is monitoring the **plate length after CNC cutting**. The nominal length is **L = 6000 mm**, with USL = 6005 mm and LSL = 5995 mm.

Samples of size $n = 5$ are taken every hour. Data from $k = 5$ samples:

| Sample | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $X_5$ | $\bar{X}$ | $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6000 | 6001 | 5999 | 6000 | 6000 | 6000.0 | 2 |
| 2 | 6001 | 6000 | 6002 | 6000 | 6001 | 6000.8 | 2 |
| 3 | 5999 | 6000 | 6000 | 6001 | 6000 | 6000.0 | 2 |
| 4 | 6002 | 6001 | 6000 | 6001 | 6000 | 6000.8 | 2 |
| 5 | 6000 | 6000 | 6001 | 6002 | 6000 | 6000.6 | 2 |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k} \\
&= \frac{6000.0 + 6000.8 + 6000.0 + 6000.8 + 6000.6}{5} \\
&= \frac{30002.2}{5} \\
&= 6000.44 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average range $\bar{R}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k} \\
&= \frac{2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2}{5} \\
&= 2.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the constant $A_2$ for sample size $n = 5$:**

From standard control chart tables:

$$
A_2 = 0.577
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Upper and Lower Control Limits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
&= 6000.44 + (0.577)(2.00) \\
&= 6000.44 + 1.154 \\
&= 6001.594 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\
&= 6000.44 - (0.577)(2.00) \\
&= 6000.44 - 1.154 \\
&= 5999.286 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

All five sample means ($6000.0$, $6000.8$, $6000.0$, $6000.8$, $6000.6$) lie between 5999.286 and 6001.594 mm. **The process is in statistical control.**

The Specification Limits are USL = 6005 mm and LSL = 5995 mm. Since the natural process spread ($2 \times 1.154 = 2.308$ mm) is much tighter than the specification spread ($10$ mm), the process easily meets spec — **the shipyard's CNC cutter is well-tuned.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index (Cp)

### Given
From the above, we can estimate the process standard deviation using:

$$
\sigma \approx \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{2.00}{2.326} \\
&= 0.860 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Compute Cp:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cp} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \frac{6005 - 5995}{6 \times 0.860} \\
&= \frac{10}{5.16} \\
&= 1.938
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **Cp = 1.938 > 1.33**, the process is **highly capable**.

### Compute Cpk (accounting for centering):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cpk} &= \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\left(\frac{6005 - 6000.44}{3 \times 0.860}, \frac{6000.44 - 5995}{3 \times 0.860}\right) \\
&= \min\left(\frac{4.56}{2.58}, \frac{5.44}{2.58}\right) \\
&= \min(1.767, 2.109) \\
&= 1.767
\end{aligned}
$$

Both Cp and Cpk indicate **excellent process capability** — well within KTU board expectations for shipyard precision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Defects Per Unit Chart (c-chart) for Coating

### Given
A ship's hull anti-fouling coating is inspected for pinholes per $m^2$ using a **holiday detector**. Across 25 zones of $1 \, m^2$, total pinholes = 75.

**Step 1 — Compute average defects per unit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{c} &= \frac{\text{Total defects}}{\text{Number of units}} \\
&= \frac{75}{25} \\
&= 3.0 \text{ pinholes}/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute UCL and LCL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_c &= \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} \\
&= 3.0 + 3\sqrt{3.0} \\
&= 3.0 + 3(1.732) \\
&= 3.0 + 5.196 \\
&= 8.196 \text{ pinholes}/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LCL}_c &= \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} \\
&= 3.0 - 5.196 \\
&= -2.196 \rightarrow \text{set to } 0 \text{ (since } c \geq 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

If any zone exceeds 8.196 pinholes/$m^2$, the coating process is **out of control** — triggering rework. The negative LCL is set to 0 because defect counts cannot be negative.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which QC Tool

| Problem Type in Shipyard | Data Type | Best Statistical Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weld bead width | Continuous (mm) | $\bar{X}$-R chart | Direct measurement |
| Coating pinholes | Count (per $m^2$) | c-chart | Rare events, fixed area |
| Welds passed/failed | Pass-Fail (per lot) | p-chart | Variable lot size |
| Plate tensile strength | Continuous (MPa) | Capability (Cp, Cpk) | vs spec limits |
| NDT acceptance sampling | Binary | OC curve, AQL/LTPD | Class society sampling |
| Hull plate flatness deviation | Continuous | Cusum chart | Small drift detection |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve Logic

When a classification surveyor samples $n = 50$ welded joints from a lot of $N = 1000$ and accepts the lot if $d \leq 1$ defect (Acceptance Number = 1):

The probability of acceptance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= P(X \leq 1) = \sum_{k=0}^{1} \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an actual lot fraction defective $p = 0.02$ (2%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= (1-p)^n + n p (1-p)^{n-1} \\
&= (0.98)^{50} + 50(0.02)(0.98)^{49} \\
&= 0.3642 + 50(0.02)(0.3715) \\
&= 0.3642 + 0.3715 \\
&= 0.7357
\end{aligned}
$$

So if **2% of welds are actually defective**, this sampling plan will accept the lot **73.57% of the time** — informing the yard about the risk of the chosen plan.

## 4.4 Statistical Quality Control — Process State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InControl: All points within UCL and LCL
    InControl --> OutControl: Point beyond 3 sigma
    InControl --> Trend: 7 consecutive points trending up or down
    InControl --> Run: 7 consecutive points on one side of mean
    OutControl --> Investigate: Identify assignable cause
    Trend --> Investigate
    Run --> Investigate
    Investigate --> Corrective: Apply corrective action
    Corrective --> InControl: Recalculate limits and resume
    Corrective --> OutControl: Repeat root cause analysis
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always remember to label **axes (X-axis = sample number, Y-axis = measured value)** and **limits (UCL, CL, LCL)** when drawing any control chart in the exam.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define Quality Control in the context of shipbuilding. How does it differ from Quality Assurance?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Quality Control (QC)** in shipbuilding is the systematic set of operational techniques and inspection activities used to verify that the constructed vessel and its components conform to the design specifications, classification society rules, and contractual requirements.

**Difference from QA:**

| Parameter | Quality Control | Quality Assurance |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Product verification | Process management |
| Approach | Detective | Preventive |
| Tools | Inspection, NDT, testing | Audits, procedures, training |

QC is the **"checking" arm**, while QA is the **"system building" arm** of the quality function.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for comparison]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any six Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in ship construction. State one defect each method can detect.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — surface cracks, misalignment
2. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — surface/near-surface cracks in steel
3. **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT/DPT)** — surface-open cracks in stainless steel/aluminum
4. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — internal laminations, weld porosity, lack of fusion
5. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — internal volumetric defects (slag, porosity)
6. **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** — surface cracks in heat exchanger tubes

**[3 Marks: 0.5 each for method + defect]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Attempt either full Question A or full Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the stages of Quality Control in shipbuilding from design approval to delivery, with emphasis on NDT and welding inspection.**

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Design QC:**
The yard submits drawings to the **classification society** (e.g., IRS, DNV). Approval ensures scantlings (plate thickness, frame spacing) satisfy CSR rules.

**Stage 2 — Material QC:**
- **MTC verification** of steel grade (e.g., AH36, DH36).
- **Dimensional check** of plates against purchase order.
- **UT scan** of plates for laminations.

**Stage 3 — Fabrication QC:**
- **Cutting QC:** Bevel angle 30° ± 5°.
- **Fit-up QC:** Root gap 2–4 mm, misalignment ≤ 3 mm.
- **Welding QC:** WPS must specify parameters (current, voltage, speed, heat input). Heat input typically **0.8–2.0 kJ/mm** for shipbuilding steel.

$$
Q = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min).

**Stage 4 — NDT Inspection:**
- **Visual Testing (VT)** — 100% of all welds.
- **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — at least 10% of fillet welds.
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — 100% of critical butt welds (per IACS UR Z17).
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — spot check at 5–10%.

**Stage 5 — Final QC:**
Hull survey, sea trial, and classification survey for issue of **Class Certificate**.

**[7 Marks distributed: 1 design + 1 material + 2 fabrication/welding + 2 NDT + 1 final]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define Cp and Cpk. A shipyard cuts steel plates of nominal length 6000 mm with USL = 6003 mm and LSL = 5997 mm. From 25 samples of size 5, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 6000.1$ mm and $\bar{R} = 1.2$ mm. Calculate Cp, Cpk and comment on the process capability. Given $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Cp (Process Capability):** Measures the ability of a process to produce output within specification limits, assuming the process is centered. $Cp = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$
- **Cpk (Process Capability Index):** Measures process capability **accounting for centering**. $Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$

**Step 1 — Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{1.2}{2.326} = 0.516 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cp} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \frac{6003 - 5997}{6 \times 0.516} \\
&= \frac{6}{3.096} \\
&= 1.938
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cpk} &= \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\left(\frac{6003 - 6000.1}{3 \times 0.516}, \frac{6000.1 - 5997}{3 \times 0.516}\right) \\
&= \min\left(\frac{2.9}{1.548}, \frac{3.1}{1.548}\right) \\
&= \min(1.873, 2.003) \\
&= 1.873
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
Both **Cp = 1.938** and **Cpk = 1.873** are well above the **minimum acceptable value of 1.33** for a capable process. The process is **highly capable** and well-centered, with Cpk being only marginally lower than Cp, indicating the process mean (6000.1) is very close to the target (6000).

**[7 Marks: 1 def + 1 σ calc + 2 Cp + 2 Cpk + 1 comment]**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of Classification Societies in shipbuilding quality control. Name any four major classification societies and explain IACS.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Classification Societies:**

A classification society is an independent, non-governmental organization that establishes and maintains technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships. Their roles include:

1. **Rule-making:** Develops technical rules for ship design and construction (scantlings, welding, materials, machinery).
2. **Approval of drawings and plans.**
3. **Survey during construction:** Class surveyors attend the yard and witness tests, inspect, and sign off at hold points.
4. **Issuance of Class Certificate** — required by insurance companies, charterers, and port state authorities.
5. **Statutory certification on behalf of flag states** (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL).

**Four Major Classification Societies:**
1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — UK, founded 1760.
2. **DNV** (Det Norske Veritas) — Norway.
3. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** — USA.
4. **Bureau Veritas (BV)** — France.
5. *(Bonus)* **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** — India.
6. *(Bonus)* **Korean Register (KR)** — South Korea.
7. *(Bonus)* **ClassNK** — Japan.

**IACS (International Association of Classification Societies):**
- Founded **1969**, headquartered in **London**.
- A non-governmental body that brings together major classification societies.
- Develops **Unified Requirements (UR)** — common technical standards.
- Examples: **UR Z17** (welding), **UR Z23** (NDT).
- Promotes harmonization of rules to avoid divergent requirements.

**[7 Marks: 2 roles + 1 each for 4 societies + 2 for IACS explanation]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain with a neat sketch: (i) the concept of a Control Chart, (ii) how the Upper and Lower Control Limits are calculated, and (iii) how out-of-control signals are detected.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Concept:**
A control chart is a graphical tool used to monitor a manufacturing process over time. It plots sample statistics (mean, range, proportion) against time, with three horizontal lines:
- **Center Line (CL)** = process average.
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** = CL + 3σ.
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** = CL − 3σ.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
   X̄ ─┐
       │       UCL ───────────── (CL + 3σ)
       │   ╱╲   ╱╲
       │  ╱  ╲ ╱  ╲  ╱╲
       │─╳────╳────╳──╳── CL ─── (process mean)
       │ ╲  ╱ ╲  ╱
       │  ╲╱   ╲╱
       │       LCL ───────────── (CL − 3σ)
       └─────────────────────── Sample Number
```

**(ii) Calculation of UCL and LCL (for $\bar{X}$-chart):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = grand mean, $\bar{R}$ = average range, $A_2$ = table constant.

**(iii) Out-of-Control Signals (Western Electric Rules):**
1. Any single point beyond UCL or LCL.
2. **Two of three** consecutive points beyond the **2σ** warning limit on the same side.
3. **Four of five** consecutive points beyond the **1σ** limit on the same side.
4. **Seven or more** consecutive points on the same side of CL (run).
5. **Seven or more** consecutive points trending up or down (trend).

When detected, the process is stopped, **assignable cause investigated**, corrective action taken, and chart restarted with revised limits.

**[7 Marks: 1 concept + 1 sketch + 2 formulas + 3 rules]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse QA with QC** — many students lose 1–2 marks by writing the definition of QA when QC is asked.
> 2. **Always include units** in numerical answers (mm, kJ/mm, MPa).
> 3. In Cp/Cpk problems, students often forget to **subtract LSL from USL** correctly — read carefully.
> 4. In control chart sketches, **label the axes** (Sample No. vs Measured Value) and **mark UCL, CL, LCL** explicitly.
> 5. For classification society questions, write **at least one specific society with its country** — avoid generic answers.
> 6. Mention **IACS UR Z17** when discussing welding QC — it is a high-yield KTU keyword.
> 7. For NDT questions, do not forget to state the **defect type detected** along with the method name.
> 8. In SQC derivations, the constant $A_2$ must match the **sample size $n$** given — table values differ.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU Board Exam:**

- **QC vs QA:** QC = product, QA = process. Remember the mnemonic.
- **Three Pillars of QC:** Material → Process → Product.
- **Classification Societies:** LR, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS, KR, ClassNK. Know countries + **IACS** (formed 1969, HQ London).
- **IACS Key URs:** **Z17** (welding), **Z23** (NDT).
- **Six NDT methods:** VT, MT, PT, UT, RT, ET (+ ACFM for underwater).
- **MT** for ferromagnetic surface cracks; **PT** for non-magnetic (SS, Al); **UT** for internal; **RT** for critical volumetric.
- **Heat Input formula:** $Q = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm. Typical range **0.8–2.0 kJ/mm**.
- **Bevel angle** standard: **30° ± 5°**.
- **Root gap** for ship welds: **2–4 mm**.
- **Statistical Control Chart formulas:**
  - $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$
  - $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$
  - $A_2$ for $n=5$ is **0.577**; for $n=4$ is **0.729**; for $n=3$ is **1.023**.
- **Three-Sigma Rule:** $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ captures **99.73%** of normal distribution.
- **Cp formula:** $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$; **Cpk** adds centering via $\mu$.
- **Process Capability Benchmarks:** Cp ≥ 1.33 = capable; Cpk ≥ 1.33 = capable + centered.
- **OC Curve logic:** $P_a$ = Probability of acceptance of lot for given $p$, $n$, and acceptance number.
- **c-chart** used for **rare events** (coating pinholes); **p-chart** for **variable sample size**.
- **Hull QC chain:** Material → Cutting → Fit-up → Welding → NDT → Coating → Assembly → Trials → Class Delivery.
- **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5** = Documented Information requirement — always mention for QA questions.
- **WPS-PQR-Welder Qualification** = the **welding triangle** of approval.
- **Sea Trial QC** includes: speed, fuel consumption, maneuverability, vibration, noise, steering gear test.
- **Painting QC**: Surface prep to **Sa 2.5** (ISO 8501-1); DFT check with **Elcometer**; **Holiday Detection** for pinholes.
- **Common KTU keyword triggers:**
  - "Class certificate" → classification society
  - "Process capability" → Cp, Cpk
  - "Out of control" → 3-sigma rule + Western Electric rules
  - "Welder qualification" → WPS-PQR-Welder trio
- **Numbers to remember:** IACS = 1969 (London); $A_2(n=5)$ = 0.577; $d_2(n=5)$ = 2.326; Standard bevel = 30°.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Quality Control**

topics.title: Capacity Planning and Cost Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity Planning and Cost Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacity Planning in Shipbuilding

**Capacity Planning** in a shipyard is the systematic process of determining the production volume (in **DWT/year**, **CGT/year**, or **vessels/year**) that the yard's resources — berths, building docks, slipways, workshops, cranes, and skilled labor — can sustain over the planning horizon, and aligning these resources to meet forecasted market demand.

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity** is the maximum output rate that a shipbuilding facility can produce under normal operating conditions, measured in terms of compensated gross tonnage (CGT), deadweight tonnage (DWT), or number of vessels per year. **Capacity Planning** is the long-term strategic decision of matching facility resources to the order book to optimize throughput and return on investment.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen Restaurant:** Imagine a shipyard as a large restaurant kitchen. The number of stoves (building docks), ovens (panelshops), waiters (welders/fitters), and prep tables (stockyards) determine how many complex meals (vessels) you can produce per day. Capacity planning is deciding whether to add a new stove, hire more chefs, or extend kitchen hours to handle more dinner orders. Just as a restaurant that over-promises gets delayed meals, a shipyard that over-commits beyond capacity suffers delivery slippage and cost overrun.

### Types of Capacity

| Type | Definition | Example (Shipyard) |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Design Capacity** | Theoretical maximum output under ideal conditions | 12 Panamax bulk carriers/year on 3 building docks |
| **Effective Capacity** | Output achievable under realistic working conditions (considering maintenance, breaks) | 9 vessels/year after accounting for ~75% utilization |
| **Actual Output** | Realized production considering breakdowns, absenteeism, weather | 7 vessels/year typical realized output |

## 1.2 Cost Estimation in Shipbuilding

**Cost Estimation** in shipbuilding is the analytical process of forecasting the financial resources required to design, construct, outfit, and deliver a vessel — typically expressed as a function of **light ship weight (LSW)**, **CGT**, or **DWT** — from the earliest quotation stage through contract signing.

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Cost Estimation** is the engineering-economic procedure that decomposes a vessel into work packages (steel, outfit, machinery, painting, electrical), assigns material, labor, and overhead costs to each, and aggregates them into a contract price, often using parametric models calibrated against historical yard data.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a Custom House:** Building a ship is like commissioning a custom multi-story house. The contractor first estimates the cost of raw materials (steel plates, pipes, engines = bricks, tiles, fittings), then the labor hours for each trade (welders, fitters, electricians = masons, plumbers, electricians), adds project overhead (site office, insurance = site engineer, permits), and finally applies a profit margin. Without a structured estimate, both the builder and the client are in the dark about the actual cost.

### Typical Cost Breakdown of a Newbuilding
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Material Cost (Steel, Outfit, Machinery):** ~**60–70%** of total cost
> - **Labor Cost (Direct Man-hours):** ~**15–20%** of total cost
> - **Overhead & Indirect Cost (Design, Supervision, Utilities):** ~**10–15%** of total cost
> - **Profit Margin:** ~**3–8%** depending on market conditions

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacity Planning — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Capacity Measurement Metrics in Shipyards

A shipyard's capacity is best expressed through multiple metrics simultaneously, because no single number captures the complete picture.

- **Annual Tonnage Output (DWT/year or CGT/year):** Total deadweight (or compensated gross tonnage) of all vessels delivered per year.
- **Steel Throughput (tonnes/year):** Quantity of fabricated and erected steel per year across all berths.
- **Vessel Equivalents/year:** Number of standardized vessels (e.g., Aframax tankers) deliverable annually.
- **Building Dock Hours/year:** Effective working hours available on the building dock, given occupancy.

### 2.1.2 Factors Affecting Shipyard Capacity

- **Number and size of building docks / slipways / dry docks**
- **Steel pre-fabrication workshop capacity** (panel line productivity)
- **Crane capacity** (goliath, gantry, tower cranes) for heavy lifts
- **Skilled labor pool** (welders, fitters, electricians, pipefitters)
- **Steel stockyard area** for plate storage
- **Outfitting quay length and outfitting berths**
- **Design and engineering office throughput**
- **Supply chain reliability** (delays in main engine, steel, electrical equipment)
- **Working hours per week** (single-shift vs. double-shift operations)

### 2.1.3 Capacity Planning Strategies

| Strategy | Logic | Risk |
|----------|-------|------|
| **Lead Strategy** | Build capacity *ahead* of demand | High capital exposure during downturns |
| **Lag Strategy** | Add capacity *after* demand rises | Lost orders, market share erosion |
| **Match (Tracking) Strategy** | Add capacity in small increments matching demand growth | Best for cyclical shipbuilding market |

### 2.1.4 Capacity Utilization (CU)

$$CU = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Effective Capacity}} \times 100\%$$

A **healthy utilization for a shipyard** lies between **75% and 85%**; beyond this, the yard becomes congested and slippage occurs. Below 60%, fixed cost absorption is poor.

### 2.1.5 Bottleneck Identification (Theory of Constraints)

The yard's throughput is governed by its **single most constrained resource** — the *drum*. Capacity planning must identify and elevate the bottleneck (the *drum-buffer-rope* method by Goldratt).

## 2.2 Cost Estimation — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Cost Classification

- **By Behaviour:**
  - **Fixed Costs:** Design cost, yard infrastructure, depreciation — independent of vessel size.
  - **Variable Costs:** Steel, consumables, labor hours — scale with vessel size.

- **By Traceability:**
  - **Direct Costs:** Steel plates, pipes, cables, main engine — traceable to a specific vessel.
  - **Indirect Costs (Overheads):** Yard supervision, utilities, design office — shared across vessels.

### 2.2.2 Cost Estimation Methods

1. **Analogical (Comparative) Estimation:** Cost of new vessel ≈ Cost of similar past vessel × size ratio factor.
2. **Parametric Estimation:** Cost = a × (LSW)^b or Cost = a × (CGT)^b — fitted by regression.
3. **Bottom-Up / Detailed Estimation:** Sum of work-package costs (steel, outfit, machinery, electrics).
4. **Activity-Based Costing (ABC):** Cost = Σ (Activity cost-driver rate × Activity consumption).

### 2.2.3 Learning Curve Effect

As cumulative production doubles, the labor hours per unit decrease by a fixed percentage (the **learning rate**, typically **80–90%** in shipbuilding).

$$Y = a X^{b}$$

where the learning exponent is:

$$b = \frac{\log(\text{Learning Rate})}{\log 2}$$

For an **80% learning curve** (a 20% reduction on doubling):

$$b = \frac{\log 0.8}{\log 2} = \frac{-0.0969}{0.3010} \approx -0.3219$$

### 2.2.4 Man-Hour Curves and Cost-per-Ton

A standard yard metric is **man-hours per ton of steel** (mhr/t) for hull fabrication, and a separate metric for outfitting. The **newbuilding price per ton** ($/LSW-t) is a yard-specific figure used for benchmarking.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save these equations — they appear in every KTU Numerical and Part B question on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $CU = \dfrac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%$ | Capacity Utilization | % |
| 2 | $C_{total} = C_m + C_L + C_{OH} + C_{profit}$ | Total newbuilding price | USD |
| 3 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{LSW}$ | Cost per light-ship ton | USD/ton |
| 4 | $Y = a X^{b}$ | Learning curve equation | mhr/unit |
| 5 | $b = \dfrac{\log r}{\log 2}$ | Learning exponent (r = learning rate) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{new} = C_{ref} \times \left(\dfrac{LSW_{new}}{LSW_{ref}}\right)^{0.6\text{ to }0.8}$ | Parametric scaling (power law) | USD |
| 7 | $C_{escalated} = C_{today} \times (1+i)^{n}$ | Cost escalation over n years | USD |
| 8 | $P_{berth} = \dfrac{T_{steel}}{HR_{dock}}$ | Berth productivity (tonnes/hr) | t/hr |
| 9 | $N_v = \dfrac{H_{annual}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Annual vessels from one berth | vessels/year |
| 10 | $OH_{alloc} = \dfrac{OH_{total}}{CGT_{annual}}$ | Overhead allocation rate | USD/CGT |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quotation & Bidding:** Shipyards must respond to Requests for Proposal (RFP) within 4–8 weeks with credible cost estimates — parametric models enable this.
- **Budgeting & Cash-Flow Planning:** A 3-year build program needs accurate cost forecasts to secure working-capital loans.
- **Investment Decisions:** Adding a new building dock costs >$200M; capacity planning justifies such capital outlay.
- **Negotiation Leverage:** A parametric cost benchmark (e.g., $2,400/LSW-t for a tanker) gives buyers and sellers an objective reference price.
- **Risk Management:** Sensitivity analysis on steel price, currency, and learning rate hedges against market volatility.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity Utilization with Bottleneck

**Problem Context:** A shipyard has **2 building docks** and **3 outfitting berths**. Each building dock produces **4 vessels/year** and each outfitting berth handles **3 vessels/year**. Find the bottleneck and the realistic annual capacity.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Calculate output per resource group:**

Building dock capacity:

$$C_{dock} = 2 \text{ docks} \times 4 \text{ vessels/dock/year} = 8 \text{ vessels/year}$$

Outfitting berth capacity:

$$C_{berth} = 3 \text{ berths} \times 3 \text{ vessels/berth/year} = 9 \text{ vessels/year}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the bottleneck:**

The bottleneck is the resource with the **lowest capacity**, because it throttles the entire system. Comparing:

$$\min(8, 9) = 8 \text{ vessels/year}$$

Hence the **building docks** are the bottleneck (the "drum").

**Step 3 — Apply a buffer factor (realistic utilization ≈ 80%):**

$$C_{effective} = 8 \times 0.80 = 6.4 \text{ vessels/year}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to CGT equivalent (assume each vessel = 25,000 CGT):**

$$CGT_{annual} = 6.4 \times 25{,}000 = 160{,}000 \text{ CGT/year}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Capacity Utilization if yard actually delivers 5.5 vessels:**

$$CU = \frac{5.5}{6.4} \times 100\% = 85.94\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is at the upper edge of healthy utilization; the yard should consider adding a third building dock or a 4th outfitting berth (a *lead strategy*) before signing more orders.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Parametric Cost Estimation (Power-Law Model)

**Problem Context:** A reference Aframax tanker cost **$48 million** at a light ship weight (LSW) of **12,000 tonnes**. A prospective Suezmax tanker has an LSW of **20,000 tonnes**. The yard's cost-size scaling exponent is **0.72** (typical for ship steel + outfit). Estimate the new vessel's material+outfit cost.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — State the parametric model:**

$$C_{new} = C_{ref} \times \left(\frac{LSW_{new}}{LSW_{ref}}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$C_{new} = 48{,}000{,}000 \times \left(\frac{20{,}000}{12{,}000}\right)^{0.72}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the size ratio:**

$$\frac{20{,}000}{12{,}000} = 1.6667$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponentiated term:**

$$1.6667^{0.72} = e^{0.72 \times \ln 1.6667} = e^{0.72 \times 0.5108} = e^{0.3678} \approx 1.4446$$

**Step 5 — Final cost:**

$$C_{new} = 48{,}000{,}000 \times 1.4446 = 69{,}340{,}800 \text{ USD} \approx \$69.34 \text{ million}$$

**Step 6 — Verify by computing cost per ton:**

$$\frac{C_{ref}}{LSW_{ref}} = \frac{48{,}000{,}000}{12{,}000} = \$4{,}000/\text{t}$$

$$\frac{C_{new}}{LSW_{new}} = \frac{69{,}340{,}800}{20{,}000} = \$3{,}467/\text{t}$$

The cost-per-ton decreases with size (economies of scale), which is physically consistent — larger ships spread the design, engineering, and overhead costs over more tonnes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Learning Curve Application

**Problem Context:** The first vessel in a series required **200,000 man-hours** of direct labor. The learning rate is **85%** (15% reduction each time cumulative production doubles). Find the labor hours for the **4th vessel** and the **8th vessel** in the series.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute the learning exponent b:**

$$b = \frac{\log 0.85}{\log 2} = \frac{-0.07058}{0.30103} = -0.2345$$

**Step 2 — Apply the learning curve $Y = a X^b$:**

For the 1st vessel, a = 200,000, X = 1, so Y₁ = 200,000 (sanity check ✓).

For the 4th vessel (X = 4):

$$Y_4 = 200{,}000 \times 4^{-0.2345} = 200{,}000 \times e^{-0.2345 \times \ln 4}$$

$$= 200{,}000 \times e^{-0.2345 \times 1.3863} = 200{,}000 \times e^{-0.3251}$$

$$= 200{,}000 \times 0.7223 = 144{,}465 \text{ mhr}$$

For the 8th vessel (X = 8):

$$Y_8 = 200{,}000 \times 8^{-0.2345} = 200{,}000 \times e^{-0.2345 \times 2.0794}$$

$$= 200{,}000 \times e^{-0.4877} = 200{,}000 \times 0.6140 = 122{,}800 \text{ mhr}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check using the 85% rule directly:**

At doubling (1→2, 2→4, 4→8), hours reduce by 15%:

$$Y_2 = 200{,}000 \times 0.85 = 170{,}000 \text{ mhr}$$

$$Y_4 = 170{,}000 \times 0.85 = 144{,}500 \text{ mhr} \checkmark$$

$$Y_8 = 144{,}500 \times 0.85 = 122{,}825 \text{ mhr} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exponential formula and the successive-doubling shortcut agree to within rounding error. KTU examiners accept either method; the shortcut is faster in exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cost Build-Up of a Sample Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks for a *cost sheet* or *build-up table*. Memorize this structure.

| S. No. | Work Package | Quantity | Unit | Rate (USD) | Cost (USD) | % of Total |
|--------|--------------|----------|------|------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Hull Steel (plates, profiles) | 6,000 t | USD/t | 850 | 5,100,000 | 30.0% |
| 2 | Outfit Steel (casing, ladders) | 800 t | USD/t | 1,200 | 960,000 | 5.6% |
| 3 | Pipes & Valves | Lot | LS | — | 1,500,000 | 8.8% |
| 4 | Cables & Electrical | Lot | LS | — | 900,000 | 5.3% |
| 5 | Main Engine + Auxiliaries | 1 set | LS | — | 4,200,000 | 24.7% |
| 6 | Paint & Corrosion Protection | 8,000 L | USD/L | 14 | 112,000 | 0.7% |
| 7 | Direct Labor (Hull) | 180,000 mhr | USD/hr | 18 | 3,240,000 | 19.0% |
| 8 | Direct Labor (Outfit) | 60,000 mhr | USD/hr | 18 | 1,080,000 | 6.3% |
| 9 | **Sub-total Direct Cost** | — | — | — | **17,092,000** | **100%** |
| 10 | Overhead @ 25% of Direct | — | — | — | 4,273,000 | +25% |
| 11 | **Prime Cost (Sub-total + OH)** | — | — | — | **21,365,000** | — |
| 12 | Design & Engineering (LS) | — | — | — | 700,000 | — |
| 13 | Contingency @ 5% of Prime | — | — | — | 1,068,250 | — |
| 14 | **Quoted Contract Price** | — | — | — | **23,133,250** | — |

(LSW assumed = 6,800 t ⇒ **Cost per LSW-ton ≈ \$3,402/t**)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capacity Planning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Demand Forecast] --> B[Order Book Projection]
    B --> C[Existing Resource Inventory]
    C --> D[Capacity Gap Analysis]
    D --> E{Gap Exists?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Capacity Strategy Selection]
    E -- No --> G[Maintain Current Plan]
    F --> H[Lead Strategy]
    F --> I[Lag Strategy]
    F --> J[Match Strategy]
    H --> K[Capital Investment Plan]
    I --> L[Sub-contracting Plan]
    J --> M[Phased Expansion Plan]
    K --> N[Implementation & Monitoring]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    G --> N
    N --> O[Annual Capacity Audit]
    O --> P[Utilization KPI Report]
    P --> D
```

## 4.2 Shipyard Capacity Hierarchy (Bottleneck Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Yard Annual Capacity] --> B[Steel Fabrication Workshop]
    A --> C[Building Dock Berth]
    A --> D[Outfitting Berth]
    A --> E[Paint & Insulation Shop]
    A --> F[Commissioning Quay]
    B --> B1[Capacity = Panel Line Throughput]
    C --> C1[Capacity = Dock Hours per Year]
    D --> D1[Capacity = Quay Length x Parallelism]
    E --> E1[Capacity = Booth Hours per Year]
    F --> F1[Capacity = Mooring Slot Availability]
    C1 --> G{Bottleneck Drum}
    D1 --> G
    B1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Drum-Buffer-Rope Scheduling]
    H --> I[Optimized Annual Output]
```

## 4.3 Cost Estimation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Specification & General Arrangement] --> B[Quantity Take-Off]
    B --> C[Steel Weight Estimation]
    B --> D[Outfit Weight Estimation]
    B --> E[Machinery Specification]
    C --> F[Material Cost Module]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    G[Labor Standards Database] --> H[Man-Hour Estimation]
    F --> I[Direct Cost Aggregation]
    H --> I
    J[Overhead Allocation Engine] --> I
    I --> K[Base Cost]
    K --> L[Escalation Adjustment]
    L --> M[Contingency Add-On]
    M --> N[Profit Margin]
    N --> O[Quotation Price]
    P[Parametric Benchmark Model] -. Validates .-> O
```

## 4.4 Capacity vs. Cost Trade-Off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Capacity Utilization less than 60 percent] --> A1[High Unit Cost per CGT]
    B[Optimal Utilization 75 to 85 percent] --> B1[Minimum Unit Cost per CGT]
    C[Over Utilization greater than 90 percent] --> C1[Schedule Slippage and Rework Cost]
    A1 --> D[Total Cost of Operation]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Capacity Planning Objective]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Capacity Utilization" of a shipyard. What is the typical healthy range?**

**Model Answer:**
Capacity Utilization (CU) of a shipyard is the ratio of actual output delivered in a year to the effective (realistically achievable) capacity of the yard, expressed as a percentage:

$$CU = \frac{\text{Actual Vessels Delivered}}{\text{Effective Annual Capacity}} \times 100\%$$

The **healthy range is 75%–85%**. Below 60%, fixed cost absorption is poor; above 90%, the yard becomes congested, leading to schedule slippage, quality defects, and overtime cost escalation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a "learning curve" in the context of shipbuilding cost estimation? State the typical learning rate.**

**Model Answer:**
The learning curve describes the systematic reduction in direct labor man-hours required per vessel as cumulative production doubles, due to worker familiarity, process refinement, and standardization. It is expressed as:

$$Y = a X^{b}, \quad b = \frac{\log r}{\log 2}$$

where *r* is the learning rate. The **typical learning rate in shipbuilding is 80%–90%**, meaning a 10%–20% reduction in labor hours each time cumulative output doubles. Series production of standardized vessels (e.g., naval corvettes, PSVs) realizes the strongest learning benefits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Capacity Planning Focus
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

A shipyard has **2 building docks** (each producing **3 Panamax bulk carriers/year**), **3 outfitting berths** (each handling **2.5 vessels/year**), and a steel fabrication workshop with annual capacity of **80,000 tonnes**. Each Panamax bulk carrier has a light ship weight (LSW) of **14,000 tonnes**. Calculate:

**(a)** (7 marks) The annual capacity of the yard in vessels and in CGT, identifying the bottleneck resource.

**(b)** (7 marks) The capacity utilization if the yard actually delivers **4 vessels** in a year, and recommend a strategy to eliminate the bottleneck.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Compute output per resource:
- Building dock capacity: 2 × 3 = 6 vessels/year
- Outfitting berth capacity: 3 × 2.5 = 7.5 vessels/year
- Steel workshop can produce 80,000 / 14,000 = 5.71 vessels/year

Step 2 — Identify the bottleneck (lowest output):
- The **steel fabrication workshop is the bottleneck** at 5.71 vessels/year.

Step 3 — Annual capacity:
- Vessels = **5 per year** (rounded down to maintain quality, plus 80% utilization = 4.57, take 5 with buffer scheduling).

Step 4 — CGT equivalent (assuming 1 Panamax bulk carrier ≈ 18,000 CGT):
$$CGT_{annual} = 5 \times 18{,}000 = 90{,}000 \text{ CGT/year}$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Capacity Utilization:
$$CU = \frac{4}{5} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

Step 2 — This is within the healthy range but the bottleneck caps growth.

Step 3 — Recommended strategy: **Lead Strategy** — invest in a second panel line or a new automated welding station to raise steel workshop capacity to ≥ 7 vessels/year. This matches the downstream dock and outfitting capacity and enables the yard to bid for additional orders.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cost Estimation Focus
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

A shipyard quoted **\$42 million** for a product tanker of **10,000 tonnes LSW**. A new chemical tanker of **16,000 tonnes LSW** is to be quoted. Material + outfit cost scales with a power-law exponent of **0.70**. Direct labor for the new vessel is estimated at **240,000 man-hours** at a rate of **\$20/mhr**. Overhead is **25% of direct cost**, design fee is **\$1.2 million**, contingency is **5% of prime cost**, and profit margin is **8%** of total cost.

**(a)** (7 marks) Estimate the material + outfit cost of the chemical tanker using the parametric model.

**(b)** (7 marks) Build the complete cost sheet and determine the final contract price.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

$$C_{material} = 42{,}000{,}000 \times \left(\frac{16{,}000}{10{,}000}\right)^{0.70}$$

$$= 42{,}000{,}000 \times (1.6)^{0.70} = 42{,}000{,}000 \times e^{0.70 \times \ln 1.6}$$

$$= 42{,}000{,}000 \times e^{0.70 \times 0.4700} = 42{,}000{,}000 \times e^{0.3290}$$

$$= 42{,}000{,}000 \times 1.3896 = 58{,}363{,}200 \text{ USD} \approx \$58.36 \text{ million}$$

**Part (b):**

| Item | Calculation | Amount (USD) |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| Material + Outfit | From part (a) | 58,363,200 |
| Direct Labor | 240,000 × 20 | 4,800,000 |
| **Direct Cost Subtotal** | | **63,163,200** |
| Overhead @ 25% | 0.25 × 63,163,200 | 15,790,800 |
| **Prime Cost** | | **78,954,000** |
| Design Fee | LS | 1,200,000 |
| Contingency @ 5% | 0.05 × 78,954,000 | 3,947,700 |
| **Cost before Profit** | | **84,101,700** |
| Profit @ 8% | 0.08 × 84,101,700 | 6,728,136 |
| **Final Contract Price** | | **≈ \$90.83 million** |

Verification — Cost per LSW-ton:

$$\frac{90{,}830{,}000}{16{,}000} = \$5{,}677/\text{t}$$

This is within the typical chemical-tanker benchmark of **\$5,000–\$6,000/LSW-t**, validating the estimate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Capacity Planning & Cost Estimation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Design Capacity" with "Effective Capacity":** Always apply the 75–85% utilization factor before computing utilization. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using design capacity directly.
> 2. **Forgetting to round down vessel count:** A yard delivering 5.71 vessels cannot deliver a 0.71 fractional vessel. Always round down to the next integer and state the assumption.
> 3. **Mixing the power-law exponent (0.6–0.8) for cost vs. learning exponent (negative) for labor:** These are entirely different equations. Label them clearly.
> 4. **In cost sheets, omitting either overhead OR profit:** A cost sheet that lists direct cost but skips overhead is *incomplete* — KTU deducts 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Always write USD, USD/ton, mhr, or CGT explicitly. An answer "90.83" without units loses a mark.
> 6. **Misidentifying the bottleneck:** Always compare **all** resource outputs and select the *minimum*. A common error is to pick the largest resource by mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacity Planning & Cost Estimation**
>
> **Capacity Planning**
> - Three types of capacity: Design > Effective > Actual.
> - Healthy CU range: **75%–85%**.
> - Capacity strategies: **Lead, Lag, Match** — Match is preferred in cyclical shipbuilding markets.
> - **Bottleneck = resource with minimum output rate**; use Drum-Buffer-Rope (Goldratt) to elevate it.
> - Capacity expressed in **DWT/year, CGT/year, vessels/year, or tonnes of steel/year**.
>
> **Cost Estimation**
> - Typical cost split: **Material 60–70% | Labor 15–20% | Overhead 10–15% | Profit 3–8%**.
> - Parametric model: $C_{new} = C_{ref} \times (LSW_{new}/LSW_{ref})^n$, with **n = 0.6–0.8**.
> - Learning curve: $Y = aX^b$, $b = \log r / \log 2$. Typical **r = 0.80–0.90**.
> - Cost escalation: $C_{n} = C_{0} (1+i)^n$.
> - Cost per LSW-ton: standard yard benchmark, **\$2,000–\$6,000/t** depending on vessel type.
>
> **Exam Strategy**
> - Always show **step-by-step** calculation — KTU awards method marks even if the final number is wrong.
> - Use **mermaid/flowchart** in answers to score full marks for "describe" type questions.
> - Memorize the **cost build-up table structure** — at least 8–10 line items.
> - Practice **2–3 numerical problems** on parametric cost and learning curve — these are guaranteed 7-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productivity in Shipyard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Productivity in Shipyard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Productivity in a Shipyard** is formally defined as the ratio of the *output* (a measurable quantity of ship construction work completed, such as compensated gross tons of steel fabricated, number of blocks erected, or completed hull sections) to the *input* (resources consumed, primarily direct labor man-hours, materials, capital, and machine time) expended to produce that output, over a specified accounting period.

Mathematically, the simplest expression is:

$$
\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (Value/Quantity of Work)}}{\text{Input (Resources Consumed)}}
$$

In shipbuilding literature, productivity is most commonly expressed in terms of **Man-Hours per Compensated Gross Ton (MH/CGT)** — a universal benchmark metric adopted by shipyards worldwide. The lower the MH/CGT, the higher the productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Productivity is the measure of efficiency with which a shipyard converts inputs (labor, materials, capital, time) into a finished vessel or major ship component. It is the cornerstone metric of shipyard competitiveness under the 2024 Scheme syllabus."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef preparing **Biryani** for a wedding. If you and your team cook **50 kg** of biryani in **8 hours** using 3 cooks, your *labor productivity* is 50 kg / (3 × 8) = **2.08 kg per cook-hour**. If next month the same team cooks the same 50 kg in only 5 hours, productivity has improved by **37.5%** — you produced the same output with less input.

A **shipyard is essentially a giant cooking kitchen for steel**. The "cooks" are welders, fitters, plumbers, electricians, and painters. The "rice" is the steel plates, profiles, and outfitting components. The "finished dish" is the completed ship. **Productivity tells the shipyard manager how efficiently the team is converting raw steel, time, and labor into ship tonnage.** Just as a good chef uses standardized recipes, prep stations, and timed processes, a productive shipyard uses **standardization, pre-fabrication, line-flow production, and lean construction** to minimize wasted man-hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Metric to Remember:**
> World-class shipyards (Korean, Japanese) achieve **15–25 MH/CGT**, while traditional European shipyards often operate at **40–80 MH/CGT**. The gap represents the productivity frontier of modern shipbuilding.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally applied in shipyard productivity analysis:

- **Compensated Gross Ton (CGT)**: A standardized tonnage measure developed by the **OECD** that accounts for both the size and complexity of a vessel. A 1 CGT = **100 hours of standard labor content** for a reference ship.
- **Man-Hour per CGT (MH/CGT)**: The most common labor productivity indicator.
- **Steel Man-Hour per Ton (SMH/T)**: Used specifically for steel fabrication productivity.
- **Overall Labor Productivity**: Total direct man-hours / total CGT output.
- **Schedule Adherence Index (SAI)**: Ratio of actual milestone completion to planned milestone completion.

> [!NOTE]
> **CGT Formula (OECD Standard):**
> $$\text{CGT} = A \times (\text{GT})^B + C \times (\text{DWT})^D$$
> where $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ are ship-type specific coefficients (e.g., for tankers, bulk carriers, container ships) and GT is Gross Tonnage, DWT is Deadweight Tonnage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Shipyard Productivity

Productivity in a shipyard is not a single number — it is a **system of interrelated measurements** that reflect how well the yard's engineering, procurement, fabrication, assembly, and outfitting processes are integrated. The following structured logic explains the layered analysis.

### 2.1.1 Why Measure Productivity?

- **Bidding & Pricing**: Shipbuilders must quote competitive prices; productivity directly determines labor cost component.
- **Strategic Benchmarking**: Compare yards globally (Korea, Japan, China, Europe).
- **Internal Performance Tracking**: Identify bottlenecks in specific trades (welding, outfitting, painting).
- **Investment Justification**: Justify purchase of automated cutting/welding machines by quantifying MH savings.

### 2.1.2 How is Productivity Measured?

The KTU 2024 syllabus framework classifies productivity measurement into three layers:

**Layer 1 — Single-Factor Productivity (Partial Productivity)**
- **Labor Productivity** = Output / Man-Hours
- **Capital Productivity** = Output / Capital Invested
- **Material Productivity** = Output / Material Consumed

**Layer 2 — Total Factor Productivity (TFP)**
$$
\text{TFP} = \frac{\text{Total Output Value}}{\text{Labor Cost} + \text{Capital Cost} + \text{Material Cost} + \text{Energy Cost}}
$$

**Layer 3 — Composite Productivity (yard-level)**
$$
\text{Composite Productivity Index} = \frac{\text{Aggregate Output Index}}{\text{Aggregate Input Index}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most 14-mark problems test the **Single-Factor (Labor) Productivity** and **Man-Hours per CGT** relationships. Always convert all time units consistently (hours, not hours-minutes mix) and round to **2 decimal places**.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Shipyard Productivity

The following 12 factors are cited in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Design standardization** and modularization level
2. **Level of pre-fabrication** (sub-assembly, block size)
3. **Degree of automation** (NC cutting, robotic welding)
4. **Workforce skill level** and trade certification
5. **Layout and material flow** of the yard
6. **Workplace conditions** (lighting, ventilation, height access)
7. **Supervision quality** and crew-size ratio
8. **Method of construction** (unit method, zone method, block method)
9. **Schedule discipline** and planning accuracy
10. **Outfitting content and timing** (pre-outfitting level)
11. **Weather and environmental** factors
12. **Learning curve** and series production effect

## 2.3 The Learning Curve (Wright's Law)

A critical concept in shipbuilding productivity. As cumulative output doubles, the **man-hours per unit decrease by a fixed percentage** (the learning rate).

$$
Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{\log_2(\text{LR})}
$$

where:
- $Y_x$ = man-hours to produce the $x$-th unit
- $Y_1$ = man-hours for the first unit
- $x$ = unit number
- $\text{LR}$ = learning rate (e.g., 0.80 for an 80% learning curve)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Learning Rates in Shipbuilding:**
> - Steel fabrication: **80%–85%**
> - Hull assembly: **85%–90%**
> - Outfitting: **90%–95%**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Basic Productivity | $P = \text{Output} / \text{Input}$ | Output/Input | Universal definition |
| 2 | Labor Productivity | $P_L = \text{CGT} / \text{Man-Hours}$ | CGT/MH | Yard-level labor efficiency |
| 3 | MH per CGT | $\text{MH/CGT} = \text{Total MH} / \text{CGT}$ | MH/CGT | Industry standard metric |
| 4 | Steel Man-Hour per Ton | $\text{SMH/T} = \text{Welding MH} / \text{Steel Tons}$ | MH/ton | Steel workshop KPI |
| 5 | Total Factor Productivity | $\text{TFP} = V_O / (C_L + C_K + C_M + C_E)$ | ratio | Multi-input efficiency |
| 6 | Learning Curve | $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{\log_2(\text{LR})}$ | hours | Series production |
| 7 | Productivity Index | $\text{PI} = (P_{\text{new}} / P_{\text{old}}) \times 100$ | % | Period-to-period comparison |
| 8 | Schedule Adherence | $\text{SAI} = (A_{\text{actual}} / A_{\text{planned}}) \times 100$ | % | Project control |
| 9 | Capital Productivity | $P_K = \text{Output} / \text{Capital Invested}$ | tons/Rs | ROI evaluation |
| 10 | Cost per CGT | $C/\text{CGT} = \text{Total Cost} / \text{CGT}$ | Rs/CGT | Pricing baseline |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shipyard productivity metrics drive critical business decisions:

- **Bid pricing**: A 5 MH/CGT reduction can reduce labor cost by 20–30% on a Panamax bulker.
- **Capacity planning**: Determines how many ships per year a yard can deliver.
- **Investment appraisal**: Robotic welding ROI calculated as: $\text{ROI} = (\text{MH saved} \times \text{rate} - \text{depreciation}) / \text{capital cost}$.
- **Workforce planning**: Manpower headcount = (Target MH/CGT × Order Book CGT) / Available Annual MH per worker (~1800 hr).
- **Yard modernization**: Productivity gap analysis justifies CNC plate lines, panel lines, and T-beam lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Man-Hour per Compensated Gross Ton (MH/CGT)

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between total labor man-hours consumed and the productivity metric MH/CGT.

**Step 1 — Define Total Direct Labor Input.**
Let $M$ be the total direct man-hours consumed by all trades (cutting, welding, fitting, blasting, painting, outfitting, electrical, piping) to complete a vessel.

$$
M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i
$$

where $m_i$ is the man-hour consumption of trade $i$, and $n$ is the number of trades.

*Explanation:* This is a sum-of-contributions model. Each trade contributes its own man-hour consumption; the total is the simple arithmetic sum because man-hours are additive across trades within the same yard accounting period.

**Step 2 — Define Output in Compensated Gross Tons.**
The OECD Compensated Gross Ton formula for a tanker is given by:

$$
\text{CGT} = A \cdot (\text{GT})^B + C \cdot (\text{DWT})^D
$$

For a typical product tanker, $A = 24$, $B = 0.75$, $C = 32$, $D = 0.65$ (illustrative coefficients).

*Explanation:* The CGT normalizes ship complexity — a 30,000 DWT product tanker and a 30,000 DWT bulker are NOT equal in work content. The OECD coefficients reflect the empirical labor content of each ship type.

**Step 3 — Form the Productivity Ratio.**
By definition, productivity $P$ is output per unit input. Inverting to match industry convention (lower is better):

$$
P = \frac{M}{\text{CGT}}
$$

*Explanation:* We deliberately place MH in the numerator because shipbuilders track "how many hours we burn per ton of ship." A lower $P$ means higher efficiency.

**Step 4 — Units and Boundary Conditions.**
Units: $P$ has units of $\text{MH/CGT}$. For a world-class yard, $P \in [15, 25]$; for a traditional yard, $P \in [40, 80]$.

Boundary condition: $P$ is undefined if CGT = 0 (no ship in scope).

**Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{\text{MH/CGT} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}{A \cdot \text{GT}^B + C \cdot \text{DWT}^D}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Learning Curve (Wright's Law) Application

**Statement:** A shipyard produces 5 sister product tankers in a series. The first vessel consumed **250,000 man-hours**. Assuming an **85% learning curve**, derive the cumulative man-hours for the 5 vessels.

**Step 1 — State the Learning Curve Equation.**

$$
Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}
$$

where $b = \log_2(\text{LR}) = \log_2(0.85)$.

*Explanation:* The exponent $b$ is negative because the learning rate is less than 1, so as $x$ increases, $Y_x$ decreases.

**Step 2 — Compute the Exponent $b$.**

$$
b = \log_2(0.85) = \frac{\ln(0.85)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.1625}{0.6931} = -0.2345
$$

*Explanation:* Using the change-of-base formula $\log_2(x) = \ln(x) / \ln(2)$.

**Step 3 — Compute Unit Man-Hours for Each Vessel.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= 250{,}000 \times 1^{-0.2345} = 250{,}000 \text{ MH} \\
Y_2 &= 250{,}000 \times 2^{-0.2345} = 250{,}000 \times 0.8500 = 212{,}500 \text{ MH} \\
Y_3 &= 250{,}000 \times 3^{-0.2345} = 250{,}000 \times 0.8082 = 202{,}050 \text{ MH} \\
Y_4 &= 250{,}000 \times 4^{-0.2345} = 250{,}000 \times 0.7225 = 180{,}625 \text{ MH} \\
Y_5 &= 250{,}000 \times 5^{-0.2345} = 250{,}000 \times 0.6869 = 171{,}725 \text{ MH}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each vessel is faster than the previous; the MH ratio between vessel $x$ and $2x$ is exactly the learning rate 0.85.

**Step 4 — Sum for Cumulative Man-Hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cum}} &= Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + Y_4 + Y_5 \\
&= 250{,}000 + 212{,}500 + 202{,}050 + 180{,}625 + 171{,}725 \\
&= 1{,}016{,}900 \text{ MH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Average MH/Vessel.**

$$
\bar{M} = \frac{1{,}016{,}900}{5} = 203{,}380 \text{ MH/vessel}
$$

*Explanation:* The yard saves approximately **18.65%** in average man-hours per vessel through learning — a powerful economic incentive for series production.

**Step 6 — Productivity Improvement.**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{250{,}000 - 203{,}380}{250{,}000} \times 100\% = 18.65\%
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Productivity Index (Period-over-Period)

**Statement:** A shipyard fabricated 12,000 tons of steel in 2023 using 240,000 MH. In 2024, it fabricated 14,000 tons using 252,000 MH. Compute the productivity index.

**Step 1 — Compute 2023 Productivity.**

$$
P_{2023} = \frac{12{,}000}{240{,}000} = 0.0500 \text{ tons/MH}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2024 Productivity.**

$$
P_{2024} = \frac{14{,}000}{252{,}000} = 0.0556 \text{ tons/MH}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Productivity Index.**

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{P_{2024}}{P_{2023}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0556}{0.0500} \times 100 = 111.11
$$

*Explanation:* A PI > 100 indicates productivity improvement. The yard's productivity increased by **11.11%** in 2024, despite a 5% increase in man-hours, because steel output grew by 16.67%.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-wise MH Distribution Benchmark

| Trade | Typical MH Share (%) | High-Yard MH/CGT | Traditional Yard MH/CGT |
|-------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------------|
| Steel Preparation & Cutting | 8–10 | 1.8 | 4.0 |
| Welding & Assembly | 30–35 | 7.5 | 18.0 |
| Block Painting & Coating | 6–8 | 1.5 | 3.5 |
| Outfitting (Machinery) | 15–18 | 4.0 | 9.0 |
| Piping | 10–12 | 2.8 | 6.0 |
| Electrical | 5–7 | 1.5 | 3.5 |
| Insulation & Interior | 6–8 | 1.7 | 4.0 |
| Launching & Docking | 3–4 | 0.8 | 2.0 |
| Trials & Commissioning | 4–5 | 1.0 | 2.5 |
| **Total** | **100** | **22–25** | **45–60** |

*Explanation:* The table reveals that welding + assembly alone consumes nearly 1/3 of all man-hours — making automation in welding the single highest-leverage productivity intervention.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Productivity Measurement Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Shipyard Inputs] --> B[Labor Man-Hours]
    A --> C[Capital Equipment]
    A --> D[Materials Steel]
    A --> E[Energy and Overheads]

    B --> F[Single Factor Productivity MH per CGT]
    C --> G[Capital Productivity]
    D --> H[Material Yield Percentage]
    E --> I[Energy per Ton]

    F --> J[Composite Productivity Index]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K[Benchmarking vs Global Yards]
    J --> L[Bid Pricing Decisions]
    J --> M[Investment ROI Analysis]
    J --> N[Workforce Planning]

    K --> O[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P[Reduced MH per CGT]
    P --> A
```

*Explanation:* The flow shows that all input categories feed into specialized productivity metrics, which roll up into a composite index. This index drives four key business decisions, which feed a continuous improvement loop that reduces the very inputs that started the cycle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Hierarchy of Productivity Levels

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1 Workshop Productivity] --> L2[Level 2 Department Productivity]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Yard Productivity]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Industry Benchmark]

    L1 --> L1a[Cutting tons per MH]
    L1 --> L1b[Welding meters per MH]
    L1 --> L1c[Painting sqm per MH]

    L2 --> L2a[Steel MH per ton]
    L2 --> L2b[Outfitting MH per system]
    L2 --> L2c[Erection MH per block]

    L3 --> L3a[Total MH per CGT]
    L3 --> L3b[Cost per CGT]
    L3 --> L3c[Schedule Adherence Index]

    L4 --> L4a[OECD Global Average]
    L4 --> L4b[Best in Class Korea Japan]
    L4 --> L4c[Regional Benchmark India China]
```

*Explanation:* Productivity is measured at four hierarchical levels — from a single workshop station (Level 1) up to global industry comparison (Level 4). Improvements at lower levels cascade upward.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Productivity Improvement Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Root: Improve MH per CGT] --> S1[Strategy 1 Design]
    R --> S2[Strategy 2 Fabrication]
    R --> S3[Strategy 3 Assembly]
    R --> S4[Strategy 4 Management]

    S1 --> S1a[Standardize hull forms]
    S1 --> S1b[Modularize outfitting units]
    S1 --> S1c[Increase block size]

    S2 --> S2a[NC plasma cutting]
    S2 --> S2b[Robotic welding lines]
    S2 --> S2c[Panel and T-beam lines]

    S3 --> S3a[Mega block erection]
    S3 --> S2d[Lift with gantry cranes]
    S3 --> S2e[Pre outfitting at ground]

    S4 --> S4a[Lean construction]
    S4 --> S4b[Work standardization]
    S4 --> S4c[Worker training]
    S4 --> S4d[Incentive schemes]
```

*Explanation:* The four pillars of shipyard productivity improvement are: (1) design-led gains, (2) fabrication automation, (3) assembly-scale enlargement, and (4) management system upgrades. Each branch lists specific KTU 2024-syllabus interventions.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Productivity Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WS1[Workshop 1 Steel] -->|Daily MH logs| DB[(Productivity Database)]
    WS2[Workshop 2 Outfit] -->|Daily MH logs| DB
    WS3[Workshop 3 Paint] -->|Daily MH logs| DB
    WS4[Erection Site] -->|Daily MH logs| DB

    DB --> ANAL[Analytics Engine]
    ANAL --> KPI1[Compute MH per ton]
    ANAL --> KPI2[Compute MH per CGT]
    ANAL --> KPI3[Compute Schedule Index]
    ANAL --> KPI4[Compute Learning Curve]

    KPI1 --> REP[Management Dashboard]
    KPI2 --> REP
    KPI3 --> REP
    KPI4 --> REP

    REP --> DEC1[Bid Decision]
    REP --> DEC2[Manpower Plan]
    REP --> DEC3[Capex Justification]
    REP --> DEC4[Bottleneck Alert]
```

*Explanation:* This block diagram maps the functional flow from shop-floor data capture to executive decision support. It illustrates how MH-per-CGT and other KPIs become actionable intelligence in a modern shipyard's digital control tower.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define productivity in a shipyard. State the most common metric used to measure labor productivity.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Productivity in a shipyard is the ratio of output (work completed, usually measured in Compensated Gross Tons or tons of steel fabricated) to input (resources consumed, primarily man-hours). The most common labor productivity metric is **Man-Hours per Compensated Gross Ton (MH/CGT)**, where a lower value indicates higher productivity. World-class shipyards achieve 15–25 MH/CGT.

---

### **Question 2: What is a learning curve? State the typical learning rate for hull fabrication in series shipbuilding.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A learning curve describes the reduction in man-hours per unit as cumulative production doubles. Mathematically, $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{\log_2(\text{LR})}$. For hull fabrication in series shipbuilding, the typical learning rate is **80%–85%**, meaning each time the cumulative hull count doubles, the man-hours per hull drop to 80–85% of the previous value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**A shipyard delivers three identical Panamax bulk carriers in a series. The man-hour consumption for each vessel is tabulated below:**

| Vessel No. | Steel Work (MH) | Outfitting (MH) | Painting (MH) | Trials (MH) | Total (MH) |
|------------|----------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | 180,000 | 120,000 | 50,000 | 25,000 | 375,000 |
| 2 | 162,000 | 114,000 | 47,500 | 23,750 | 347,250 |
| 3 | 153,000 | 110,500 | 46,000 | 23,000 | 332,500 |

**Each vessel has a Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT) of 12,500.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compute the MH/CGT for each vessel and the cumulative MH/CGT after the 3rd vessel. Comment on the trend.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute MH/CGT for Vessel 1.**

$$
P_1 = \frac{375{,}000}{12{,}500} = 30.00 \text{ MH/CGT}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MH/CGT for Vessel 2.**

$$
P_2 = \frac{347{,}250}{12{,}500} = 27.78 \text{ MH/CGT}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute MH/CGT for Vessel 3.**

$$
P_3 = \frac{332{,}500}{12{,}500} = 26.60 \text{ MH/CGT}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cumulative MH/CGT after Vessel 3.**

$$
P_{\text{cum}} = \frac{375{,}000 + 347{,}250 + 332{,}500}{3 \times 12{,}500} = \frac{1{,}054{,}750}{37{,}500} = 28.13 \text{ MH/CGT}
$$

**Comment on Trend:** The MH/CGT decreases monotonically from 30.00 → 27.78 → 26.60, indicating a **productivity improvement of 11.33%** from Vessel 1 to Vessel 3 due to the learning curve effect.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Compute the trade-wise learning rates (Steel, Outfitting, Painting, Trials) and comment on which trade has the steepest learning curve.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Steel Work Learning Rate.**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{steel}} = \frac{Y_2}{Y_1} = \frac{162{,}000}{180{,}000} = 0.900 \text{ (90%)}
$$

**Step 2 — Outfitting Learning Rate.**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{outfit}} = \frac{114{,}000}{120{,}000} = 0.950 \text{ (95%)}
$$

**Step 3 — Painting Learning Rate.**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{paint}} = \frac{47{,}500}{50{,}000} = 0.950 \text{ (95%)}
$$

**Step 4 — Trials Learning Rate.**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{trials}} = \frac{23{,}750}{25{,}000} = 0.950 \text{ (95%)}
$$

**Comment:** **Steel work has the steepest learning curve (90%)** — meaning it benefits most from series production. Outfitting, painting, and trials are all at 95%, indicating a milder learning effect. This is typical of shipbuilding: physical fabrication and welding improve faster than installation and commissioning tasks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**A shipyard produced 8,000 tons of steel in 2023 using 160,000 man-hours. In 2024, the same yard produced 10,500 tons using 189,000 man-hours. The order book for 2025 contains 2 product tankers of 18,000 CGT each, and the management has set a target MH/CGT of 28.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compute the productivity index for 2024 with 2023 as base year, and the percentage change in labor productivity.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute 2023 Productivity.**

$$
P_{2023} = \frac{8{,}000}{160{,}000} = 0.0500 \text{ tons/MH}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2024 Productivity.**

$$
P_{2024} = \frac{10{,}500}{189{,}000} = 0.0556 \text{ tons/MH}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Productivity Index.**

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{0.0556}{0.0500} \times 100 = 111.11
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Percentage Change.**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{0.0556 - 0.0500}{0.0500} \times 100\% = 11.11\%
$$

*Conclusion:* The yard's steel-fabrication productivity improved by **11.11%** year-on-year.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Determine the total man-hour budget for 2025 and the required workforce size, assuming each worker contributes 1,800 productive hours per year.**
