<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Shipyard Capacity and State the Role of Process in Determining It. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Shipyard capacity** is the maximum sustainable annual output of a shipbuilding facility, expressed in **CGT/year**, **vessels/year**, or **DWT/year**, as limited by its most constrained resource (berth, dock, steel shop, or outfitting quay).

> **Role of process:** The **process** (the *sequence and method* of operations — block construction, pre-outfitting %, erection sequence) directly determines the **cycle time per unit** at every resource. A well-designed process shortens cycle times, raises throughput, and elevates capacity without any infrastructure expansion. A poorly designed process creates congestion, rework, and inventory build-ups, suppressing capacity even when physical resources are abundant.

> **Conclusion:** Process is the *software* of a shipyard; infrastructure is the *hardware*. The KTU examiner's view is that **capacity = f(Process, Procedure, People, Plant)** — a multiplicative relationship, not additive.

---

### Question 2: List Four Standard Procedures Mandatory for Shipyard Capacity Audits. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (4 of the most critical procedures):**

1. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** as per ASME IX or ISO 15614, governing every joint quality and defect-rate data feeding OEE calculations.
2. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** defining every deliverable from steel order to delivery, enabling accurate time and man-hour estimation.
3. **Erection Sequence Plan** with detailed block-joining logic, controlling berth cycle time and occupancy.
4. **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for Pre-outfitting**, governing the % outfitting done at block vs. quay stage, which is the single largest determinant of berth cycle time.

> **Examiner's note:** Students often confuse *process* (the design of workflow) with *procedure* (the documented rules). KTU expects this distinction to be made explicitly.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Analysis of Process and Procedure in Shipyard Capacity

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *With suitable formulas, explain how the process parameters ($T_a$, $N$, $t_c$) determine the annual capacity of a shipyard berth. Take a numerical example of a shipyard with 3 berths, $T_a = 240$ days, and $t_c = 120$ days/vessel. Calculate the annual capacity and the CUF if the actual output is 5 vessels/year.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation for berth capacity.**

$$C_{\text{berth}} = \frac{T_a \times N}{t_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{berth}} &= \frac{240 \times 3}{120} \\
&= \frac{720}{120} \\
&= 6 \text{ vessels/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Capacity Utilization Factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CUF} &= \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5}{6} \times 100 \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A CUF of 83.33% is in the **healthy operating zone** (75–90%), indicating the yard is well-loaded without being over-stretched. The buffer of 1 vessel/year (~17%) provides contingency for unexpected delays.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Discuss with examples how standardization of procedures (WPS, SOPs, NDT protocols) contributes to shipyard capacity improvement. Use a process-improvement sensitivity table to quantify the impact.*

**Model Solution:**

**Standardization Impact Mechanism:**

1. **Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS):** Standardized welding reduces defect rates from 8% to 4%, eliminating rework man-hours and freeing ~6% of welding capacity.
2. **Pre-outfitting SOPs:** Increasing pre-outfitting from 30% to 50% reduces berth occupancy by 18%, enabling one additional vessel per year on the same berth.
3. **NDT Protocols:** Faster, standardized ultrasonic/radiographic testing reduces inspection time per block from 6 hours to 3 hours, doubling inspection throughput.
4. **Block Painting SOPs:** Climate-controlled blasting halls reduce recoat frequency by 60%, freeing ~5% of painting shop capacity.

**Process-Improvement Sensitivity Table:**

| Lever | Original | Improved | Cycle Time Reduction | Capacity Gain |
|-------|----------|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| WPS standardization | 8% defect | 4% defect | -12 hours/block | +6% |
| Pre-outfitting | 30% | 50% | -10 days/vessel | +8.3% |
| NDT protocol | 6 hr/block | 3 hr/block | -3 hours/block | +4% |
| Painting SOP | 12% rework | 5% rework | -1 day/block | +3% |

**Cumulative Capacity Gain:** ~21% over baseline, with **zero capital expenditure** — purely procedural.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $C_{\text{berth}}$ without specifying *effective* working time $T_a$ and confuse gross calendar days with net available days. Always state: $T_a = T_{\text{calendar}} - T_{\text{holidays}} - T_{\text{maintenance}} - T_{\text{weather}}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In CUF calculation, students forget to multiply by 100. The answer is a **percentage**, not a decimal.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** For part (b), students describe *what* procedures are but forget to *quantify* the impact. KTU's 2024 scheme demands **numerical sensitivity** in 7-mark answers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Bottleneck Analysis and Procedural Optimization for Capacity Enhancement

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4 Alternative]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *A shipyard has the following resources: Steel shop capacity = 8 vessels/year; Panel shop = 10 vessels/year; Outfitting quay = 6 vessels/year; Berth = 7 vessels/year. Identify the bottleneck and compute the system capacity. If the outfitting quay is upgraded to handle 9 vessels/year, what is the new system capacity and the % gain?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bottleneck.**

The bottleneck is the resource with the **lowest** capacity. Comparing: Steel = 8, Panel = 10, Outfitting = 6, Berth = 7.

The **outfitting quay (6 vessels/year) is the bottleneck.**

**Step 2 — Compute the original system capacity.**

$$C_{\text{system}} = \min(C_{\text{steel}}, C_{\text{panel}}, C_{\text{outfit}}, C_{\text{berth}}) = 6 \text{ vessels/year}$$

**Step 3 — Re-evaluate after outfitting upgrade to 9 vessels/year.**

New capacities: Steel = 8, Panel = 10, Outfitting = 9, Berth = 7.

The new bottleneck is the **berth (7 vessels/year)**.

$$C_{\text{system,new}} = 7 \text{ vessels/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage capacity gain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Gain} &= \frac{7 - 6}{6} \times 100 \\
&= 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Discuss the role of Theory of Constraints (TOC) and the 5-Focusing-Steps methodology in shipyard capacity enhancement. Illustrate with the above example.*

**Model Solution:**

**Theory of Constraints (TOC) — Goldratt's 5 Focusing Steps:**

1. **Identify** the constraint: Outfitting quay at 6 vessels/year.
2. **Exploit** the constraint: Maximize utilization of the outfitting quay — schedule pre-outfitting work to feed the quay at full capacity, avoid idle time, run shifts if needed.
3. **Subordinate** all other resources to the constraint: Steel shop and panel shop should not over-produce beyond the outfitting quay's pace (avoid inventory pile-up).
4. **Elevate** the constraint: Invest in upgrading outfitting quay capacity to 9 vessels/year.
5. **Repeat:** After elevation, the new constraint becomes the berth (7 vessels/year). Begin the cycle anew.

**Application to the Example:**

After upgrading the outfitting quay, the constraint shifts to the berth. The yard should now exploit the berth (maximize pre-erection work, parallel outfitting at multiple quay sections) and consider elevating the berth in the next investment cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Students often sum the individual capacities. The **correct** aggregation is the **minimum**, not the sum. Capacity is governed by the *weakest link*.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In TOC answers, students miss the **subordinate** step, which is conceptually subtle. KTU examiners value the full 5-step sequence.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Forgetting to express the capacity gain as a **percentage** in part (a) loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4**

- ✅ **Shipyard capacity = f(Process × Procedure × People × Plant)** — multiplicative, not additive.
- ✅ **CGT** (Compensated Gross Tonnage) is the OECD-standard output metric; $\text{CGT} = \text{GT} \times C_f$.
- ✅ **Berth capacity formula:** $C = T_a \times N / t_c$. Remember $T_a \approx 240$ days/year for Indian yards.
- ✅ **CUF** range: **75–90%** is healthy. Below 60% = under-utilization; above 90% = over-booking.
- ✅ **Takt time** = available time / demand. It sets the production pulse.
- ✅ **Bottleneck** = resource with **minimum** capacity. System capacity = $\min(C_i)$.
- ✅ **Theory of Constraints (TOC) — 5 Steps:** Identify, Exploit, Subordinate, Elevate, Repeat.
- ✅ **Process** = the *design* of workflow (block construction, pre-outfitting %, erection sequence).
- ✅ **Procedure** = the *documented rules* (WPS, SOPs, NDT, painting specs).
- ✅ **OEE** world-class benchmark ≥ 85%; typical shipyard 40–60%.
- ✅ **Pre-outfitting %** is the single largest lever on berth cycle time. Aim for 50–60%.
- ✅ **Standardization** (WPS, SOPs, class-approved procedures) yields **15–30% capacity gain** with zero capex.
- ✅ **Critical path method (CPM)** determines the minimum project duration: $L_{\text{project}} = \max(L_i)$.
- ✅ **Berth Occupancy Days (BOD)** = 365 / vessels per year; lower is better (faster turnaround).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers: **always quantify** procedural impact with numbers and tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Wisdom:** The difference between a 7-mark answer and a 14-mark answer lies in **three things** — (1) a clear governing equation, (2) a numerical example with substituted values, and (3) a sensitivity or impact table. Memorize the formula sheet, practice substituting values, and your marks will be safe.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipyard Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipyard Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shipyard Layout** refers to the systematic spatial organization and arrangement of all physical facilities, production zones, material handling systems, and support infrastructure within a ship construction and repair facility. It is the master engineering blueprint that determines the geometric positioning of building docks, slipways, outfitting berths, fabrication shops, material stores, and administrative blocks to optimize **steel throughput**, minimize **work-in-progress (WIP) inventory**, and ensure **safe, ergonomic material flow** from raw plate reception to vessel launch.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST632)** syllabus framework, shipyard layout is studied as a sub-component of **Module 4: Shipyard Infrastructure & Production Planning**, and it directly addresses the Course Outcome **CO4**: *"Plan and evaluate shipyard infrastructure, layout strategies, and production methodologies for efficient ship construction."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Shipyard layout is **NOT** just a civil engineering drawing. It is an **integrated production-engineering decision** that intertwines naval architecture, industrial engineering, logistics, and occupational safety. Examiners specifically look for the student's ability to *justify* a layout choice (not merely *describe* it).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Hospital" Metaphor

Imagine a **super-specialty hospital**:

| Hospital Zone | Shipyard Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Emergency Triage (first point of contact) | **Raw Steel Stockyard** (plate & section reception) |
| Operation Theatres (precision critical work) | **Fabrication & Assembly Shops** |
| ICU (continuous patient monitoring) | **Building Dock / Slipway** (active hull construction) |
| Recovery Ward (post-care before discharge) | **Outfitting Berth / Fitting-out Quay** |
| Mortuary / Parking (final storage) | **Launch Basin / Sea Channel** |
| Laundry, Kitchen, Pharmacy (support) | **Workshops, Pipe Shop, Electrical Shop** |

Just as a hospital cannot place its **ICU next to the kitchen** (cross-contamination of critical functions), a shipyard cannot place its **paint shop upwind of the steel stockyard** (contamination + fire hazard). **Logical zoning = efficient flow + safety.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A well-designed shipyard layout reduces the **"tonne-metres"** of material movement. Every unnecessary metre a 30-tonne steel plate travels through the yard costs money (crane hours, fuel, labour, time). Layout is therefore a **cost-control instrument**, not just a site plan.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Shipyards

The KTU syllabus demands a clear taxonomy. The three primary classifications are:

### (a) Coastal (Shore-based) Shipyard
- Located directly on a **sea coast** or **tidal estuary**.
- Launching is typically done **sideways into the sea** or **end-launch** into deep water.
- **Examples:** Cochin Shipyard (India), Hyundai Heavy Industries (Ulsan, South Korea).

### (b) Inland (River/Lake-based) Shipyard
- Built along a **river, canal, or large lake**, away from the open sea.
- Vessels are launched into the water body and then **towed** to the sea via canals.
- **Examples:** Most European historical yards (e.g., along the River Elbe, Germany).

### (c) Floating Shipyard (Afloat Repair Complex)
- A **floating dry dock** or **pontoon** that can be towed to any location.
- Used primarily for **repairs**, refits, and emergency salvage.
- Not suitable for new-building due to size constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Industry Benchmark):**
> A modern large-scale shipyard typically occupies **1.5 to 3.0 square kilometres** of land area to achieve an annual throughput of **1 to 3 million DWT (Deadweight Tonnes)**. The **land-to-water ratio** is a critical design parameter, ideally maintained at approximately **60:40 (land:water)** for balanced operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Design Principles of Shipyard Layout

A KTU examiner expects you to articulate the **"Five Pillars"** of shipyard layout design. These are derived from classical industrial engineering (Taylor, 1911; later refined by Muther, 1973) and adapted for marine heavy-engineering:

1. **Principle of Integration** — All processes that belong together must be **physically co-located** (e.g., steel preparation next to panel fabrication).
2. **Principle of Minimum Distance** — Material should travel the **shortest possible path** between consecutive operations.
3. **Principle of Flow** — Layout must permit either **straight-line** or **U-shaped** flow without back-tracking.
4. **Principle of Safety & Environment** — Hazardous zones (paint, welding fume, fuel) must be **downwind and isolated** from assembly areas.
5. **Principle of Flexibility & Future Expansion** — Modular zoning allowing phased capacity expansion without disrupting ongoing production.

---

## 2.2 Functional Zones of a Modern Shipyard (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Primary Production Zones
These are the **revenue-generating** zones where steel is transformed into a ship.

- **Steel Stockyard** — Covered storage for plates, sections, and profiles. Often serviced by **gantry cranes** of 30–50 tonne capacity.
- **Steel Processing Shop** — Shot blasting, priming, cutting (oxy-fuel + plasma), and cold forming. Houses CNC plate-cutting machines and **panel lines**.
- **Sub-assembly & Block Fabrication Shop** — Where small sub-assemblies (brackets, stiffeners) and **blocks** (50–600 tonne mega-blocks) are welded.
- **Erection Berth / Building Dock / Slipway** — The location where blocks are **lumped** (joined) to form the complete hull. Modern yards favour **giant block construction** with blocks up to **1,500 tonnes**.
- **Outfitting Berth** — Post-launch quay where piping, cabling, machinery, accommodation, and painting are completed.

### 2.2.2 Support Workshops
- **Pipe Shop**, **Machinery Shop**, **Electrical Shop**, **Joinery Shop**, **Sail Loft**, **Foundry**, **Forging Shop**, **Foundry**.
- These are typically clustered in a **"Workshop Island"** for centralized services.

### 2.2.3 Logistics & Material Handling
- **Heavy-lift Crawler Cranes** (capacity up to **1,000 tonnes** for mega-block lifts).
- **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** for moving blocks from shop to berth.
- **Conveyor Systems** for plate transfer.

### 2.2.4 Service & Administrative Zones
- Design office, technical school, hospital, fire station, canteen, and security office.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Annual Steel Throughput** | $S_{annual} = N_{ships} \times C_{steel} \times LWT$ | tonnes/year | Determines stockyard & fabrication capacity |
| 2 | **Land Area Requirement** | $A_{land} = k \cdot S_{annual}$ | m² | $k \approx 0.8$ to $1.2$ m² per annual tonne |
| 3 | **Water Area Requirement** | $A_{water} = A_{land} \times R_{lw}$ | m² | $R_{lw} \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$ (land-water ratio) |
| 4 | **Building Dock Length** | $L_{dock} = L_{pp} + 2d$ | metres | $d \approx 5$ to $10$ m clearance per end |
| 5 | **Building Dock Width** | $W_{dock} = B_{mld} + 2w$ | metres | $w \approx 3$ to $5$ m for keel blocks & staging |
| 6 | **Block Lift Capacity** | $W_{lift} = k_s \cdot W_{block}$ | tonnes | $k_s \approx 1.25$ safety factor |
| 7 | **Crane Utilization Factor** | $\eta_{crane} = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | % | Target $\geq 75\%$ for healthy yard |
| 8 | **Productivity Index** | $PI = \dfrac{C_{steel}}{Man\text{-}hours}$ | tonnes/man-hr | Modern yard target: $0.05$ to $0.15$ |
| 9 | **Stockyard Storage Days** | $D_{stock} = \dfrac{Q_{yard}}{Q_{daily\,use}}$ | days | Target $D_{stock} \approx 30$ to $60$ days |
| 10 | **Material Flow Distance** | $D_{flow} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot d_i$ | tonne-metres | Minimization objective (linear programming) |

**Where:**
- $N_{ships}$ = Number of ships built per year.
- $C_{steel}$ = Steel content per ship (tonnes).
- $LWT$ = Lightweight correction / conversion factor.
- $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars of the largest ship.
- $B_{mld}$ = Moulded breadth of the largest ship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Formulas (3) and (4) above are routinely used by naval architects during **yard feasibility studies** for greenfield shipyard projects. A miscalculation in **Land Area Requirement** is the single most expensive error a yard planner can make, as retrofitting is virtually impossible after commissioning.

---

## 2.4 Material Flow Philosophy: "T-Shaped" vs. "L-Shaped" vs. "Linear"

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **flow geometry** as a primary classification criterion.

| Flow Pattern | Description | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear (I-Shape)** | Materials move in a straight line from receipt to launch. | Long coastal yards (e.g., Hyundai Ulsan). Highest throughput but requires long coastline. |
| **L-Shaped** | Flow turns 90° at the erection area. | Compact yards with limited coastline (e.g., many European yards). |
| **T-Shaped / U-Shaped** | Steel enters one arm, ships exit the other; fabrications in the stem. | Modern integrated shipyards optimizing land use (e.g., Daewoo Okpo, DSME). |
| **Cluster (Island) Layout** | Workshops arranged in functional clusters; no strict flow direction. | Repair yards and small boat builders. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** When asked "Which layout is best?", the academically correct answer is: **"There is no universally best layout. The optimum layout is a function of (i) annual throughput, (ii) maximum ship size, (iii) site topography, (iv) tidal range, and (v) capital budget."** Examiners reward students who demonstrate this contextual reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Land Area Requirement from Annual Throughput

This is a **high-yield KTU problem** that appears almost every examination cycle. The derivation below is exhaustive and should be memorized in its entirety.

### Problem Setup

A shipyard plans to construct **$N$** ships per year. Each ship has an average **steel content** of $C$ tonnes (lightweight). The yard designer uses a standard industrial coefficient $k$ (in m² per annual tonne) to estimate the total land area required.

**Given:**
- $N$ = number of ships per year.
- $C$ = steel per ship (tonnes).
- $k$ = land area coefficient (m² per annual tonne).

**Find:** Total land area $A_{land}$ in m².

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the annual steel throughput:**

The total weight of steel processed by the yard in one year equals the number of ships multiplied by the steel content of each ship.

$$
S_{annual} = N \times C
$$

This equation tells us how many **tonnes of steel** the stockyard, fabrication shop, and erection berth must collectively handle in 12 months.

**Step 2 — Define the land area coefficient:**

The coefficient $k$ is an **empirical constant** derived from historical yard data. It encapsulates the combined effect of stockyard, shop floor, roadways, and administrative space. Typical values are:

$$
k \in [0.8, \ 1.2] \quad \text{m}^2 \text{ per annual tonne}
$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the area equation:**

Total land area is the product of annual throughput and the area coefficient.

$$
A_{land} = k \cdot S_{annual}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 3:**

$$
A_{land} = k \cdot (N \times C)
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{land} = k \cdot N \cdot C}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Validation:**

Suppose a yard plans $N = 10$ ships/year, each with $C = 8{,}000$ tonnes of steel, using $k = 1.0$ m²/(t·yr).

$$
S_{annual} = 10 \times 8{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ t/yr}
$$

$$
A_{land} = 1.0 \times 80{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 0.08 \text{ km}^2
$$

This is consistent with a small-to-medium shipyard.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Building Dock Dimensional Design

The building dock (or slipway) must physically accommodate the largest vessel the yard intends to construct.

### Given
- $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars of largest ship (m).
- $B_{mld}$ = Moulded breadth (m).
- $d$ = longitudinal clearance per end (typically 5–10 m).
- $w$ = transverse clearance per side (typically 3–5 m).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Length of the dock:**

The dock must extend beyond both perpendiculars of the vessel to allow for **overhang during block erection**, **keel block placement**, and **launching clearances**.

$$
L_{dock} = L_{pp} + 2d
$$

**Step 2 — Width of the dock:**

The dock must accommodate the full moulded breadth plus the keel blocks (central support) and working platforms on either side.

$$
W_{dock} = B_{mld} + 2w
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of the dock (for graving docks only):**

Depth is dictated by the ship's depth ($D$) plus an under-keel clearance $u$ (typically 1.5–3 m) for block supports and bilge drainage.

$$
H_{dock} = D + u
$$

**Step 4 — Consolidated design equation:**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned} L_{dock} &= L_{pp} + 2d \\ W_{dock} &= B_{mld} + 2w \\ H_{dock} &= D + u \end{aligned}}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example

A bulk carrier has $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B_{mld} = 32$ m, depth $D = 18$ m. Choose $d = 8$ m, $w = 4$ m, $u = 2$ m.

$$
L_{dock} = 200 + 2(8) = 216 \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_{dock} = 32 + 2(4) = 40 \text{ m}
$$

$$
H_{dock} = 18 + 2 = 20 \text{ m}
$$

A modern building dock is therefore **216 m × 40 m × 20 m** — comparable to a 7-storey building laid on its side.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Launching Facility Selection

The KTU syllabus expects students to recommend the **correct launching facility** based on vessel and yard characteristics.

| Vessel Type | Yard Tidal Range | Recommended Launching Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small ship (< 5,000 DWT) | Any | **Slipway (end launch)** | Low capital cost; gravity launch is sufficient. |
| Medium ship (5,000–50,000 DWT) | < 2 m | **Building Dock with Float-up** | Controlled docking; precision alignment. |
| Medium ship (5,000–50,000 DWT) | 2–5 m | **Inclined Building Berth (Side Launch)** | Uses tidal assistance; lower dock cost. |
| Large ship (50,000–200,000 DWT) | Any | **Graving / Floating Dry Dock** | Only feasible method; can be re-used. |
| Very Large ship (> 200,000 DWT, VLCC) | Any | **Building Dock + Float-out** | Dry build ensures precision; float-out at completion. |
| Inland yard, narrow river | Any | **End Launch into Canal** | Side-launch is impossible due to width. |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Modern Yard Layout Comparison

| Layout Parameter | Linear (I-Shape) | L-Shaped | U-Shaped / T-Shaped | Cluster (Island) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land efficiency | Low (long stretch) | Medium | **High** | Very High |
| Throughput capacity | **Very High** | High | High | Low–Medium |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Material flow distance | **Minimum** | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Flexibility for expansion | Limited by coastline | Moderate | **High** | Very High |
| Suitable for new-build | **Yes** (large yards) | Yes | **Yes** (modern yards) | Mostly repair |
| Risk of cross-flow interference | Low | Medium | Low | **High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Modern Integrated Shipyard

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional zones** and **material flow** of a modern U-shaped shipyard. Each node represents a functional zone, and arrows represent primary material or information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z1["A1 RAW STEEL STOCKYARD"]
    Z2["A2 STEEL PROCESSING SHOP"]
    Z3["A3 PANEL LINE AND TENTING"]
    Z4["A4 SUB ASSEMBLY SHOP"]
    Z5["A5 BLOCK FABRICATION SHOP"]
    Z6["A6 MEGA BLOCK ERECTION DOCK"]
    Z7["A7 LAUNCH BASIN"]
    Z8["A8 OUTFITTING QUAY"]
    Z9["A9 SEA TRIAL BERTH"]
    Z10["B1 PIPE SHOP"]
    Z11["B2 MACHINERY SHOP"]
    Z12["B3 ELECTRICAL SHOP"]
    Z13["B4 PAINT AND BLAST SHOP"]
    Z14["C1 DESIGN OFFICE"]
    Z15["C2 ADMIN AND HOUSING"]
    Z16["C3 UTILITY CENTRAL"]
    Z17["D1 SPMT YARD"]
    Z18["D2 HEAVY LIFT CRANE BAY"]

    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z4 --> Z5
    Z5 --> Z6
    Z6 --> Z7
    Z7 --> Z8
    Z8 --> Z9

    Z10 --> Z8
    Z11 --> Z8
    Z12 --> Z8
    Z13 --> Z5

    Z14 --> Z6
    Z14 --> Z8
    Z15 --> Z14
    Z16 --> Z6
    Z16 --> Z8

    Z17 --> Z5
    Z17 --> Z6
    Z18 --> Z5
    Z18 --> Z6
    Z18 --> Z8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **A-series zones** represent the **primary production flow** (steel → ship).
> - The **B-series zones** are **support workshops** feeding directly into outfitting and block fabrication.
> - The **C-series zones** are **administrative and utility** services.
> - The **D-series zones** are the **material-handling backbone** (cranes and transporters).

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Launching Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["START What is the ship size in DWT"]
    N2["DWT under 5000"]
    N3["DWT 5000 to 50000"]
    N4["DWT over 50000"]
    N5["Tidal range under 2m"]
    N6["Tidal range 2 to 5m"]
    N7["Use SLIPWAY end launch"]
    N8["Use BUILDING DOCK with float up"]
    N9["Use SIDE LAUNCH inclined berth"]
    N10["Use GRAVING DOCK or FLOATING DRY DOCK"]
    N11["Use BUILDING DOCK plus float out"]

    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N1 --> N4
    N3 --> N5
    N3 --> N6
    N5 --> N8
    N6 --> N9
    N4 --> N10
    N4 --> N11
    N2 --> N7
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Launching Facility Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["GRAVING DRY DOCK"]
        direction LR
        G1["SHIP HULL"]
        G2["KEEL BLOCKS"]
        G3["DOCK FLOOR"]
        G4["FLOOD AND FLOAT"]
    end

    subgraph S2["SLIPWAY END LAUNCH"]
        direction LR
        P1["INCLINED WAYS"]
        P2["SHIP HULL"]
        P3["LAUNCH SLIP TO WATER"]
        P4["FLOAT AWAY"]
    end

    subgraph S3["FLOATING DRY DOCK"]
        direction LR
        F1["PONTOON BALLAST DOWN"]
        F2["SHIP TOWED IN"]
        F3["BALLAST UP LIFT SHIP"]
        F4["REPAIR AND REFLOAT"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The three subgraphs above represent the **three dominant launching philosophies** in global shipbuilding. When asked to compare, always structure your answer around: **(i) capital cost, (ii) tide independence, (iii) reusability, (iv) maximum ship size, and (v) geographical suitability.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "shipyard layout" and list any four governing principles.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Shipyard layout** is the systematic spatial arrangement of all production, support, and service facilities within a ship construction facility, designed to optimize material flow, minimize cost, and ensure safe operations.
>
> **Four governing principles:**
> 1. **Integration** — Co-locate related processes.
> 2. **Minimum distance** — Shortest material travel path.
> 3. **Flow continuity** — Unidirectional, no back-tracking.
> 4. **Safety & flexibility** — Hazard isolation and modular expansion.

*(Award 0.5 marks for the definition, 0.5 marks each for the four principles, totalling 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between a coastal shipyard and an inland shipyard.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Coastal Shipyard | Inland Shipyard |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On sea coast or tidal estuary. | On river, canal, or lake. |
| **Launching** | Directly into the sea. | Into the water body, then towed to sea. |
| **Tide dependency** | Often tide-dependent. | Can be tide-independent via locks. |
| **Vessel size limit** | Practically unlimited (with sufficient dredging). | Limited by canal/river width and bridge clearances. |
| **Example** | Cochin Shipyard, India. | Historical Elbe river yards, Germany. |

*(Award 1 mark each for any three distinct points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ✅ CHOICE A

#### **Question A (Part a — 7 marks):**
*Explain with neat sketches the various types of launching facilities used in shipyards. Discuss their suitability for different vessel sizes.* `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Slipway (End Launch) — 2 marks:**
An inclined plane extending from the building area into the water. The ship is built on the inclined ways and launched by gravity. **Suitable for small vessels (< 5,000 DWT).** Low capital cost; tide-sensitive.

**(ii) Inclined Building Berth (Side Launch) — 2 marks:**
The ship is built on an inclined berth and launched sideways using sliding ways, triggered by removing a trigger. **Suitable for medium vessels (5,000–50,000 DWT).** Uses tidal assist; cannot be re-used efficiently for the next vessel.

**(iii) Graving Dry Dock — 2 marks:**
A rectangular basin with a removable gate. The dock is flooded to float the ship out after completion. **Suitable for large vessels (50,000–200,000 DWT).** Tide-independent; re-usable; highest capital cost.

**(iv) Floating Dry Dock — 1 mark:**
A submersible pontoon that lifts the ship out of the water. **Suitable for repairs and refits** of medium to large vessels. Mobile and versatile.

#### **Question A (Part b — 7 marks):**
*A shipyard plans to construct 12 bulk carriers per year. Each carrier has a steel content of 12,000 tonnes. If the land-area coefficient is 1.0 m² per annual tonne, calculate the total land area required and recommend a suitable layout pattern.* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 12$ ships/year
- $C = 12{,}000$ tonnes/ship
- $k = 1.0$ m²/(t·yr)

**Step 1 — Annual steel throughput:**

$$
S_{annual} = N \times C = 12 \times 12{,}000 = 1{,}44{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total land area:**

$$
A_{land} = k \times S_{annual} = 1.0 \times 1{,}44{,}000 = 1{,}44{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

Converting to km²:

$$
A_{land} = \dfrac{1{,}44{,}000}{10^6} = 0.144 \text{ km}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Water area (using land-water ratio $R_{lw} = 0.65$):**

$$
A_{water} = 0.144 \times 0.65 \approx 0.094 \text{ km}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

For an annual throughput of 144,000 tonnes, the **U-shaped / T-shaped modern integrated layout** is recommended because:
- (i) It maximizes land utilization within a compact footprint.
- (ii) It allows parallel workflow in fabrication and outfitting.
- (iii) It supports future capacity expansion without disrupting ongoing production.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert m² to km²** in their final statement. Always state both units. Also, students often **omit the recommended layout**, costing 1 mark. The layout recommendation is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B

#### **Question B (Part a — 7 marks):**
*Discuss in detail the functional zones of a modern shipyard. Explain how material flow is optimized through zoning.* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

A modern shipyard is organized into **three concentric zones**:

**(1) Core Production Zone (Inner Ring) — 3 marks:**
- Steel stockyard, processing shop, panel line, block fabrication, erection dock.
- These zones follow the **sequential material flow** from raw steel to hull blocks.
- Material moves in a **straight or U-shaped path**, minimizing re-handling.

**(2) Support Workshop Zone (Middle Ring) — 2 marks:**
- Pipe shop, machinery shop, electrical shop, paint shop.
- Positioned **adjacent** to the erection dock and outfitting quay for just-in-time delivery of components.

**(3) Administrative & Utility Zone (Outer Ring) — 2 marks:**
- Design office, training school, medical centre, fire station, power substation.
- Provides services without interfering with the core production.

**Material flow optimization** is achieved by:
- Co-locating sequential processes (block fabrication next to erection dock).
- Using **gantry cranes** and **SPMTs** for heavy block transfer.
- Eliminating cross-flow interference by isolating hazardous zones (paint, welding fume).

#### **Question B (Part b — 7 marks):**
*For a Panamax bulk carrier of length between perpendiculars $L_{pp} = 180$ m, moulded breadth $B_{mld} = 28$ m, and depth $D = 15$ m, determine the dimensions of the building dock. Take clearances: $d = 7$ m, $w = 4$ m, $u = 2$ m.* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_{pp} = 180$ m, $B_{mld} = 28$ m, $D = 15$ m, $d = 7$ m, $w = 4$ m, $u = 2$ m.

**Step 1 — Dock length:**

$$
L_{dock} = L_{pp} + 2d = 180 + 2(7) = 180 + 14 = 194 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Dock width:**

$$
W_{dock} = B_{mld} + 2w = 28 + 2(4) = 28 + 8 = 36 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Dock depth:**

$$
H_{dock} = D + u = 15 + 2 = 17 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Final dimensions:**

$$
\boxed{L_{dock} \times W_{dock} \times H_{dock} = 194 \text{ m} \times 36 \text{ m} \times 17 \text{ m}}
$$

**Validation:** The dock volume is $194 \times 36 \times 17 \approx 1{,}18{,}728$ m³, which is realistic for a Panamax-class facility.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss-of-Marks Errors:**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $L_{pp} + 2d$ (clearance applies to **both** ends).
> - Mixing up moulded breadth $B_{mld}$ with extreme breadth.
> - Omitting the **depth calculation** entirely, even though the question provides $D$.
> - Failing to state **units (metres)** in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Shipyard layout** = spatial arrangement of production, support, and service zones to optimize material flow and cost.
- 🔹 The **three primary yard types** are **coastal**, **inland**, and **floating** — each suited to specific geographies.
- 🔹 The **five design principles** are Integration, Minimum Distance, Flow, Safety, and Flexibility.
- 🔹 The **land area formula** is $A_{land} = k \cdot N \cdot C$ with $k \approx 0.8$ to $1.2$ m²/(t·yr).
- 🔹 The **building dock dimensions** are $L_{dock} = L_{pp} + 2d$, $W_{dock} = B_{mld} + 2w$, $H_{dock} = D + u$.
- 🔹 The **four launching methods** are slipway (small ships), side launch (medium), graving dock (large), and floating dry dock (repairs).
- 🔹 The **four layout geometries** are linear (I), L-shaped, U/T-shaped, and cluster (island).
- 🔹 The **land-water ratio** is ideally **60:40** for balanced operations.
- 🔹 **Modern yards** use **giant block construction** (blocks up to **1,500 tonnes**) with **SPMTs** and **gantry cranes**.
- 🔹 Always **justify your layout choice** in exam answers — never just describe it.
- 🔹 Remember to convert all area answers to **both m² and km²** for completeness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Design of Shipyard Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Design of Shipyard Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PENST632 – Shipbuilding Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the **Design of a Shipyard Layout** is defined as the systematic spatial arrangement and functional zoning of all shipbuilding infrastructure — including building docks/berths, fabrication shops, outfitting quays, material yards, launching facilities, workshops, administrative blocks, and supporting utilities — to achieve an optimum, uninterrupted flow of materials, components, and workforce from raw stock to the delivered vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> *Shipyard layout* refers to the geometric arrangement of production facilities, building berths, outfitting quays, workshops, storage areas, and transport networks within the available land–water interface, governed by a set of **technical, economic, geographic, and regulatory constraints** that collectively determine the productivity, safety, and long-term scalability of the shipyard.

A **"Factor Affecting Design"** is therefore any internal (yard-controlled) or external (site-specific or market-driven) variable that constrains the geometric configuration, dimensional sizing, or functional sequencing of these facilities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Factory on the Seashore" Analogy

Imagine you are planning to build a giant kitchen to cook a **5-course meal for 1,000 people simultaneously**. You would never arrange the stoves far from the serving counter, place the dishwasher in the middle of the dining area, or chop vegetables on a slippery floor near the entrance. Every item — the stove, sink, refrigerator, prep table, and serving station — must be placed where the **workflow is logical, the floors are level, the ventilation is good, and there is room to grow**.

A shipyard is **exactly that**, scaled up by a factor of **10⁶**. Instead of vegetables, you are moving **steel plates weighing up to 100 tonnes**; instead of a stove, you have **welding bays and assembly cradles**; instead of a serving counter, you have a **launching slipway or dry dock**. The "kitchen design rules" of the shipyard are called **factors affecting layout design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> *Layout = Geography of Production.* Get the geography wrong, and no amount of management or automation can rescue the workflow. Get it right, and even a moderately equipped yard can deliver ships on time and at profit.

## 1.3 Classification of Factors — The Big Three Buckets

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to cluster these factors into the following **three primary domains** (this is the typical 7-mark short-note framing):

1. **Technical / Production Factors** — driven by the *type and size of ships* the yard intends to build.
2. **Site / Geographic Factors** — driven by the *natural environment* of the chosen coastline.
3. **Economic / Strategic Factors** — driven by *capital, market, workforce, and regulation*.

Each factor is independent yet interlinked — a change in one (e.g., building larger VLCCs instead of Handysize bulk carriers) cascades into a re-evaluation of nearly all others (crane capacity, dock floor area, launching draft, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember the Factor Categories — "T-S-E":**
> - **T** → **T**echnical (ship type, size, production volume)
> - **S** → **S**ite / Geography (topography, metocean, soil, water depth)
> - **E** → **E**conomic / Strategic (capital, labour, transport, regulation, expansion)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor 1 — Type, Size, and Range of Ships to be Built

The **single most dominant factor**. The maximum vessel length (LOA), breadth (B), depth (D), and loaded displacement (Δ) dictate:

- **Building dock / berth dimensions** (length × width × floor area),
- **Crane hook height and outreach** (must clear the highest superstructure block),
- **Steel stockyard capacity** (steel intensity ≈ **0.20 – 0.35 × lightweight** of ship),
- **Block erection strategy** (panamamax, post-panamamax, or "mega-block" assembly).

A yard designed for **Aframax tankers (~120,000 DWT)** cannot physically accommodate a **VLCC (~320,000 DWT)** because the building dock length difference alone is roughly **80 – 100 m**.

## 2.2 Factor 2 — Production Volume & Order Book

Production volume is typically measured in **Compensated Gross Tonnes (CGT) per year** or **tonnes of steel processed per year (TPSPY)**. The yard's *annual throughput* determines:

- Number of parallel berths,
- Workshop redundancy,
- Stockyard inventory turnover.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** **Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)** = a productivity index used by OECD and shipbuilding economists to compare yards building different ship types. Reference value: 1 CGT ≈ 1 tonne of work-equivalent for an average product tanker of ~30,000 DWT.

## 2.3 Factor 3 — Availability of Land Area and Water Frontage

A shipyard needs **two scarce resources**: flat land **behind** the waterfront, and a sufficiently long **linear quay frontage**.

- **Linear waterfrontage** governs the number of simultaneous berths: $N_{berths} = L_{quay} / (L_{ship} + S_{clear})$
- **Land depth** (distance from quay to inland boundary) must accommodate stockyards, prefab shops, and transport corridors. A rule of thumb is **3 – 5 times the maximum ship breadth (B)** of land depth for a fully integrated yard.

## 2.4 Factor 4 — Topographical and Geological Conditions

- **Flat coastal land** is preferred (low earthworks cost).
- **Soil bearing capacity** (typically required ≥ **100 kN/m²** for dry dock floors) determines whether piled foundations or raft foundations are needed.
- **Rocky seabeds** increase dredging cost dramatically.
- **Tidal flats** restrict access and may require reclamation.

## 2.5 Factor 5 — Meteorological and Oceanographic (Metocean) Conditions

The *passive* environmental envelope around the yard:

- **Wind:** Dominant wind direction dictates orientation of open stockyards (to minimize dust/debris on painted surfaces). Maximum operating wind speed for crane work: typically **20 m/s (Beaufort 8)**.
- **Wave climate:** Significant wave height $H_s$ at the berth must be ≤ **0.5 – 0.8 m** for safe berthing and cargo operations.
- **Tidal range:** Affects launching slipway gradient and graving dock flooding/emptying time.
- **Currents:** Longshore currents > **1.0 m/s** complicate berthing.
- **Storm surge & cyclone risk:** Drives breakwater design.

## 2.6 Factor 6 — Availability of Skilled & Unskilled Labour

Shipbuilding is a **labour-intensive** industry (despite automation). Factors include:

- Proximity to existing **shipbuilding clusters** (e.g., Cochin, Vizag, Mumbai, Kollam in Kerala).
- Wage parity with neighbouring industries.
- Availability of welders, fitters, electricians, naval architects.

A yard located away from a labour pool must build **worker housing colonies**, raising CAPEX.

## 2.7 Factor 7 — Transportation Infrastructure

- **Road access:** For heavy haulage of engines, propellers (single piece weight can be **100 – 250 tonnes**).
- **Rail connectivity:** For bulk steel and bunker coal.
- **Inland waterways:** Useful in deltaic regions (e.g., Kerala backwaters).
- **Nearby international airport:** For expediting foreign crew and design teams.

## 2.8 Factor 8 — Supply Chain Proximity

Yards situated near **steel mills, foundries, electrical equipment manufacturers, and engine works** enjoy lower inbound logistics cost. The **"Just-in-Time (JIT)"** philosophy works only when suppliers are within **50 – 150 km**.

## 2.9 Factor 9 — Utilities — Power, Water, Compressed Air, Gas

A medium-sized yard consumes:
- **Power:** **20 – 50 MW** (continuous),
- **Potable water:** **5,000 – 15,000 m³/day**,
- **Welding gas (Ar/CO₂):** **50 – 200 cylinders/day**,
- **Compressed air:** **0.6 – 0.8 MPa** at multiple stations.

## 2.10 Factor 10 — Government Policies, Defence Restrictions & Environmental Clearance

- CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone) norms in India limit construction within **200 m of HTL** for non-essential activities.
- Defence shipyards may be located within restricted zones.
- SEZ / industrial-park benefits can offset CAPEX.
- Environmental clearances for **dredging, painting effluents, and ballast water treatment** are increasingly stringent.

## 2.11 Factor 11 — Future Expansion Provision

A well-designed yard reserves **25 – 35 %** of its land area for **Phase 2 / Phase 3** expansion (larger ships, new product lines, offshore wind platforms).

## 2.12 Factor 12 — Safety, Security & Workflow Efficiency

- Segregation of **red-zone** (hot work, painting) from **green-zone** (assembly, outfitting).
- Emergency evacuation routes.
- Cyber-secure zones for **defence / intelligence** vessels.

---

## 2.13 📋 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is a high-yield revision aid — memorize all rows before the exam.**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of building berths | $N_b = L_{quay} / (L_{ship} + S_{clear})$ | dimensionless | Capacity planning |
| 2 | Steel intensity of a ship | $W_{steel} = \alpha \cdot \Delta_{light}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.20$ – $0.35$ | Stockyard sizing |
| 3 | Stockyard area (approx) | $A_{yard} = 6 \cdot L_{ship} \cdot B_{ship}$ | m² | Land requirement |
| 4 | Berth occupancy ratio | $BOR = (T_{occ} / T_{cycle}) \cdot 100\%$ | % (target ≤ 70 %) | Productivity metric |
| 5 | Annual throughput | $TPSPY = \dfrac{CGT_{annual}}{c_{CGT}}$ | tonnes steel/yr | Benchmarking |
| 6 | Crane lifting capacity | $W_{crane} \geq k \cdot W_{max,block}$ | tonnes, $k \approx 1.25$ | Crane selection |
| 7 | Wharf load capacity | $q \geq 50$ kN/m² (general) | kN/m² | Foundation design |
| 8 | Dry dock floor pressure | $p \geq 100$ kN/m² | kN/m² | Geotechnical design |
| 9 | Power demand (medium yard) | $P_{peak} \approx 20$ – $50$ MW | MW | Substation sizing |
| 10 | Water demand | $Q_{water} \approx 5,000$ – $15,000$ m³/day | m³/day | Desalination/plumbing |
| 11 | Wind limit for crane work | $V_{crane} \leq 20$ m/s | m/s | Metocean criterion |
| 12 | Wave height at berth | $H_s \leq 0.8$ m | m | Breakwater design |
| 13 | Soil bearing capacity | $q_{ult} \geq 100$ kN/m² | kN/m² | Foundation type |
| 14 | Land depth behind quay | $D_{land} \geq 3 \cdot B_{ship}$ | m | Land acquisition |
| 15 | Expansion reserve | $A_{reserve} \geq 25\%$ of total | % | Master-plan rule |
| 16 | Building dock floor area | $A_{dock} = L_{dock} \cdot W_{dock}$ | m² | Civil works cost |
| 17 | Slipway length | $L_{slip} = (1.25$ – $1.5) \cdot L_{ship}$ | m | Launching design |
| 18 | Worker housing ratio | $N_{houses} \approx 1.2 \cdot N_{workers}$ | dimensionless | Township design |
| 19 | Harbour depth | $D_{harbour} = d_{ship} + U_{k} + S_{safety}$ | m | Dredging design |
| 20 | Production line balance | $\eta_{line} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{max}}$ | ratio (0 – 1) | Workflow efficiency |

### 2.14 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL)**, the limited water frontage at **Mattancherry** forced the adoption of a **"dock-based" rather than "berth-based"** building strategy — proving that *site geometry dictates workflow philosophy*.
- In **Hyundai Heavy Industries (Ulsan, South Korea)** — the world's largest yard — the **"U-shaped" layout** minimizes material travel distance, made possible only because of an artificially reclaimed flat land area.
- The choice of **graving dock vs. floating dock vs. slipway** (covered in Module 5) is itself a *layout factor decision*, not a stand-alone technology choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Building Berths from Quota Frontage

**Problem Statement:** A medium-sized shipyard has a total linear waterfrontage of **1,200 m** available for shipbuilding berths. The yard plans to build **Panamax bulk carriers** of LOA = **229 m**, with a clearance between adjacent berthed ships of **30 m** (for safety, fendering, and crane slewing). How many berths can the yard accommodate?

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let:
- $L_{quay}$ = total available quay length = $1200$ m,
- $L_{ship}$ = length overall of the design vessel = $229$ m,
- $S_{clear}$ = inter-ship clearance = $30$ m.

**Step 2 — Recall the layout formula.**
From the KTU Formula Sheet, Row 1:

$$
N_b = \frac{L_{quay}}{L_{ship} + S_{clear}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
N_b = \frac{1200}{229 + 30} = \frac{1200}{259}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the quotient.**

$$
N_b = 4.633
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the physical (integer) constraint.**
You cannot build a fractional berth. The yard can therefore accommodate:

$$
N_b^{actual} = 4 \text{ berths (rounded down)}
$$

**Step 6 — Check residual quay length for ancillary use.**

$$
L_{residual} = 1200 - 4 \times 259 = 1200 - 1036 = 164 \text{ m}
$$

This residual **164 m** can be allocated to an **outfitting quay**, a **tug berth**, or a **bunker barge jetty**.

### Result:
The yard can host **4 parallel building berths**, with **164 m of auxiliary quay** available. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stockyard Area Requirement

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the minimum steel stockyard area required to support the construction of an **Aframax tanker** with the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{pp}$ = **245 m**,
- Breadth, $B$ = **32.2 m**,
- Lightweight, $\Delta_{light}$ = **12,000 tonnes**,
- Steel coefficient, $\alpha$ = **0.30**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Estimate total steel required.**

$$
W_{steel} = \alpha \cdot \Delta_{light} = 0.30 \times 12{,}000 = 3{,}600 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stockyard footprint heuristic** (Formula Sheet Row 3).

$$
A_{yard} = 6 \cdot L_{ship} \cdot B_{ship}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ship dimensions.**

$$
A_{yard} = 6 \times 245 \times 32.2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$
A_{yard} = 6 \times 7{,}889 = 47{,}334 \text{ m}^2 \approx 4.73 \text{ hectares}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using steel density on yard.**

Assuming an average yard storage intensity of **0.08 tonnes/m²** (plates, sections, pipes stacked):

$$
A_{check} = \frac{W_{steel}}{\rho_{yard}} = \frac{3{,}600}{0.08} = 45{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Reconcile.**
The two estimates ($47{,}334$ m² and $45{,}000$ m²) agree within **±5 %**, validating the rule of thumb. The **higher value (47,334 m²)** should be adopted for conservative planning.

### Result:
Minimum stockyard area required ≈ **47,334 m² ≈ 4.73 ha**. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annual Production Capacity in CGT

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard operates **3 building berths**, each completing **1.2 ships per year**, with an average ship size of **25,000 CGT**. Estimate the yard's annual CGT throughput.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- $N_b = 3$,
- Throughput per berth per year, $\eta = 1.2$ ships,
- Ship size, $S = 25{,}000$ CGT.

**Step 2 — Total ships per year.**

$$
N_{ships} = N_b \cdot \eta = 3 \times 1.2 = 3.6 \text{ ships/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual CGT output.**

$$
CGT_{annual} = N_{ships} \cdot S = 3.6 \times 25{,}000 = 90{,}000 \text{ CGT/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A yard of **90,000 CGT/year** falls in the **medium-tier global yard** category (Korean giants produce >1,000,000 CGT/yr).

### Result:
**Annual capacity ≈ 90,000 CGT.** ✅

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Berth Occupancy Ratio (BOR)

**Problem Statement:** A berth is occupied by a vessel for **265 days** in a calendar year of **365 days**. Compute the BOR and comment on its health.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recall the formula** (Formula Sheet Row 4).

$$
BOR = \frac{T_{occ}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
BOR = \frac{265}{365} \times 100\% = 72.60\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with industry benchmark.**
Industry-healthy target: **BOR ≤ 70 %**. A BOR of **72.6 %** indicates:
- The berth is **slightly over-loaded**,
- Risk of schedule slippage during rework or weather delays,
- Recommend either **adding a fourth berth** or **compressing the cycle time by 10 days**.

### Result:
**BOR = 72.6 %** — marginally overloaded; mitigation recommended. ✅

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Harbour Depth for Safe Access

**Problem Statement:** A Capesize bulk carrier with loaded draft $d_{ship} = 18.5$ m is to call at a new shipyard harbour. Determine the required harbour depth, given:
- Under-keel clearance factor, $U_k = 15\%$ of draft,
- Safety margin, $S_{safety} = 1.0$ m.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recall the harbour-depth formula** (Formula Sheet Row 19).

$$
D_{harbour} = d_{ship} + U_k + S_{safety}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the under-keel clearance.**

$$
U_k = 0.15 \times 18.5 = 2.775 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all terms.**

$$
D_{harbour} = 18.5 + 2.775 + 1.0 = 22.275 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the next half-metre for dredging tolerance.**

$$
D_{harbour}^{final} = 22.5 \text{ m}
$$

### Result:
**Required dredged depth ≈ 22.5 m** (very deep — confirms the Capesize is a "deep-draft" class requiring purpose-built harbours). ✅

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix — Impact Ranking of Layout Factors

The following **Impact-Score Matrix** is a KTU-style analytical tool that examiners love to see in 14-mark answers. Each factor is rated on:
- **CAPEX impact** (capital cost sensitivity),
- **Schedule impact** (effect on build duration),
- **Operational impact** (effect on day-to-day workflow),
- **Strategic impact** (long-term competitiveness),
- **Overall score** (1 – 5, 5 = highest impact).

| # | Layout Factor | CAPEX | Schedule | Ops | Strategic | Overall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type & size of ships | 5 | 5 | 5 | 5 | **5.0** |
| 2 | Production volume / capacity | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | **4.5** |
| 3 | Land area & waterfront length | 5 | 3 | 4 | 5 | **4.3** |
| 4 | Topography & soil | 5 | 2 | 2 | 3 | **3.0** |
| 5 | Metocean conditions | 4 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **3.8** |
| 6 | Skilled labour availability | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | **3.8** |
| 7 | Transport infrastructure | 3 | 3 | 5 | 4 | **3.8** |
| 8 | Supply-chain proximity | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | **3.8** |
| 9 | Utilities (power, water, gas) | 4 | 2 | 5 | 3 | **3.5** |
| 10 | Govt policy / CRZ / defence | 3 | 2 | 2 | 5 | **3.0** |
| 11 | Future expansion reserve | 3 | 1 | 2 | 5 | **2.8** |
| 12 | Safety, security, workflow | 2 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **3.3** |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the matrix:** Factors #1 and #2 dominate every dimension — *start every layout design brief by locking down the ship type and volume*. Factors #4 and #10 are "fixed" by geography/policy and are therefore *site-selection filters*, not layout knobs.
