# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define shipbuilding strategy and list its five core components.**

**Model Answer:**

Shipbuilding strategy is the integrated long-term plan formulated by a shipyard to align its production capabilities, technology, workforce, and market positioning with global maritime demand and regulatory environment.

The five core components are:
1. **Market Strategy** — vessel segment and customer selection
2. **Product (Vessel) Strategy** — design and complexity choice
3. **Build / Production Strategy** — construction methodology
4. **Technology & Innovation Strategy** — R&D and digitalization
5. **Resource & Risk Strategy** — workforce, finance, supply chain, risk

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "learning curve" and state its strategic significance in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

The learning curve describes the systematic reduction in man-hours (or cost) required to produce each successive unit in a series as workers and processes become more efficient.

**Strategic significance:**
- Enables accurate cost forecasting for serial production
- Justifies lower bid prices for follow-on vessels
- Encourages repeat orders and long-term customer relationships
- Guides investment in automation to accelerate learning

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) writing generic management text without shipbuilding-specific context, (2) forgetting to mention **CGT** as the strategic output metric, (3) omitting the **learning curve** when discussing cost leadership, and (4) failing to link each strategy element to a **specific ship type** (e.g., VLCC, LNG, frigate).

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a PESTEL framework, analyse the external environment influencing modern shipbuilding strategy formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three generic strategies (Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus) as applied by global shipyards, with one real-world example of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PESTEL Analysis for Shipbuilding:**

- **Political:** Government subsidies (Korea's KOSHIPA, China's CSSC), defence procurement indigenization (India's Make in India naval projects), trade tariffs on imported steel, US Jones Act restrictions on domestic builds.
- **Economic:** Volatile freight rates driving orderbook cycles, steel price fluctuations (HRC index), USD/KRW exchange rate exposure for Korean yards, global GDP growth affecting seaborne trade.
- **Social:** Ageing workforce in Japan and Europe, scarcity of skilled welders, urban encroachment limiting yard expansion, evolving safety culture (helmet, harness, gas-monitoring).
- **Technological:** Digital twin shipyards (HD Hyundai), AI-driven steel nesting, robotic welding (Hyundai Heavy Industries' 80% automation), additive manufacturing of marine parts.
- **Environmental:** IMO 2030 (40% GHG reduction), IMO 2050 (net-zero), Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII), Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI), demand for alternative fuels (LNG, methanol, ammonia, hydrogen).
- **Legal:** SOLAS, MARPOL Annex VI, Ballast Water Management Convention, Hong Kong Convention on ship recycling, classification rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV).

**(b) Three Generic Strategies in Shipbuilding:**

| Strategy | Yard Example | Vessel Type | Strategic Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost Leadership** | Hyundai Heavy Industries (Korea) | VLCC, Container | Scale, automation, low man-hr/CGT (~30) |
| **Differentiation** | Fincantieri (Italy) | Cruise ships, Naval | Design IP, quality, complex integration |
| **Focus** | Cochin Shipyard (India) | Aircraft Carrier, Naval | Sovereign capability, niche specialization |

- **Cost Leadership:** Achieved through high volume, modular block construction, robotic welding, and aggressive learning curve exploitation. Korean and Chinese yards dominate here.
- **Differentiation:** European yards like Fincantieri, Chantiers de l'Atlantique compete via design sophistication, customization, and after-sales service.
- **Focus:** Mid-tier yards specialize in naval, offshore, or repair segments where margins are stable and customer base is limited but loyal.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A shipyard estimates the first vessel of a new series will require **1,500,000 man-hours**. Assuming an **80% learning rate**, calculate the man-hours for the **6th vessel** and the **total man-hours for the first 6 vessels**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **digital transformation** in implementing a modern shipbuilding strategy. Mention at least four digital technologies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Learning Curve Calculation:**

Step 1 — Calculate the exponent $b$:

$$b = \dfrac{\ln(1/0.80)}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{\ln(1.25)}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{0.2231}{0.6931} = 0.3219$$

Step 2 — Calculate man-hours for the 6th vessel using $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$:

$$C_6 = 1{,}500{,}000 \cdot 6^{-0.3219}$$

Step 3 — Compute the power:

$$6^{-0.3219} = e^{-0.3219 \cdot \ln 6} = e^{-0.3219 \cdot 1.7918} = e^{-0.5768} = 0.5620$$

Step 4 — Final man-hours for 6th vessel:

$$C_6 = 1{,}500{,}000 \cdot 0.5620 = 8{,}43{,}000 \text{ man-hours}$$

Step 5 — Total man-hours for first 6 vessels:

$$H_{total} = \sum_{n=1}^{6} C_1 \cdot n^{-b} = 1{,}500{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.8409 + 0.7671 + 0.7220 + 0.6896 + 0.5620)$$

$$H_{total} = 1{,}500{,}000 \cdot 4.5816 = 6{,}872{,}400 \text{ man-hours}$$

**Result:** The 6th vessel needs approximately **8,43,000 man-hours**; the cumulative total is approximately **68,72,400 man-hours**.

**(b) Digital Transformation in Shipbuilding Strategy:**

1. **3D CAD/CAM Integration (e.g., AVEVA Marine, Tribon, CATIA):** Enables clash-free design, automatic nesting, and direct CNC plate cutting — reducing rework and material waste.
2. **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) & Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES):** Integrates procurement, production scheduling, and finance into one data backbone, enabling real-time KPI tracking.
3. **Digital Twin Technology:** A live virtual replica of the ship under construction, allowing engineers to simulate outfitting, optimize block erection sequence, and predict defects before physical work begins.
4. **Robotic Welding & Automation:** High-repeatability welding cells, automated blasting, and robotic paint applicators reduce dependence on scarce skilled labour and improve safety.
5. **AI-driven Planning & Scheduling:** Algorithms that optimize block flow, crane utilization, and workforce allocation, achieving schedule adherence improvement of 15–25%.
6. **IoT-enabled Asset Tracking:** RFID tags on every plate, pipe, and component provide end-to-end traceability and support predictive maintenance of yard equipment.

Strategic outcome: yards adopting these technologies achieve **man-hr/CGT as low as 25–30**, on-time delivery rates above 95%, and defect density reductions of 40–60% compared to traditional yards.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Developing Shipbuilding Strategy**

- **Strategy** is the long-term integrated plan, not a short-term operational plan.
- **Five Pillars:** Market, Product, Build, Technology, Resource & Risk.
- **PESTEL** is the standard external scanning tool.
- **SWOT** synthesizes internal strengths/weaknesses with external opportunities/threats.
- **Three Generic Strategies (Porter):** Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus.
- **Strategic output metric:** **CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage)**.
- **Key productivity benchmark:** **30–50 man-hours per CGT**; leading yards aim below 30.
- **Steel utilization:** approximately **1 ton of steel per CGT**.
- **Learning curve equation:** $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ where $b = \dfrac{\ln(1/LR)}{\ln 2}$.
- Typical shipbuilding **learning rate:** **80–90%**.
- **Schedule Adherence Index (SAI)** and **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** are the two principal KPIs.
- **Build strategies** range from unit construction → series construction → mass production.
- **Digital transformation** spans CAD → 3D → CAM nesting → digital twin → Industry 4.0 autonomous yard.
- **Risk categories:** market, technical, financial, regulatory, geopolitical.
- **Strategic decisions** are evaluated on the yard's **5-year plan alignment** and **risk-adjusted ROI**.
- Always link strategy to a **specific vessel type** for full marks in KTU answers.
- The **strategic role of IMO 2030/2050** and alternative fuels (LNG, methanol, ammonia) is now a permanent part of every shipbuilding strategy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Estimation Methods in Ship Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST632 - SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Estimation Methods in Ship Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cost estimation in ship production** is the systematic, analytical process of predicting the total monetary expenditure required to design, construct, outfit, and deliver a merchant or naval vessel, by applying standardized engineering economic models, historical yard data, and parametric relationships between cost-driving ship attributes (such as lightweight, deadweight, main engine power, and length overall) and corresponding cost elements (material, labor, overhead, and profit).

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cost estimation in shipbuilding is classified under **Pre-Contract Engineering Economics** and forms the basis of the **Shipbuilding Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** used by classification societies, OEMs, and procurement agencies (e.g., Indian Navy, INSA, Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Cost estimation is a prerequisite for **bid preparation, contract negotiation, financing, and profitability analysis**. The estimator must distinguish between *First Cost*, *Operating Cost*, and *Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a custom house. Before laying a single brick, you roughly estimate: "It will cost roughly **₹2,000 per square foot** because my neighbor built a similar house for that much." That is **parametric estimation**. If instead you list every brick, every bag of cement, and every hour of mason work, summing them up — that is the **detailed unit-cost (bottom-up) method**.

**In shipbuilding, the same logic applies**, but the numbers are enormous (a Panamax bulker may cost **USD 30–35 million**), and the variables are more intertwined. Ship cost estimators use:

- A **regression equation** that links ship deadweight to steel weight, and steel weight to man-hours, and man-hours to labor cost.
- A **learning curve** that says: "the 2nd ship in a series costs ~80% of the 1st ship because workers have learned the tricks."
- A **cost index** that updates a 2015 quotation to a 2025 quotation using escalation factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Constants / Standard Metrics used in KTU Module 4:**
> - **Building Cost per Ton (BCPT)** = Total building cost / Light Ship Weight (LSW) — unit: **USD/ton** or **INR/ton**
> - **Cost per Deadweight Ton (DWT)** — used for tankers and bulkers
> - **Cost per Shaft Horsepower (SHP)** — used for naval and high-speed vessels
> - **Learning Rate (LR)** typically **75% – 90%** in shipyards
> - **Standard labor productivity:** 25–40 man-hours per ton of steel (varies with ship type and yard efficiency)

## 1.3 Why Cost Estimation Matters in Ship Production

A ship is a **one-of-a-kind, capital-intensive, long-lead-time product** (build time: 12–36 months). Unlike mass-produced cars, every ship is a **prototype** in some sense. Therefore:

1. **Bidding Phase:** Without an accurate estimate, the yard may either lose the contract (over-quote) or incur loss (under-quote).
2. **Production Phase:** Cost estimation provides the *cost baseline* against which actual expenditure is monitored — this is called **Earned Value Management (EVM)**.
3. **Contract Variations:** Any design change must be translated into a cost impact using the same estimation model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ship Costs (Cost Breakdown Structure)

The total cost of a ship $C_{\text{total}}$ is decomposed as:

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{overhead}} + C_{\text{profit}} + C_{\text{finance}}$$

| Cost Element | Sub-components | Typical % of Total (Merchant Ship) |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Cost ($C_m$)** | Steel, outfit, main engine, auxiliaries, paint, electrical | 55 – 65 % |
| **Labor Cost ($C_L$)** | Direct man-hours × wage rate, including overtime | 15 – 25 % |
| **Overhead ($C_{OH}$)** | Yard facilities, supervision, design, insurance, utilities | 8 – 12 % |
| **Profit Margin ($C_P$)** | Builder's profit, usually % of $C_m + C_L + C_{OH}$ | 5 – 10 % |
| **Finance Cost ($C_f$)** | Working capital interest, letter of credit, guarantees | 2 – 5 % |

## 2.2 The Six Major Cost Estimation Methods

### Method 1 — Detailed (Bottom-Up / Unit-Cost) Method
This is the **most accurate** but **most time-consuming** method. Every component is priced individually.

- **Steel cost** = (Steel weight in tons) × (Unit steel price per ton)
- **Pipe cost** = (Total pipe length in meters) × (Unit pipe price per meter)
- **Cable cost** = (Total cable length in km) × (Unit cable price per km)
- **Paint cost** = (Painted surface area in m²) × (Unit paint cost per m²)
- **Labor cost** = (Total man-hours) × (Hourly wage rate)

**Equation:**

$$C_{\text{direct}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot P_i$$

where $Q_i$ is the quantity of item $i$, and $P_i$ is its unit price.

### Method 2 — Parametric (Statistical / Regression) Method
Based on **historical cost data** from previously built similar ships. Uses statistical regression to relate cost to one or more physical parameters.

The general form is a **power law**:

$$C = a \cdot X^b$$

where $X$ is the cost driver (e.g., DWT, LSW, SHP), and $a$, $b$ are regression coefficients determined from past data.

**Common parametric forms:**

$$C_{\text{steel}} = 800 \cdot (\text{LSW})^{0.95} \quad \text{[USD]}$$

$$C_{\text{outfit}} = 250 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{1.05} \quad \text{[USD]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a power law?** Because both cost and ship size scale with the **square-cube law** (areas scale as $L^2$, volumes/mass as $L^3$).

### Method 3 — Analogy (Comparative) Method
The estimator compares the proposed ship to a *similar ship* already built (the **reference vessel**) and applies a **correction factor**:

$$C_{\text{new}} = C_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{X_{\text{new}}}{X_{\text{ref}}} \cdot \frac{I_{\text{new}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}$$

where $I$ is a cost index (e.g., LME steel index, BLS labor index).

### Method 4 — Cost Index (Escalation) Method
Used to **update a past quotation** to current prices:

$$C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{past}} \cdot \frac{\text{Index}_{\text{present}}}{\text{Index}_{\text{past}}}$$

Common cost indices:
- **LME Steel Index** (for steel price)
- **BLS Wage Index** (for labor)
- **Shipbuilders' Price Index (SPI)** — published by maritime economics journals

### Method 5 — Learning Curve Method
When a yard builds **multiple sister ships in series**, the cost per ship **decreases** with each repetition. This is quantified by the **learning rate (LR)** — typically **80%** in shipbuilding.

The **Crawford unit-learning curve** equation:

$$Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$$

where:
- $Y_x$ = cost (or man-hours) of the $x$-th unit
- $Y_1$ = cost of the 1st unit
- $x$ = unit number (cumulative)
- $b$ = learning curve exponent

The exponent $b$ is related to LR by:

$$b = \frac{\log(\text{LR})}{\log(2)} = \frac{\log(0.80)}{\log(2)} \approx -0.3219$$

### Method 6 — Activity-Based Costing (ABC) Method
Allocates overhead to specific shipbuilding activities (cutting, welding, painting, erection). More accurate than traditional %-based overhead.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \sum Q_i P_i$ | Direct cost (unit-cost method) | Currency |
| 2 | $C = a X^b$ | Parametric power-law cost | Currency |
| 3 | $C_{\text{new}} = C_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{X_{\text{new}}}{X_{\text{ref}}} \cdot \dfrac{I_{\text{new}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}$ | Analogy + index escalation | Currency |
| 4 | $C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{I_{\text{now}}}{I_{\text{old}}}$ | Cost index escalation | Currency |
| 5 | $Y_x = Y_1 x^{b}$, $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{LR})}{\log 2}$ | Learning curve | Hours/Currency |
| 6 | $\text{BCPT} = \dfrac{C_{\text{build}}}{\text{LSW}}$ | Building cost per ton | USD/ton |
| 7 | $C_m = w \cdot p \cdot (1 + s)$ | Material cost (w=weight, p=unit price, s=scrap %) | Currency |
| 8 | $C_L = H \cdot r$ | Labor cost (H=hours, r=rate) | Currency |
| 9 | $C_{OH} = k \cdot C_L$ | Overhead (k = 0.4 – 0.8) | Currency |
| 10 | $C_{\text{total}} = (C_m + C_L + C_{OH})(1 + m)$ | Total with profit margin $m$ | Currency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yard Management:** Used in **monthly progress reviews** to compare estimated vs. actual cost.
- **Procurement:** Helps decide **make-or-buy** for components (e.g., is it cheaper to fabricate a hatch cover in-house or buy from Korea?).
- **Design Optimization:** Cost estimation is iterated during **preliminary design** — a 1% reduction in lightship weight may save 0.6% in total cost.
- **Contract Law:** Cost estimates form part of **fixed-price contracts**, **cost-plus contracts**, and **letter of intent** negotiations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Learning Curve Cost for a Series of Sister Ships

**Problem:** A shipyard quotes the first tanker at **120,000 man-hours**. The learning rate is **80%**. Find the man-hours required to build the **5th tanker** and the **cumulative man-hours** for the first 5 ships.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

The unit-learning curve equation is:

$$Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$$

where $b$ is the learning exponent, given by:

$$b = \frac{\log(\text{LR})}{\log(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the exponent $b$.**

For LR = 0.80 (i.e., 80 %):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\log(0.80)}{\log(2)} \\
  &= \frac{-0.09691}{0.30103} \\
  &= -0.32193
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the man-hours for the 5th unit ($x = 5$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_5 &= 120{,}000 \cdot 5^{-0.32193} \\
    &= 120{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{5^{0.32193}}
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $5^{0.32193}$. Using logarithms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(5^{0.32193}) &= 0.32193 \times \log(5) \\
                  &= 0.32193 \times 0.69897 \\
                  &= 0.22504
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{0.32193} &= 10^{0.22504} \\
            &\approx 1.6788
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_5 &= \frac{120{,}000}{1.6788} \\
    &\approx 71{,}482 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the trend.** The 5th ship should take less time than the 1st ship. ✓ (71,482 < 120,000).

**Step 5 — Compute man-hours for units 1 through 5.**

For $x = 1$: $Y_1 = 120{,}000$
For $x = 2$: $Y_2 = 120{,}000 \times 2^{-0.32193} = 120{,}000 \times 0.8000 = 96{,}000$
For $x = 3$: $Y_3 = 120{,}000 \times 3^{-0.32193}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(3^{0.32193}) &= 0.32193 \times 0.47712 = 0.15361 \\
3^{0.32193} &= 10^{0.15361} = 1.4241
\end{aligned}
$$

So $Y_3 = 120{,}000 / 1.4241 = 84{,}265$ hours.

For $x = 4$: $Y_4 = 120{,}000 \times 4^{-0.32193}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
4^{0.32193} &= 2^{0.64386} = 1.5620 \\
Y_4 &= 120{,}000 / 1.5620 = 76{,}826 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $x = 5$: $Y_5 \approx 71{,}482$ hours (from Step 3).

**Step 6 — Sum for cumulative man-hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{cum}} &= 120{,}000 + 96{,}000 + 84{,}265 + 76{,}826 + 71{,}482 \\
               &= 448{,}573 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret.** The yard saves roughly 596,000 − 448,573 ≈ **147,427 hours** by the 5th ship (≈ 25 % productivity gain), validating the strategic value of series production.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost Index Escalation with Parametric Update

**Problem:** In 2020, a 50,000 DWT bulker cost **USD 28 million**. Estimate its 2024 price using:
- LME steel index: 2020 = 480, 2024 = 615
- BLS wage index: 2020 = 110, 2024 = 132
- Steel is **60 %** of cost, labor is **25 %**, and other items (escalation 8 % over 4 years) make up the remaining 15 %.

**Step 1 — Decompose the 2020 cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{steel, 2020}} &= 0.60 \times 28{,}000{,}000 = 16{,}800{,}000 \text{ USD} \\
C_{\text{labor, 2020}} &= 0.25 \times 28{,}000{,}000 = 7{,}000{,}000 \text{ USD} \\
C_{\text{other, 2020}} &= 0.15 \times 28{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}200{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply index-based escalation to each component.**

Steel escalates by the LME ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{steel, 2024}} &= 16{,}800{,}000 \times \frac{615}{480} \\
                       &= 16{,}800{,}000 \times 1.28125 \\
                       &= 21{,}525{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

Labor escalates by the BLS ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{labor, 2024}} &= 7{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{132}{110} \\
                       &= 7{,}000{,}000 \times 1.20 \\
                       &= 8{,}400{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

Other items escalate by 8 % over 4 years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{other, 2024}} &= 4{,}200{,}000 \times 1.08 \\
                       &= 4{,}536{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum up to find the total 2024 cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total, 2024}} &= 21{,}525{,}000 + 8{,}400{,}000 + 4{,}536{,}000 \\
                       &= 34{,}461{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a parametric escalation factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Escalation factor} &= \frac{34{,}461{,}000}{28{,}000{,}000} \\
                         &\approx 1.231
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., a **23.1 % price increase** over 4 years, or roughly **5.3 % per annum** — consistent with typical shipbuilding inflation rates.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Estimation Method

| Method | Data Required | Accuracy | Time | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detailed (Unit-cost) | Full bill of materials, hours, rates | Very High (±2–5%) | Weeks–months | Final bid, contract stage |
| Parametric (Regression) | Historical yard database | High (±5–10%) | Hours–days | Concept / preliminary design |
| Analogy (Comparative) | 1–2 reference ships + indices | Medium (±10–15%) | Days | Feasibility study |
| Cost Index Escalation | Old quote + indices | Medium (±8–12%) | Minutes | Quick budget update |
| Learning Curve | 1st-unit cost + LR | High (±5–8%) | Hours | Series production planning |
| Activity-Based Costing (ABC) | Activity-level data | Very High (±3–6%) | Weeks | Overhead allocation, lean yard |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **parametric cost estimation** and **detailed unit-cost estimation** in ship production. State two advantages of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Parametric Method | Detailed Unit-Cost Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Historical regression equations | Bottom-up bill of materials + hours |
| **Data required** | Few key parameters (DWT, LSW, SHP) | Complete design and material list |
| **Speed** | Very fast (hours) | Slow (weeks to months) |
| **Accuracy** | ± 5–10 % | ± 2–5 % |
| **Stage of use** | Conceptual / preliminary design | Final bid / contract stage |

**Two advantages of parametric method:** (1) rapid estimation even with limited data; (2) useful for sensitivity analysis (changing DWT and observing cost).

**Two advantages of unit-cost method:** (1) high accuracy; (2) suitable for procurement and shop-floor cost control.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **learning rate (LR)** in shipbuilding. A yard has a learning rate of 85 %. What does it mean, and what is the value of the learning exponent $b$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **learning rate (LR)** is the fractional reduction in man-hours (or cost) required to build each successive unit in a series, when the cumulative production doubles. An LR of **85 %** means that when the yard has built **twice as many ships**, the man-hours per ship drop to **85 %** of the previous level.

The learning exponent is calculated as:

$$
b = \frac{\log(0.85)}{\log(2)} = \frac{-0.07058}{0.30103} \approx -0.2345
$$

Thus, the unit cost follows the relation $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{-0.2345}$.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** 

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** of a merchant ship. List the major cost elements and indicate their typical percentage share of the total new-building price.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A shipyard uses a **learning rate of 80 %** to build a series of four identical product tankers. The first ship required **150,000 man-hours**. Determine (i) the man-hours required for the 4th ship, and (ii) the total cumulative man-hours for the 4 ships.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS):**

The CBS of a merchant ship is a hierarchical decomposition of the total new-building price into its constituent cost elements. The five primary cost categories are:

1. **Material Cost (55 – 65 %)** — Steel hull plates, profiles, pipes, cables, paint, main engine, auxiliary machinery, navigation electronics.
2. **Labor Cost (15 – 25 %)** — Direct man-hours (cutting, welding, fitting) × wage rate. Includes overtime, supervision.
3. **Overhead (8 – 12 %)** — Yard facilities, design office, administration, depreciation of cranes and slipways, insurance, utilities.
4. **Profit (5 – 10 %)** — Builder's margin.
5. **Finance (2 – 5 %)** — Working capital interest, bank guarantees.

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_m + C_L + C_{OH} + C_P + C_f
$$

**(b) Learning Curve Calculation:**

**Given:** $Y_1 = 150{,}000$ hours, LR = 0.80, $x = 4$.

**Step 1 — Exponent.**

$$
b = \frac{\log(0.80)}{\log(2)} = \frac{-0.09691}{0.30103} = -0.3219
$$

**Step 2 — Man-hours for the 4th ship.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_4 &= 150{,}000 \times 4^{-0.3219} \\
    &= 150{,}000 \times \frac{1}{4^{0.3219}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $4^{0.3219}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(4^{0.3219}) &= 0.3219 \times \log(4) \\
                &= 0.3219 \times 0.6021 \\
                &= 0.1938 \\
4^{0.3219} &= 10^{0.1938} = 1.562
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_4 &= \frac{150{,}000}{1.562} \\
    &= 96{,}030 \text{ man-hours (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Hours for intermediate ships.**

$Y_2 = 150{,}000 \times 0.80 = 120{,}000$ hours
$Y_3 = 150{,}000 \times 3^{-0.3219}$

$$
3^{0.3219} = 1.424, \quad Y_3 = 150{,}000 / 1.424 = 105{,}337 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{cum}} &= 150{,}000 + 120{,}000 + 105{,}337 + 96{,}030 \\
               &= 471{,}367 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 4th ship requires only **64 %** of the 1st ship's hours, saving **~54,000 hours** and demonstrating the strategic value of series production.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat sketches/flowcharts, explain the **parametric method** of cost estimation in shipbuilding. Give the form of the power-law equation and identify the most common cost drivers used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The 2019 price of a 30,000 DWT chemical tanker was **USD 32 million**, with the cost share being: steel 58 %, labor 22 %, others 20 %. Using the following indices, estimate the **2024 price**:

| Item | 2019 Index | 2024 Index |
|---|---|---|
| LME Steel | 420 | 580 |
| BLS Labor | 105 | 128 |
| General CPI (Others) | 100 | 114 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parametric Method Explanation:**

The **parametric (statistical) method** correlates historical ship costs with one or more physical parameters using regression analysis. The most widely used form is the **power law**:

$$C = a \cdot X^b$$

where:
- $C$ = total cost (USD)
- $X$ = cost driver (e.g., DWT, LSW, SHP)
- $a, b$ = regression coefficients

**Common cost drivers:**
1. **Deadweight (DWT)** — for tankers, bulkers.
2. **Lightship weight (LSW)** — for all ships (steel + outfit weight).
3. **Shaft horsepower (SHP)** — for naval and high-speed vessels.
4. **Length overall (LOA)** — for offshore platforms.
5. **Number of TEU** — for container ships.

**Procedure (flowchart steps):**
1. Collect cost data from $n$ historical ships of similar type.
2. Plot $\log C$ vs. $\log X$ — the slope gives $b$, the intercept gives $\log a$.
3. Use the derived equation to predict cost of a new ship with parameter $X_{\text{new}}$.

**Advantages:** Fast, suitable for concept and preliminary design, enables sensitivity studies.

**(b) Cost Index Escalation:**

**Step 1 — Decompose the 2019 cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{steel, 2019}} &= 0.58 \times 32{,}000{,}000 = 18{,}560{,}000 \text{ USD} \\
C_{\text{labor, 2019}} &= 0.22 \times 32{,}000{,}000 = 7{,}040{,}000 \text{ USD} \\
C_{\text{other, 2019}} &= 0.20 \times 32{,}000{,}000 = 6{,}400{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply respective indices.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{steel, 2024}} &= 18{,}560{,}000 \times \frac{580}{420} \\
                       &= 18{,}560{,}000 \times 1.3810 \\
                       &= 25{,}631{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{labor, 2024}} &= 7{,}040{,}000 \times \frac{128}{105} \\
                       &= 7{,}040{,}000 \times 1.2190 \\
                       &= 8{,}582{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{other, 2024}} &= 6{,}400{,}000 \times \frac{114}{100} \\
                       &= 6{,}400{,}000 \times 1.14 \\
                       &= 7{,}296{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the escalated cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total, 2024}} &= 25{,}631{,}000 + 8{,}582{,}000 + 7{,}296{,}000 \\
                       &= 41{,}509{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective escalation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Escalation factor} &= \frac{41{,}509{,}000}{32{,}000{,}000} \\
                         &\approx 1.297
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., a **29.7 % increase over 5 years** (≈ 5.3 % CAGR).

**Conclusion:** The 2024 estimated price of the 30,000 DWT chemical tanker is approximately **USD 41.5 million**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent sign:** Students often write $b = +\log(\text{LR}) / \log(2)$ and get a *positive* exponent, leading to *increasing* cost — completely wrong. Always remember **$b$ is negative** for a learning curve with LR < 1.
> 2. **Mixing units in cost index method:** Never apply a single overall index to the entire cost. Always **decompose** the cost by element and apply the appropriate index.
> 3. **Ignoring overhead in parametric method:** A common error is to estimate only material cost via regression and forget that overhead is proportional to labor, not material.
> 4. **Not stating the cost driver:** In KTU valuation, you must **always state which parameter you are using** (DWT, LSW, SHP). A bare regression equation without context attracts zero marks.
> 5. **Rounding errors:** When using logs, carry at least 4 decimal places — premature rounding loses 1–2 marks.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS):** $C_{\text{total}} = C_m + C_L + C_{OH} + C_P + C_f$.
- **Six methods:** Detailed unit-cost, Parametric, Analogy, Cost index, Learning curve, Activity-Based Costing (ABC).
- **Parametric power law:** $C = a X^b$ — uses DWT, LSW, SHP as drivers.
- **Cost index escalation:** $C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{old}} \cdot (I_{\text{now}} / I_{\text{old}})$ — must decompose cost by element.
- **Learning curve:** $Y_x = Y_1 x^{b}$, $b = \log(\text{LR}) / \log 2$ — $b$ is **negative**; LR is typically **80 %** in shipyards.
- **BCPT** (Building Cost per Ton) = $C_{\text{build}} / \text{LSW}$ — key benchmarking metric.
- **Typical shares:** Material 60 %, Labor 20 %, Overhead 10 %, Profit 7 %, Finance 3 %.
- **Estimation accuracy** improves with design maturity: Conceptual (parametric) → Preliminary (analogy) → Detailed (unit-cost).
- **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** gives best overhead allocation for lean shipyards.
- **Risk and contingency** of 5–10 % is added to the rolled-up cost before bidding.
